The line where the **sedimentary thickness is at least 1% of the distance to the foot of the slope**, OR 5. The 350 nm line from the baseline (whichever gives the greater continental shelf area, subject to the overall 350 nm cap). ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A coastal State has a baseline low-water line. The territorial sea extends to 12 nm. The EEZ extends to 200 nm. The continental shelf, as confirmed by the Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf (CLCS), extends to 280 nm. Determine: > (a) The breadth of the Contiguous Zone. > (b) The breadth of the EEZ in km. > (c) Whether the continental shelf claim is legally valid under UNCLOS. **Step 1 — Identify the given data** - Baseline → Territorial Sea: $T = 12 \text{ nm}$ - Baseline → EEZ outer limit: $E = 200 \text{ nm}$ - Baseline → Continental Shelf outer limit: $C = 280 \text{ nm}$ **Step 2 — Compute the breadth of the Contiguous Zone** The Contiguous Zone extends **24 nm** from the baseline (Art. 33). Its breadth is therefore the **excess** over the Territorial Sea: $$ B_{CZ} = 24 - T = 24 - 12 = 12 \text{ nm} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the breadth of the EEZ in km** The EEZ extends to 200 nm from the baseline. The territorial sea already covers 0 → 12 nm. So the **non-overlapping EEZ breadth** is: $$ B_{EEZ} = 200 - 0 = 200 \text{ nm} $$ Converting to km: $$ B_{EEZ} = 200 \times 1.852 = 370.4 \text{ km} $$ **Step 4 — Legality of the Continental Shelf claim** Per Art. 76(5), the continental shelf can extend to a maximum of 350 nm from the baseline. Since $280 < 350$, the claim is **legally valid** under UNCLOS, provided the State has submitted appropriate geological/geophysical data to the CLCS and obtained its recommendations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse the EEZ with the Continental Shelf. The EEZ is a **water-column** concept, whereas the Continental Shelf is a **seabed-and-subsoil** concept. Their outer limits may coincide at 200 nm, but the rights are different. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Zone Differs in Sovereignty > [!TIP] > The following matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorise this! | Aspect | Internal Waters | Territorial Sea | Contiguous Zone | EEZ | High Seas | The Area | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Sovereignty** | Full | Full (subject to innocent passage) | Limited (control only) | Sovereign rights over resources | None | None (common heritage) | | **Innocent Passage** | No | Yes | Yes (through TS) | Yes (full freedom of nav.) | Yes (full freedom) | N/A (subsoil only) | | **Resource Rights** | All | All | Customs, fiscal | Resources only | Freedom of fishing | Regulated by ISA | | **Hot Pursuit** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Begins here | N/A | | **Bordering State** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No | ### 3.4 Decision Tree — Is a Ship in a Particular Zone? A ship is in a given maritime zone based on its **distance from the nearest baseline**. The flowchart below can be visualised in the next section. Let $D$ be the perpendicular distance from the baseline to the ship: $$ \text{Zone} = \begin{cases} \text{Internal Waters} & \text{if } D = 0 \text{ (landward of baseline)} \\ \text{Territorial Sea} & \text{if } 0 < D \leq 12 \text{ nm} \\ \text{Contiguous Zone} & \text{if } 12 < D \leq 24 \text{ nm} \\ \text{EEZ} & \text{if } 24 < D \leq 200 \text{ nm} \\ \text{High Seas} & \text{if } D > 200 \text{ nm} \end{cases} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Visualisation of UNCLOS Maritime Zones (Cross-Section) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["LAND TERRITORY
Full Sovereignty"] --> B["BASELINE
Normal or Straight"] B --> C["INTERNAL WATERS
Full Sovereignty
0 nm"] C --> D["TERRITORIAL SEA
Sovereignty + Innocent Passage
0 to 12 nm"] D --> E["CONTIGUOUS ZONE
Customs and Immigration Control
12 to 24 nm"] E --> F["EXCLUSIVE ECONOMIC ZONE
Sovereign Rights over Resources
0 to 200 nm"] F --> G["HIGH SEAS
Open to All States
Beyond 200 nm"] G --> H["THE AREA
International Seabed
Beyond Continental Shelf"] F -.->|"Continental Shelf
Seabed and Subsoil
0 to 200 nm or up to 350 nm"| H ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Identifying the Maritime Zone ```mermaid flowchart TD Q0["Start: Ship at distance D from baseline"] --> Q1{"D is landward of baseline?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2["INTERNAL WATERS
Full Sovereignty"] Q1 -- No --> Q3{"0 to 12 nm?"} Q3 -- Yes --> Q4["TERRITORIAL SEA
Innocent Passage Applies"] Q3 -- No --> Q5{"12 to 24 nm?"} Q5 -- Yes --> Q6["CONTIGUOUS ZONE
Customs and Immigration Control"] Q5 -- No --> Q7{"24 to 200 nm?"} Q7 -- Yes --> Q8["EEZ
Sovereign Rights over Resources"] Q7 -- No --> Q9{"Beyond 200 nm?"} Q9 -- Yes --> Q10{"Is the location on seabed
beyond outer continental shelf?"} Q10 -- Yes --> Q11["THE AREA
ISA Jurisdiction"] Q10 -- No --> Q12["HIGH SEAS
Freedom of Navigation"] ``` ### 4.3 Article 76 — Continental Shelf Outer Edge Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Identify 2500 m Isobath"] --> B["Identify Foot of Slope"] B --> C{"Apply Formula 1:
350 nm from baseline?"} B --> D{"Apply Formula 2:
100 nm beyond 2500 m isobath?"} B --> E{"Apply Formula 3:
Sediment thickness greater than or equal to 1 percent of distance to foot?"} C -- Yes --> F["Outer Edge Located"] D -- Yes --> F E -- Yes --> F F --> G["Submit to CLCS for Validation"] G --> H{"Maximum cap: 350 nm from baseline"} H -- Pass --> I["Approved Extended Shelf"] H -- Fail --> J["Truncate to 350 nm line"] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hot Pursuit Sequence (Art. 111) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Foreign Ship Violates Laws in Internal Waters or Territorial Sea or EEZ"] --> S2{"Coastal Authority
Issues Order to Stop?"} S2 -- No --> S3["Cannot Initiate Hot Pursuit"] S2 -- Yes --> S4["Hot Pursuit Begins
Continuous Visual or Electronic Contact"] S4 --> S5{"Ship Enters Territorial Sea
of Another State?"} S5 -- Yes --> S6["Pursuit Must Be Abandoned"] S5 -- No --> S7{"Pursuit Remains Continuous?"} S7 -- Yes --> S8["Coastal State May Board and Arrest"] S7 -- No --> S9["Pursuit Becomes Illegal"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Contiguous Zone" as per UNCLOS. Mention the article and the limit of the zone.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Contiguous Zone is a belt of sea adjacent to the territorial sea, extending up to **24 nautical miles** from the baseline, as defined under **Article 33 of UNCLOS, 1982**. In this zone, the coastal State may exercise control to: - (i) Prevent infringement of its customs, fiscal, immigration, or sanitary laws within its territory or territorial sea. - (ii) Punish infringement of the above laws committed within its territory or territorial sea. The coastal State does **not** have full sovereignty here; it exercises only **control** for the specific purposes stated above. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for Article + 1 for purpose)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between the EEZ and the Continental Shelf?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | EEZ | Continental Shelf | |---|---|---| | **Article** | Art. 57 | Art. 76 | | **Extent** | Up to 200 nm from baseline | 200 nm (default) or up to 350 nm (extended) | | **Subject Matter** | Water column, seabed, subsoil | Seabed and subsoil only | | **Nature of Rights** | Sovereign rights over living and non-living resources; jurisdiction over artificial islands, marine scientific research | Sovereign rights over mineral and non-living resources + sedentary species | The key distinction is that the EEZ is a **water-column-based concept**, while the Continental Shelf is a **subsoil-based concept**. *(3 marks: 1 for EEZ + 1 for Shelf + 1 for distinguishing point)* ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Total = 14 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) Explain in detail the various maritime zones under UNCLOS, 1982, with their respective limits and rights of the coastal State. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** UNCLOS, 1982, codified the existing customary international law of the sea and established a comprehensive framework dividing the ocean space into distinct zones, each with a defined legal status. **Maritime Zones and their Limits (3 marks):** 1. **Internal Waters (Art. 8):** Waters on the landward side of the baseline. The coastal State exercises **full sovereignty**, equivalent to its land territory. No right of innocent passage for foreign vessels. 2. **Territorial Sea (Art. 3, 17):** A belt extending **12 nm** from the baseline. The coastal State has sovereignty, subject to the right of **innocent passage** (Art. 17–26) for foreign ships. 3. **Contiguous Zone (Art. 33):** A belt extending to **24 nm** from the baseline. The coastal State may exercise control to prevent and punish infringement of its customs, fiscal, immigration, and sanitary regulations. 4. **EEZ (Art. 55–57):** An area extending **200 nm** from the baseline. The coastal State has **sovereign rights** for exploring, exploiting, conserving, and managing natural resources (living and non-living) of the waters superjacent to the seabed and of the seabed and its subsoil. 5. **Continental Shelf (Art. 76):** Comprises the seabed and subsoil of submarine areas extending up to **200 nm** from the baseline, or up to **350 nm** (or beyond) where the continental margin extends further, subject to the recommendations of the CLCS. 6. **High Seas (Art. 86–115):** All parts of the sea not included in the EEZ, territorial sea, or internal waters. **No State may purport to subject any part of the high seas to its sovereignty.** Open to all States with six freedoms (Art. 87). 7. **The Area (Art. 133–191):** The seabed and ocean floor beyond the limits of national jurisdiction. It is the **common heritage of mankind**, governed by the International Seabed Authority (ISA). **Conclusion (1 mark):** This zonal architecture ensures a balance between the rights of coastal States and the freedoms of other States, providing legal certainty for navigation, resource exploitation, and marine environmental protection. **(b) Discuss the concept of innocent passage under UNCLOS. What activities are not considered innocent? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Concept of Innocent Passage (3 marks):** Under **Article 17 of UNCLOS**, ships of all States enjoy the right of **innocent passage** through the territorial sea of a coastal State. Passage is **continuous and expeditious** (Art. 18), and it is **"innocent"** so long as it is **not prejudicial** to the peace, good order, or security of the coastal State (Art. 19(1)). **Submarines and warships (1 mark):** Submarines and other underwater vehicles must navigate **on the surface** and **show their flag** while passing through the territorial sea (Art. 20). **Non-Innocent Activities (Art. 19(2)) (2 marks):** Passage is **not innocent** if a ship engages in any of the following activities in the territorial sea: - (a) Any threat or use of force against the sovereignty, territorial integrity, or political independence of the coastal State. - (b) Any exercise or practice with weapons of any kind. - (c) Any act aimed at gathering information to the prejudice of the defence or security of the coastal State. - (d) Any act of propaganda aimed at affecting the defence or security of the coastal State. - (e) The launching, landing, or taking on board of any aircraft or military device. - (f) The loading or unloading of any commodity, currency, or person contrary to the customs, fiscal, immigration, or sanitary laws of the coastal State. - (g) Any act of wilful and serious pollution. - (h) Any fishing activities. - (i) The carrying out of research or survey activities. - (j) Any act aimed at interfering with the systems of communication or other facilities or installations of the coastal State. - (k) Any other activity not having a direct bearing on passage. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The doctrine of innocent passage strikes a balance between the coastal State's sovereignty and the international community's interest in freedom of navigation. It is essential for global maritime trade and naval mobility, while still preserving the security interests of coastal States. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) Explain the concept of the Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) under UNCLOS. What sovereign rights does the coastal State enjoy in its EEZ? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** The **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ)** is an area beyond and adjacent to the territorial sea, extending **200 nm** from the baselines from which the breadth of the territorial sea is measured, as defined under **Articles 55 and 57 of UNCLOS, 1982**. **Sovereign Rights of the Coastal State in the EEZ (Art. 56) (3 marks):** In the EEZ, the coastal State has: 1. **Sovereign rights** for the purpose of **exploring, exploiting, conserving, and managing** the natural resources — whether living or non-living — of: - The waters superjacent to the seabed. - The seabed and its subsoil. 2. **Jurisdiction** with regard to: - The establishment and use of artificial islands, installations, and structures. - Marine scientific research. - The protection and preservation of the marine environment. 3. **Other rights and duties** provided for in international law (e.g., hot pursuit, customs control). **Rights and Duties of Other States (Art. 58) (1 mark):** Other States enjoy the **freedom of navigation, overflight, the laying of submarine cables and pipelines**, and other internationally lawful uses of the sea related to these freedoms. **Hot Pursuit in the EEZ (1 mark):** The coastal State may exercise **hot pursuit** (Art. 111) of a foreign ship when there are good reasons to believe that the ship has violated the laws of the coastal State applicable in the EEZ. The pursuit may continue on the high seas as long as it has not been interrupted. **(b) Discuss the legal regime of the High Seas. What freedoms do States enjoy on the High Seas? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** The **High Seas**, as defined under **Articles 86–115 of UNCLOS, 1982**, comprise all parts of the sea that are **not included** in the EEZ, territorial sea, internal waters, or archipelagic waters of any State. They are open to **all States**, whether coastal or landlocked. **Six Freedoms of the High Seas (Art. 87) (3 marks):** The High Seas are reserved for peaceful purposes, and all States enjoy the following freedoms: 1. **Freedom of navigation.** 2. **Freedom of overflight.** 3. **Freedom to lay submarine cables and pipelines.** 4. **Freedom to construct artificial islands and other installations permitted under international law.** 5. **Freedom of fishing** (subject to treaty obligations). 6. **Freedom of marine scientific research.** **Flag State Jurisdiction (Art. 92) (1 mark):** Ships on the high seas sail under the **flag of one State only** and are subject to that State's exclusive jurisdiction. A ship may change flag only during transfer of ownership or registry; otherwise, it must observe the rule of **genuine link** between flag State and ship. **Piracy (Art. 101) (1 mark):** Any State may seize a **pirate ship** on the high seas and arrest the persons and seize the property on board. Piracy is an exception to the rule of exclusive flag-State jurisdiction and represents a universal jurisdiction offence. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently **forget that the EEZ and the Continental Shelf are distinct concepts** — the EEZ governs the water column; the shelf governs the seabed. > - Many confuse **innocent passage** (in territorial sea) with **transit passage** (in international straits). Transit passage is a higher standard with no suspension. > - In numerical problems, students write **kilometres instead of nautical miles** — this costs 0.5 to 1 mark. > - The **Contiguous Zone is NOT a separate belt of full sovereignty** — it is only a control zone for customs, fiscal, immigration, and sanitary purposes (Art. 33). > - For the Continental Shelf extension, students forget that submission to the **CLCS** is mandatory; without CLCS approval, the extension is not final. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density rapid revision checklist for the night before your KTU exam:** - ⭐ **UNCLOS = 1982 Montego Bay, in force 1994**, also called the "Constitution of the Oceans." - ⭐ **320 Articles, 9 Annexes, 17 Parts, 168 Parties**. - ⭐ **Baseline = low-water line** (normal) or straight lines (Art. 7). - ⭐ **Territorial Sea = 12 nm**, full sovereignty subject to **innocent passage** (Art. 17). - ⭐ **Contiguous Zone = 24 nm**, control for customs/immigration (Art. 33). - ⭐ **EEZ = 200 nm**, sovereign rights over resources (Art. 57). - ⭐ **Continental Shelf = 200 nm default, 350 nm max** (Art. 76). - ⭐ **High Seas = beyond EEZ**, six freedoms (Art. 87), exclusive flag-State jurisdiction (Art. 92), piracy is universal (Art. 101). - ⭐ **The Area = beyond outer shelf**, common heritage of mankind (Art. 136), governed by **ISA** (Kingston, Jamaica). - ⭐ **Innocent Passage:** Submarines on surface, show flag, no fishing/weapons/espionage. - ⭐ **Hot Pursuit (Art. 111):** Must be continuous, may extend onto High Seas, abandoned if ship enters another State's territorial sea. - ⭐ **Piracy (Art. 101):** Universal jurisdiction — any State can seize. - ⭐ **1 nm = 1.852 km** — use this conversion in **every** numerical question. - ⭐ **ISA = International Seabed Authority**, headquartered in Jamaica. - ⭐ **CLCS = Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf** — required for shelf extension beyond 200 nm. - ⭐ **Coastal State rights peak in Territorial Sea**, plateau in EEZ (resources only), vanish on High Seas. - ⭐ **High Seas freedoms:** Navigation, Overflight, Cables & Pipelines, Artificial Islands, Fishing, Marine Scientific Research. - ⭐ **EEZ ≠ Continental Shelf** — write this distinction in every Part A answer. - ⭐ **Art. 76(4–6)** formula: 350 nm OR 100 nm beyond 2500 m isobath OR 1% sediment thickness. - ⭐ **Archipelagic States (e.g., Indonesia, Philippines, Maldives)** can draw archipelagic baselines, with archipelagic sea lanes. - ⭐ **Landlocked States** have right of access to and from the sea (Art. 124–132). - ⭐ **Enclosed or semi-enclosed seas** (e.g., Mediterranean, Baltic, South China Sea) have special cooperation duties (Art. 123). - ⭐ **Marine scientific research** in EEZ requires **consent** of the coastal State (Art. 246). - ⭐ **Land reclamation** in the territorial sea is permissible only if it does **not** deviate from recognised sea lanes.

topics.title: Maritime Labour Convention, Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship, Conditions of Employment, Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food and Catering, Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare and Social Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maritime Labour Convention, Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship, Conditions of Employment, Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food and Catering, Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare and Social Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006 — Formal Definition

The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006** is an International Labour Organization (ILO) convention, often described as the **"Fourth Pillar"** of international maritime law, complementing SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW. It sets out comprehensive minimum working and living standards for seafarers globally and consolidates more than **68 prior ILO maritime labour instruments**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Examiner Standard)**
> The MLC, 2006 is an international labour standard that establishes the minimum requirements for nearly every aspect of working and living conditions for seafarers, including recruitment, employment conditions, accommodation, health, welfare, and social security protection, applicable to all commercial ships engaged in international voyages.

**Formal Citation:** *International Labour Conference, 94th Session, Geneva, 7 February 2006 — Convention No. 187 amalgamated into MLC, 2006.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Seafarers' Bill of Rights"** 🚢
> 
> Imagine a migrant construction worker who travels from country to country, lives in a temporary camp at each worksite, and is exposed to dangerous machinery, harsh weather, and isolation. Without a "Bill of Rights," the worker is at the mercy of the employer and the host country. The **MLC, 2006 is the seafarer's Bill of Rights** — a globally enforceable rulebook that ensures that no matter which flag a ship flies or which port it docks at, the seafarer aboard is guaranteed minimum standards of decency, safety, health, and dignity.

The MLC uses a unique **"Regulations + Code"** two-tier structure (similar to the IMO regulatory style):

- **Regulations** → the legally binding core rights and principles.
- **Code** → the practical implementation details, split into:
  - **Part A (Mandatory Standards)** — must be enforced.
  - **Part B (Non-Mandatory Guidelines)** — recommended best practices.

## 1.3 Key Salient Features at a Glance

| Feature | Detail |
|---|---|
| Adopted | **7 February 2006** (94th ILC Session) |
| Entry into Force | **20 August 2013** (after 30 ratifications representing 33% world tonnage) |
| Ratifications (current) | Over **100 countries** representing >**91% world tonnage** |
| Coverage | All commercial ships, including yachts, engaged in international voyages |
| Structure | **5 Titles**, **Articles I–XVI**, **Regulations**, and **Code** |
| Enforcement | **Flag State + Port State Control (PSC)** |
| "No more favourable treatment" clause | Non-ratifying states must not be given more favourable treatment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:**
> A ship of **500 GT or above**, engaged in international voyages or operating from/going to a foreign port, falls under MLC 2006 scope (Regulation 1.4).

## 1.4 The Five Titles of MLC 2006

| Title | Subject |
|---|---|
| **Title 1** | Minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship |
| **Title 2** | Conditions of employment |
| **Title 3** | Accommodation, recreational facilities, food and catering |
| **Title 4** | Health protection, medical care, welfare and social security |
| **Title 5** | Compliance and enforcement |

This document focuses on **Titles 1, 2, 3, and 4** as per the KTU module brief.

# ⚓ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Legal Architecture

The MLC follows a hierarchical legal architecture:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{MLC Architecture} &= \underbrace{\text{Articles}}_{\text{Constitutional Layer (I–XVI)}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\text{Regulations}}_{\text{Legislative Layer (5 Titles)}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\text{Code (Part A + Part B)}}_{\text{Executive Implementation Layer}}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

- **Articles** define ratification, scope, and enforcement principles.
- **Regulations** outline the broad obligations under each Title.
- **Part A** of the Code is **mandatory** (must be transposed into national law).
- **Part B** of the Code is **guidance** (helps interpret Part A).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners often test the *tripartite* nature of the Convention — it was negotiated by **Governments, Shipowners, and Seafarers' Unions** (the ILO's tripartite structure).

## 2.2 Analytical Matrix — MLC 2006 Coverage Areas

The following analytical matrix is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU board exams.

| Reg. No. | Subject Area | Key Obligation | Part A (Mandatory) Highlights |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reg 1.1** | Minimum age | Seafarers must be **≥ 16 years** | Hazardous work: ≥ 18 |
| **Reg 1.2** | Medical certificate | Valid fitness certificate required | Issued by qualified medical practitioner |
| **Reg 1.3** | Training & qualifications | Certificates of competency as per STCW | Career training schemes required |
| **Reg 1.4** | Recruitment & placement | Public/private services must be regulated | No fees charged to seafarers |
| **Reg 2.1** | Seafarers' Employment Agreement (SEA) | Written contract with clear terms | Must include particulars listed in Code |
| **Reg 2.2** | Wages | Paid regularly, in legal tender, by transfer | Allotment slips allowed |
| **Reg 2.3** | Hours of work & rest | Max **14 hrs/day** & **72 hrs/week** | **10 hrs rest in any 24 hr period**; **77 hrs in 7 days** |
| **Reg 2.4** | Entitlement to leave | Annual leave with pay | Calculated as **2.5 cal days/month** minimum |
| **Reg 2.5** | Repatriation | Right to return at no cost | Includes subsistence |
| **Reg 2.6** | Crew accommodation | Decent living standards (Title 3 details) | New builds: complies from keel laying date |
| **Reg 3.1** | Accommodation standards | Size, ventilation, heating, lighting, noise | Inspector must verify |
| **Reg 3.2** | Food & catering | Qualified cook, wholesome food | Cook certificate required |
| **Reg 4.1** | Medical care on board | Ship's doctor OR qualified medical person | Radio/telemedical advice available |
| **Reg 4.2** | Shipowner's liability | Covers sickness, injury, death from service | Pay wages till cured or repatriated |
| **Reg 4.3** | Health & safety protection | Accident prevention, occupational safety | PPE, accident reporting |
| **Reg 4.4** | Access to shore-based welfare | Welfare facilities in ports | Promote welfare boards |
| **Reg 4.5** | Social security | Portable benefits across borders | Bilateral/multilateral agreements |

## 2.3 Title 1 — Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship

### 2.3.1 Minimum Age (Reg 1.1)

$$ \boxed{
\text{Minimum Age} = 
\begin{cases}
\geq 16 \text{ years for general service} \\
\geq 18 \text{ years for night work / hazardous duty}
\end{cases}
}
$$

A **Night** = any period of **≥ 9 consecutive hours between 00:00 and 05:00**.

### 2.3.2 Medical Fitness (Reg 1.2)

Every seafarer must hold a **valid medical certificate** confirming fitness for the duty they will perform.

- **Maximum validity**: **2 years** (for under 18: 1 year).
- Issued by a **duly qualified medical practitioner**.
- Colour vision, hearing, and general physical fitness tested.

### 2.3.3 Training & Qualifications (Reg 1.3)

- Must hold **STCW** certificates for their rank.
- Each Member State must promote **on-board training** and career development.
- Access to **educational and vocational training programmes** must be available.

### 2.3.4 Recruitment & Placement Services (Reg 1.4)

This regulates both **public and private** manning agencies.

- **No fees or charges** to the seafarer for placement (anti-exploitation clause).
- Agencies must be **licensed, certified, or regulated**.
- Records must be maintained for **5 years**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The anti-fee clause is unique. It is the seafarer's protection against corrupt manning agents, especially in developing countries like the Philippines, India, etc.

## 2.4 Title 2 — Conditions of Employment

### 2.4.1 Seafarers' Employment Agreement (SEA) — Reg 2.1

The SEA is the **single most important document** for a seafarer. It must be:
- **In writing** (hard copy + copy for seafarer).
- Signed by both parties.
- Contain **minimum particulars** (Standard A2.1.4(a)–(n)).

**Minimum Particulars Required in SEA:**

| S.No. | Particulars |
|---|---|
| (a) | Full name, place of birth, date of birth |
| (b) | Place and date of SEA |
| (c) | Name and address of shipowner |
| (d) | Capacity/rank |
| (e) | Wages amount & method of payment |
| (f) | Paid annual leave |
| (g) | Conditions for termination |
| (h) | Health & social security benefits |
| (i) | Maximum duration of contract |
| (j) | Repatriation entitlement |
| (k) | Repatriation costs borne by |
| (l) | Terms of collective bargaining (if any) |
| (m) | Reference to grievance procedures |
| (n) | Period of notice for termination |

### 2.4.2 Wages (Reg 2.2)

$$ \boxed{
\text{Payment Rule} = \text{Monthly or twice monthly, in legal tender, via bank transfer}
}
$$

- **Wage records** must be kept for **3 years**.
- **Allotment of wages**: Seafarer can assign portion to family via allotment slips.
- **Tax deduction** must be in line with national law.

### 2.4.3 Hours of Work & Rest (Reg 2.3) — *High-Yield*

| Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|
| Maximum working hours (24-hr) | **14 hours** |
| Minimum rest hours (24-hr) | **10 hours** |
| Maximum working hours (7 days) | **72 hours** |
| Minimum rest hours (7 days) | **77 hours** |
| Rest period subdivision | **Not more than 2 periods**, one being **≥ 6 hours** |
| Musters / drills | Counted as **working hours** |
| Emergency drills | Rest period may be split into **3 periods** for ≥ 1 day/week |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Drills and musters ARE counted as work hours. This is a frequently tested point.

### 2.4.4 Entitlement to Leave (Reg 2.4)

$$ \text{Annual Leave} = 2.5 \text{ calendar days per month of service} $$

For a 12-month contract:

$$ \text{Annual Leave} = 2.5 \times 12 = 30 \text{ calendar days (minimum)} $$

Leaves must be **paid** and cannot be replaced by financial compensation, except upon termination.

### 2.4.5 Repatriation (Reg 2.5)

Every seafarer has the right to be **repatriated** at the shipowner's expense, with the following entitlements:

- Adequate **air, sea, or land transport**.
- **Subsistence allowance** from termination to arrival.
- **Accommodation and food** during travel.
- **Medical care** if required.

**Repatriation Triggers:**
- Expiry of SEA.
- Sickness, injury, or inability to work.
- Shipwreck, sale of ship, change of flag.
- Termination for just cause.

### 2.4.6 Crew Accommodation (Reg 2.6) — Bridge to Title 3

This regulation states that the **shipowner must ensure** that accommodation standards in Title 3 are maintained throughout the voyage.

## 2.5 Title 3 — Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food and Catering

### 2.5.1 Accommodation Standards (Reg 3.1)

Detailed prescriptive standards covering:

| Parameter | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Headroom** | Adequate; minimum **≥ 203 cm** (in new builds ≥ 2000 GT) |
| **Ventilation** | Air-conditioning / mechanical ventilation in new ships |
| **Heating** | Adequate heating system |
| **Lighting** | Natural + electric; emergency lighting |
| **Noise & vibration** | Minimised per ILO/IMO codes |
| **Berths** | **One per person** (no sharing in new builds) |
| **Floor area** | Minimum specified per person per rank |
| **Sanitary facilities** | Adequate toilets, washbasins, showers per crew |
| **Hospital** | Required for ships carrying **> 15 crew** for voyage > 3 days |

### 2.5.2 Food and Catering (Reg 3.2)

**Mandatory requirements:**
- Ships must provide **wholesome, nutritious food** free of cost.
- **Qualified Cook** required for ships with **> 10 crew** OR voyages **> 3 days**.
- Cook must be **trained, qualified, and competent**.
- Food storage, hygiene, and inspection standards must be maintained.
- **Galley inspection** by **Master + Cook** at least once every **7 days**.

## 2.6 Title 4 — Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare and Social Security

### 2.6.1 Medical Care on Board & Ashore (Reg 4.1)

| Ships carrying | Doctor required |
|---|---|
| **> 100 crew** OR **voyage > 3 days** | **One qualified medical doctor** (separate from Master) |
| Smaller ships | **At least one medical practitioner** (Master can hold medical certificate) |

**Medicines & Equipment:** Must comply with **WHO International Medical Guide for Ships** (3rd edition).
**Telemedical Advice Service (TMAS):** Must be available **24/7** via satellite/radio.

### 2.6.2 Shipowner's Liability (Reg 4.2)

$$ \boxed{
\text{Shipowner's Liability} = \begin{cases}
\text{Forward wages} \leq 16 \text{ weeks from injury date} \\
\text{Medical expenses including treatment} \\
\text{Repatriation costs}
\end{cases}
}
$$

- **Death benefit** to dependents if death arises from employment.
- Applies to all seafarers, regardless of nationality.

### 2.6.3 Health & Safety Protection (Reg 4.3)

- **On-board Occupational Safety & Health (OSH)** programmes.
- **Accident prevention** through engineering, training, PPE.
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** for hazardous work.
- **Reporting of accidents** to flag State.
- **Joint OHS committees** with seafarer representation.

### 2.6.4 Access to Shore-Based Welfare (Reg 4.4)

- Member States must promote **port welfare facilities**: clubs, hostels, religious centres.
- **Welfare boards** must be set up.
- **Information board** in port cities for seafarers.
- Transport arrangements from port to town facilities.

### 2.6.5 Social Security (Reg 4.5)

- Seafarers must be entitled to **social security protection equivalent to shore workers**.
- Benefits must be **portable** across countries.
- Encourage **bilateral and multilateral agreements** between States.
- Areas: medical care, sickness, unemployment, old-age, employment injury, family, maternity, invalidity, survivors' benefits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Social Security is the **most aspirational** provision. Most countries struggle to fully comply. The "portability" principle is unique to maritime labour.

# ⚓ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — MLC Operational Logic

## 3.1 The MLC Compliance Cascade — A Step-by-Step Framework

This is the operational chain that makes the MLC 2006 work in practice:

### Step 1: Ratification by Member State

A State ratifies MLC 2006, thereby accepting **all obligations** in Articles + Regulations + Part A of the Code.

> *"By accepting the obligations, the Member assumes a general duty to ensure, so far as practicable, that the provisions are applied to all ships flying its flag and to seafarers serving on such ships."* — Article IV

### Step 2: National Legislation & Enforcement

The State must enact **national laws and regulations** to give effect to MLC standards. These laws must be:
- **As effective** as MLC provisions.
- **Enforced** through inspections, monitoring, and penalties.

### Step 3: Certification of Ships

Ships must obtain **two certificates**:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{MLC Certificates} &= \underbrace{\text{MLC Maritime Labour Certificate}}_{\text{Issued by Flag State, valid 5 years}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\text{Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC)}}_{\text{Two parts: I - national requirements; II - shipowner measures}}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

- **Interim Certificate** may be issued for **6 months** for new ships or flag changes.
- **Intermediate inspection** required between **2nd and 3rd year** of certificate validity.

### Step 4: Flag State Inspections

The flag State must:
- Maintain a **system of inspections** on ships flying its flag.
- Employ **qualified inspectors** (Article V).
- Issue certificates only after verifying compliance.

### Step 5: Port State Control (PSC)

> *"A ship of a non-ratifying State may be inspected by any Member State that has ratified the Convention to verify compliance with MLC."* — Article V, Para 4

Port State may:
- **Inspect** ships calling at its ports.
- **Detain** non-compliant ships (similar to SOLAS/MARPOL detentions).
- **Report** violations to flag State.

### Step 6: On-Board Complaint Procedure

Seafarers have the right to **complain directly to the Master** or to **the next higher authority** without retaliation. Seafarers can also **apply directly to the flag State** in case of unresolved complaint.

### Step 7: Reporting & Review

Member States submit **annual reports** on their MLC implementation to the ILO. The ILO conducts a **Tripartite Review** of working and living conditions periodically.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Annual Leave Calculation

**Given:** A seafarer signs a 9-month SEA. How much paid annual leave is due?

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.

$$
\text{Annual Leave} = 2.5 \text{ cal days} \times \text{Number of months of service}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
\text{Annual Leave} = 2.5 \times 9
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.5 \times 9 &= 2.5 \times (10 - 1) \\
&= 25 - 2.5 \\
&= 22.5 \text{ calendar days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** 22.5 calendar days of paid annual leave.

**Rule Note:** Public holidays may be added but not counted as part of minimum leave. Cannot be replaced by pay-in-lieu during service — only on termination.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Hours of Work Check

**Given:** A motorman works the following schedule in a week:
- Monday: 12 hrs work
- Tuesday: 13 hrs work
- Wednesday: 14 hrs work
- Thursday: 11 hrs work
- Friday: 13 hrs work
- Saturday: 9 hrs work
- Sunday: 0 hrs work (shore leave)

**Check Compliance:**

**Step 1:** Total weekly work.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Work} &= 12 + 13 + 14 + 11 + 13 + 9 + 0 \\
&= 72 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with limits.

$$
\text{Limit} = 72 \text{ hrs/week (max)} \quad \text{and} \quad 14 \text{ hrs/24 hrs (max)}
$$

**Step 3:** Check daily limits.

| Day | Work | Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mon | 12 | 14 | OK |
| Tue | 13 | 14 | OK |
| Wed | 14 | 14 | OK (at limit) |
| Thu | 11 | 14 | OK |
| Fri | 13 | 14 | OK |
| Sat | 9 | 14 | OK |

**Step 4:** Check rest in any 24-hour period.

If the seafarer works 14 hrs on Wednesday, then rest = 10 hrs. Compliant.

**Result:** **Compliant** with both daily and weekly limits.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of ILO Seafarer Conventions Pre- and Post-MLC

| Convention | Pre-MLC Status | Post-MLC 2006 Position |
|---|---|---|
| C 14 (1921) – Hours of Work | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 2.3 |
| C 73 (1946) – Medical Examination | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 1.2 |
| C 92 (1949) – Crew Accommodation | Active | Replaced by MLC Title 3 |
| C 108 (1958) – Seafarers' Identity Documents | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 1.4 |
| C 133 (1970) – Crew Accommodation (Supp.) | Active | Replaced by MLC Title 3 |
| C 147 (1976) – Minimum Standards | Active | Updated by MLC |
| C 163 (1987) – Welfare | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 4.4 |
| C 164 (1987) – Health Protection | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 4.3 |
| C 165 (1987) – Social Security | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 4.5 |
| C 166 (1987) – Repatriation | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 2.5 |
| C 179 (1996) – Recruitment | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 1.4 |
| C 180 (1996) – Hours of Work | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 2.3 |
| C 185 (2003) – Seafarers' ID | Active | Replaced by MLC Reg 1.4 |

**Conclusion:** MLC 2006 consolidated and **modernised** over 68 earlier ILO instruments into a single, coherent, easily enforceable framework.

# ⚓ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006. Why is it called the "Fourth Pillar" of international maritime law?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006** is an International Labour Organization (ILO) convention that sets comprehensive minimum working and living standards for seafarers worldwide, consolidating over 68 previous ILO maritime instruments. It is structured into 5 Titles, supported by Articles, Regulations, and a Code (Part A mandatory, Part B guidelines).

It is called the **"Fourth Pillar"** of international maritime law because it complements the three other major IMO conventions governing shipping:
- **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) — Safety Pillar
- **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution) — Environmental Pillar
- **STCW** (Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping) — Training Pillar
- **MLC, 2006** — Labour and Welfare Pillar

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **ILO** as the parent organisation. Marks deducted for not attributing MLC correctly.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - July 2022]`
**Explain the meaning and significance of the "no more favourable treatment" clause in MLC 2006.**

**Model Answer:**

The **"no more favourable treatment" clause** is provided in **Article V, Paragraph 7** of MLC 2006. It mandates that when a ship of a **non-ratifying State** is inspected in a port of a ratifying State, the non-ratifying ship **must not receive more favourable treatment** than a ship flying the flag of a ratifying State.

**Significance:**
1. **Prevents flag-State shopping** — discourages shipowners from registering under lax flag States.
2. **Universalises standards** — all ships calling at ports of ratifying countries are effectively bound by MLC standards.
3. **Eliminates unfair competition** — ensures that compliant shipowners are not commercially disadvantaged.
4. **Strengthens global maritime labour regime** — gives the MLC a quasi-universal application, similar to MARPOL and SOLAS.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### 📝 Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** *Discuss the minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship as stipulated under Title 1 of MLC 2006. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the conditions of employment under Title 2 of MLC 2006. (7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Title 1 of MLC 2006** prescribes four essential minimum requirements:

**(i) Minimum Age (Reg 1.1):**
- The minimum age for working on a ship is **16 years**.
- Night work and hazardous duties require a minimum age of **18 years**.
- A "night" is defined as any period of **9 consecutive hours between 00:00 and 05:00**.

**(ii) Medical Fitness (Reg 1.2):**
- Every seafarer must hold a **valid medical certificate** confirming fitness for the duties they will perform.
- Certificates are issued by a **duly qualified medical practitioner**.
- Maximum validity: **2 years** (1 year for seafarers under 18).
- Certificate is colour-vision and hearing tested.

**(iii) Training and Qualifications (Reg 1.3):**
- Seafarers must hold **STCW** certificates appropriate to their rank.
- Member States must promote **on-board training** and continuing education.
- The flag State must ensure that ships comply with STCW requirements.

**(iv) Recruitment and Placement (Reg 1.4):**
- All seafarer recruitment and placement services (public or private) must be **licensed, certified, or regulated**.
- **No fees** may be charged to seafarers for placement services — this prevents exploitation.
- Records of recruitment must be maintained for **5 years**.
- Aim is to protect seafarers from **fraudulent manning agents**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Title 2 of MLC 2006** covers the core conditions of employment:

**(i) Seafarers' Employment Agreement — SEA (Reg 2.1):**
- A **written, signed agreement** is mandatory between the seafarer and the shipowner.
- The SEA must include **14 minimum particulars** (per Standard A2.1.4): identity, wages, leave, working hours, repatriation, health, grievance procedure, etc.
- The seafarer must receive a **signed copy** for personal retention.

**(ii) Wages (Reg 2.2):**
- Wages must be paid **regularly (monthly or twice monthly)**, in **legal tender**, and may be transferred via bank.
- Wage records must be kept for **3 years**.
- Allotment of wages to family back home is allowed.

**(iii) Hours of Work and Rest (Reg 2.3):**
- Maximum **14 hours of work per 24 hours**.
- Minimum **10 hours of rest per 24 hours**.
- Maximum **72 hours of work per 7 days**.
- Minimum **77 hours of rest per 7 days**.
- Rest may be split into **2 periods**, one of which must be **at least 6 consecutive hours**.

**(iv) Entitlement to Leave (Reg 2.4):**
- Minimum **2.5 calendar days of paid annual leave per month** of service.
- For a 12-month contract, this amounts to **30 calendar days**.
- Public holidays may be in addition to this leave.
- Pay-in-lieu is permitted only on termination.

**(v) Repatriation (Reg 2.5):**
- Every seafarer has the right to be **repatriated at the shipowner's expense**.
- Triggered by: expiry of SEA, sickness, injury, shipwreck, sale of ship, change of flag, or termination for just cause.
- Entitlements include: air/sea/land transport, subsistence allowance, accommodation, food, and medical care.

**(vi) Crew Accommodation (Reg 2.6):**
- This is a bridge regulation to Title 3 — the shipowner must ensure that accommodation standards in Title 3 are maintained throughout the voyage.

---

### 📝 Question B: `[KTU University Exam - July 2023]` — **14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *Elaborate on the standards of accommodation, recreational facilities, and food and catering under Title 3 of MLC 2006. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss the provisions relating to health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security under Title 4 of MLC 2006. (7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Title 3 of MLC 2006** addresses living conditions aboard ships:

**(i) Accommodation Standards (Reg 3.1):**
Accommodation must be of decent size, quality, and with adequate facilities. Key requirements include:
- **Headroom**: Adequate, with minimum prescribed levels (≥ 203 cm in new builds).
- **Ventilation**: Adequate, mechanical ventilation in new ships.
- **Heating**: Adequate heating system.
- **Lighting**: Natural + electric + emergency lighting.
- **Berths**: One per person (no sharing in new ships).
- **Sanitary facilities**: Adequate toilets, washbasins, showers per crew.
- **Hospital**: Required for ships carrying > 15 crew on voyages > 3 days.

**(ii) Recreational Facilities:**
- Member States must ensure that ships provide adequate **recreational facilities** for all seafarers.
- Includes libraries, TV, sports equipment, internet access, etc.
- Particularly important for mental health and well-being.

**(iii) Food and Catering (Reg 3.2):**
- Ships must provide **wholesome and nutritious food** free of cost.
- **Qualified cook** required for ships with > 10 crew OR voyages > 3 days.
- Cook must be trained and competent.
- **Galley inspection** by **Master + Cook** at least once every 7 days.
- Food storage, hygiene, and preparation standards.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Title 4 of MLC 2006** covers health, welfare, and social security:

**(i) Medical Care on Board and Ashore (Reg 4.1):**
- Ships with > 100 crew OR voyages > 3 days must carry a **qualified medical doctor**.
- Smaller ships must have at least one **responsible medical person** (often the Master).
- **Medicines** must comply with **WHO International Medical Guide**.
- **Telemedical Advice Service (TMAS)** must be available 24/7.

**(ii) Shipowner's Liability (Reg 4.2):**
- Shipowner is liable for:
  - **Forward wages** for up to **16 weeks** from date of injury or sickness.
  - **Medical expenses** until full recovery or repatriation.
  - **Death benefit** to dependents in case of death arising from employment.
  - This applies **regardless of the seafarer's nationality**.

**(iii) Health and Safety Protection (Reg 4.3):**
- Mandatory **Occupational Safety and Health (OSH)** programmes.
- **PPE** provision for hazardous work.
- **Accident reporting** to flag State.
- **Joint OHS committees** with seafarer representation.

**(iv) Access to Shore-Based Welfare (Reg 4.4):**
- Member States must promote **port welfare facilities**: clubs, hostels, religious centres.
- **Welfare boards** in port cities.
- **Information board** and transport arrangements for seafarers in ports.

**(v) Social Security (Reg 4.5):**
- Seafarers must have **social security protection equivalent to shore workers**.
- Benefits include: medical care, sickness, unemployment, old-age, employment injury, family, maternity, invalidity, survivors' benefits.
- **Portability** of benefits across borders is a key principle.
- Encourages **bilateral/multilateral agreements** between States.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Forgetting Code Parts A and B**: When describing Title provisions, students often quote only the Regulation and forget to mention the Part A (mandatory) and Part B (guideline) structure. Always mention the **Standard + Guideline** structure in long answers.
> 2. **Confusing MLC with SOLAS/STCW**: MLC is an **ILO** convention, not an IMO convention. Make sure you attribute it correctly to the ILO.
> 3. **Hours of Work Numerical Errors**: A very common error is stating "12 hours rest" instead of **10 hours rest per 24 hours**. Get the numbers right.
> 4. **Omitting Drills/Musters**: Drills ARE counted as work. This is a frequently tested distinction.
> 5. **Annual Leave Calculation**: Use **2.5 calendar days/month**, not 30 days/year directly (since SEA durations may not be exactly 12 months).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚢

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **MLC 2006 = ILO Convention, Fourth Pillar of Maritime Law**
- [x] Adopted: **7 February 2006** | Entry into force: **20 August 2013**
- [x] Structure: **Articles (I–XVI) + Regulations (5 Titles) + Code (A mandatory, B guidelines)**
- [x] **Minimum Age** = 16 (general) / 18 (hazardous)
- [x] **Medical Certificate** validity = 2 years (1 year if < 18)
- [x] **No fees** charged to seafarers for recruitment
- [x] **SEA** = written, signed, with 14 minimum particulars
- [x] **Hours of Work**: Max 14 hrs/day, 72 hrs/week
- [x] **Rest**: Min 10 hrs/day, 77 hrs/week; 1 period ≥ 6 hrs
- [x] **Annual Leave**: 2.5 cal days/month = 30 days/year
- [x] **Repatriation** = at shipowner's expense, with subsistence
- [x] **Qualified Cook** required for ships > 10 crew OR voyages > 3 days
- [x] **Ship Doctor** required for ships > 100 crew OR voyages > 3 days
- [x] **Shipowner's Liability** = 16 weeks forward wages + medical + repatriation
- [x] **TMAS** (Telemedical Advice Service) must be available 24/7
- [x] **Galley Inspection** by Master + Cook every 7 days
- [x] **Social Security** must be portable across borders
- [x] **DMLC (Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance)** + **Maritime Labour Certificate** (valid 5 years)
- [x] **Intermediate Inspection** between Year 2 and 3
- [x] **Port State Control** + **Flag State Inspections** = dual enforcement
- [x] **"No more favourable treatment"** = port State may inspect non-ratifying flag ships
- [x] **MLC scope**: 500 GT+ ships on international voyages
- [x] **Tripartite origin** = Governments + Shipowners + Seafarers' Unions

### 🎯 Most-Likely KTU 2024 Exam Questions

1. Explain the structure of MLC 2006 with reference to Articles, Regulations, and the Code.
2. Discuss the minimum requirements for seafarers under Title 1.
3. Critically analyse the conditions of employment under Title 2.
4. Explain the standards of accommodation, food, and catering under Title 3.
5. Discuss the provisions on health, medical care, welfare, and social security under Title 4.
6. Explain the compliance and enforcement mechanism under Title 5.
7. Compare the MLC 2006 with STCW and SOLAS.
8. Discuss the role of the Flag State and Port State in MLC enforcement.
9. Critically evaluate the "no more favourable treatment" clause.
10. Comment on the implementation challenges of MLC 2006 in developing maritime nations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to SOLAS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to SOLAS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Introduction to SOLAS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea)** is the most important international treaty concerning the safety of merchant ships. The first version was adopted in **1914** in response to the sinking of the RMS *Titanic* in 1912. The current version is the **SOLAS 1974 Convention**, which entered into force on **25 May 1980**, and is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

The Convention prescribes minimum standards for the construction, equipment, and operation of ships, compatible with their safety. Flag States are responsible for ensuring that ships under their flag comply with the Convention's requirements, and a number of certificates are prescribed in the Convention as proof of compliance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Maritime Rulebook of the Sea"**
>
> Imagine a city with millions of vehicles, no traffic police at every corner, and the road is the open ocean. SOLAS acts as the **universal traffic and safety rulebook** for all ships. Just as road traffic laws mandate seatbelts, brakes, indicators, and speed limits, SOLAS mandates life-jackets, fire extinguishers, watertight bulkheads, navigation lights, and radio equipment. Without SOLAS, every coastal state would invent its own rules — chaos. With SOLAS, there is **one global standard** that every ship must obey, regardless of its nationality.

### 1.3 Key Historical Milestones

| Year | Event |
|------|-------|
| **1912** | Sinking of RMS *Titanic* (1,517 lives lost) triggered need for international maritime safety rules |
| **1914** | First SOLAS Convention adopted |
| **1948** | IMO established (originally as IMCO — Inter-Governmental Maritime Consultative Organization) |
| **1960** | Second major SOLAS version adopted (reflecting post-WWII technological progress) |
| **1974** | Current SOLAS Convention adopted — incorporates "tacit amendment" procedure |
| **1980** | SOLAS 1974 entered into force (25 May 1980) |

### 1.4 The "Tacit Acceptance" Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SOLAS 1974 introduced a revolutionary procedure called **"Tacit Acceptance"** for amendments. Under this, amendments enter into force on a set date unless a specified number of States (more than one-third) object within a fixed period. This speeds up the update process and keeps the treaty current with technology.

### 1.5 The Four Pillars of SOLAS Compliance

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars:**
>
> 1. **Flag State Implementation** — The country where the ship is registered ensures compliance.
> 2. **Survey and Certification** — Ships undergo regular inspections and receive statutory certificates.
> 3. **Port State Control** — Foreign port authorities can inspect visiting ships to verify compliance.
> 4. **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** — Classification societies authorized by Flag States to perform surveys on their behalf.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Structural Framework of SOLAS

## 2.1 Chapter-wise Architecture of SOLAS 1974

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SOLAS 1974 is organized into **12 Chapters**, further divided into **Parts, Regulations, and Annexed Codes**. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for KTU examination purposes.

### 2.2 Detailed Chapter Breakdown (High-Yield Table)

| Chapter | Title | Core Subject Matter | KTU Significance |
|---------|-------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Chapter I** | General Provisions | Definitions, survey procedures, certification, port state control | Foundational; defines flag/port state duties |
| **Chapter II-1** | Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery & Electrical Installations | Watertight bulkheads, subdivision, stability, main & auxiliary machinery, electrical installations | Critical for naval architects; Load Line interaction |
| **Chapter II-2** | Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction | Fire safety construction, detection systems, suppression, escape routes | FTP Code & FSS Code are integrated here |
| **Chapter III** | Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements | Lifeboats, life-rafts, life-jackets, immersion suits, muster lists, drills | LSA Code annexed; post-Titanic heritage |
| **Chapter IV** | Radiocommunications | GMDSS (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System), radio equipment, watchkeeping | Operative from 1992 onwards |
| **Chapter V** | Safety of Navigation | Navigation equipment, ECDIS, AIS, routeing, weather routing, bridge procedures | Closely aligned with COLREGs & STCW |
| **Chapter VI** | Carriage of Cargoes | Stowage, securing, grain carriage, cargo information | Tied to CSS Code |
| **Chapter VII** | Carriage of Dangerous Goods | IMDG Code application, packaging, stowage, segregation | Tightly linked to IMDG Code (Chapter VII Part A) |
| **Chapter VIII** | Nuclear Ships | Safety measures for nuclear-powered merchant ships | Rare; mostly historical relevance |
| **Chapter IX** | Management for the Safe Operation of Ships | **ISM Code** — Safety Management System (SMS) | Mandatory for most commercial vessels |
| **Chapter X** | Safety Measures for High-Speed Craft | HSC Code (1994/2000) | Specialized craft regulations |
| **Chapter XI-1** | Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety | Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR), enhanced surveys, authorized classification societies | Post-Piper Alpha & Herald of Free Enterprise reforms |
| **Chapter XI-2** | Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security | **ISPS Code** — Ship & Port Facility Security | Post-9/11 maritime security framework |
| **Chapter XII** | Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers | Bulk carrier-specific stability, hatch covers, double-side skin | Post-Bulk Jupiter & similar casualties |

### 2.3 Key Mandatory Codes Annexed/Referenced by SOLAS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Codes List:**
>
> - **ISM Code** (Chapter IX) — International Safety Management Code
> - **ISPS Code** (Chapter XI-2) — International Ship and Port Facility Security Code
> - **IMDG Code** (Chapter VII) — International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code
> - **LSA Code** (Chapter III) — Life-Saving Appliances Code
> - **FSS Code** (Chapter II-2) — Fire Safety Systems Code
> - **FTP Code** (Chapter II-2) — Fire Test Procedures Code
> - **HSC Code** (Chapter X) — High-Speed Craft Code
> - **CSS Code** (Chapter VI) — Code of Safe Practice for Cargo Stowage and Securing
> - **Grain Code** (Chapter VI) — International Code for the Safe Carriage of Grain in Bulk
> - **IGC Code** — International Code for the Construction and Equipment of Ships Carrying Liquefied Gases in Bulk
> - **IGF Code** — International Code of Safety for Ships using Gases or other Low-flashpoint Fuels
> - **Polar Code** — Ships operating in polar waters
> - **SPS Code** (Chapter VIII area) — Special Purpose Ships Code
> - **TONNAGE 1969** — International Convention on Tonnage Measurement
> - **LOAD LINE 1966** — International Convention on Load Lines

### 2.4 Statutory Certificates Prescribed by SOLAS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SOLAS Chapter I, Regulation 12 enumerates the certificates that must be issued. The following table provides a high-yield reference:

| Certificate | Issuing Authority | Survey Type |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Passenger Ship Safety Certificate | Flag State / RO | Annual + Renewal |
| Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate | Flag State / RO | Annual + Renewal |
| Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate | Flag State / RO | Annual + Renewal |
| Cargo Ship Safety Radiotelegraphy/Radiotelephony Certificate | Flag State / RO | Annual + Renewal |
| Exemption Certificate | Flag State | Conditional |
| Nuclear Passenger Ship / Cargo Ship Safety Certificate | Flag State | Special |

### 2.5 The Regulatory Hierarchy — A Pedagogical View

SOLAS regulations follow a precise **3-tier structure**:

$$\text{Chapter} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Part} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Regulation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Paragraph/Sub-paragraph}$$

For example:

$$\text{Chapter II-1} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Part A — General} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Regulation 1} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sub-paragraph 1.1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Naval architects, shipbuilders, classification surveyors, marine superintendents, and maritime lawyers all reference this hierarchy when:
> - Designing new ships (ensuring regulatory compliance from keel-laying)
> - Conducting classification surveys
> - Drafting technical specifications for ship repair
> - Litigating maritime casualties and limitation of liability
> - Preparing port state control responses
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Analytical Matrix — Evolution, Ratification & Compliance Logic

## 3.1 Analytical Trace: Evolution of SOLAS Versions

Although SOLAS is a legal/management subject and does not have engineering derivations, the following analytical trace provides a **step-by-step logical progression** that examiners often expect:

**Step 1 — Identify the Triggering Event**

The defining trigger was the sinking of the RMS *Titanic* on 15 April 1912. The loss of **1,517 lives** in a single maritime incident exposed the absence of universal safety standards.

**Step 2 — Map the Diplomatic Response**

A diplomatic conference was held in London in 1913–1914, resulting in the first SOLAS Convention signed in 1914 (effective 1 July 1915 — but never fully implemented due to WWI).

**Step 3 — Trace Subsequent Revisions**

$$\text{1914 (1st)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{1929 (2nd)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{1948 (3rd)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{1960 (4th)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{1974 (Current)}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Why 1974 is "Current"**

The 1974 Convention consolidated all previous versions and, crucially, introduced the **tacit acceptance procedure** (Regulation VIII of the Convention). This was a paradigm shift:

$$\text{Old Procedure: Explicit ratification by each State} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{New Procedure: Tacit acceptance, with override option}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Effective Ratification**

As of recent records, the SOLAS 1974 Convention has been ratified by more than **160 States**, representing over **99% of the world gross tonnage** of merchant shipping.

## 3.2 Compliance Pathway — A Procedural Algorithm

The following block represents the **standard compliance pathway** that any commercial vessel must follow:

**Stage 1 — Design Phase**

The naval architect consults SOLAS Chapters II-1 and II-2 to ensure subdivision, stability, and fire protection comply.

**Stage 2 — Keel-Laying & Construction Survey**

Classification society appointed as Recognized Organization (RO) supervises construction under SOLAS Chapter I, Regulation 7.

**Stage 3 — Commissioning Surveys**

Initial survey verifies compliance with all applicable regulations before the ship is registered.

**Stage 4 — Issuance of Statutory Certificates**

All certificates listed in Regulation I/12 are issued with a validity of **5 years** (subject to annual/periodic/renewal surveys).

**Stage 5 — Operational Phase**

The ISM Code (Chapter IX) requires the shipping company to maintain a Safety Management System (SMS), conduct **internal audits**, and undergo **external audits** by DOC/SMC auditors.

**Stage 6 — Renewal & Continuous Compliance**

$$\text{Annual Survey} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Periodical Survey} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Renewal Survey (every 5 years)}$$

**Stage 7 — Port State Control Overlay**

Any ship calling at a foreign port may be inspected by that port's authorities (PSCO — Port State Control Officer) for evidence of SOLAS non-compliance. Deficiencies can lead to **detention**.

## 3.3 Flag State vs. Port State — The Jurisdictional Dichotomy

| Aspect | Flag State | Port State |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Primary duty | Issue certificates; ensure compliance | Inspect foreign ships; verify compliance |
| Legal basis | SOLAS Chapter I, Reg. 6 | SOLAS Chapter I, Reg. 19 |
| Tools | Surveys, audits, RO oversight | PSC inspections, deficiency rectification, detention |
| Sanctions | Detention, suspension of certificates | Detention until deficiencies rectified |
| Frequency | Continuous (via RO) | Random/intermittent based on risk profile (New Inspection Regime) |

## 3.4 The GMDSS Revolution (Chapter IV)

A **conceptual derivation** of how Chapter IV transformed distress communications:

**Pre-1992 System:**

$$\text{Morse Code (500 kHz)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Manual watchkeeping} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ship-to-ship + ship-to-shore distress alerts}$$

**Post-1992 (GMDSS) System:**

$$\text{DSC (Digital Selective Calling)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{INMARSAT / COSPAS-SARSAT} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Automated distress alerts, 9 sea areas}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** GMDSS divides the world's oceans into **9 Sea Areas (A1 to A4, plus A5)** based on the type of shore-based radio infrastructure available. Each sea area mandates specific equipment carriage requirements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 SOLAS 1974 — Chapter Architecture (Block Diagram)

## 4.2 SOLAS Compliance Workflow (Sequential Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C["Initial Survey & Certification"]:::step

```

## 4.3 Concept Relationship Map (Subject Anchors)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

> **Q: Define SOLAS Convention. Mention the year of its first adoption and the year of the current version in force.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
SOLAS stands for the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea**. It is the most important international treaty concerning the safety of merchant ships, prescribing minimum standards for the construction, equipment, and operation of ships. It is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a specialized agency of the United Nations headquartered in London.

**Historical Dates (1 Mark):**
The first version was adopted in **1914** in response to the RMS *Titanic* disaster of 1912. The current version is the **SOLAS 1974 Convention**, which entered into force on **25 May 1980**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

> **Q: What is the "Tacit Acceptance" procedure under SOLAS 1974? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The **Tacit Acceptance Procedure** is a streamlined amendment mechanism introduced by the 1974 SOLAS Convention. Under this procedure, amendments to the Convention (except those to Articles of the Convention itself) are deemed to be accepted unless more than **one-third of the Contracting Parties** notify objections within a prescribed period (usually 12 months). After this, the amendment enters into force on a specified date.

**Importance (1 Mark):**
This procedure enables SOLAS to keep pace with rapidly evolving maritime technology without waiting for slow, individual State ratifications — making the treaty **dynamic, responsive, and globally enforceable** for the shipping industry.

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper Adaptation]**

> **Q (a)** Explain in detail the **organizational structure of the SOLAS 1974 Convention**, covering its 12 chapters and the main subject matter of each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Discuss the **ISM Code** under SOLAS Chapter IX, its objectives, and the role of the Designated Person Ashore (DPA). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Q (a) — Chapter-wise Structure (7 Marks)

SOLAS 1974 is organized into **12 Chapters** that systematically address all aspects of maritime safety:

**(1) Chapter I — General Provisions (1 Mark)**
Contains definitions, application, exemptions, surveys, certification, and Port State Control procedures. Regulation 12 specifies the certificates to be issued.

**(2) Chapter II-1 — Construction: Subdivision, Stability, Machinery & Electrical Installations (1 Mark)**
Addresses watertight subdivision, damage stability calculations, machinery installation standards, and electrical system safety.

**(3) Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction (1 Mark)**
Covers structural fire protection, detection systems, fire-fighting equipment, escape routes, and incorporates the FTP and FSS Codes.

**(4) Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (1 Mark)**
Prescribes lifeboats, life-rafts, life-jackets, immersion suits, and Personal Locator Beacons (PLBs). LSA Code is annexed.

**(5) Chapter IV — Radiocommunications (1 Mark)**
Mandates the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)** with carriage requirements across 9 sea areas.

**(6) Chapter V — Safety of Navigation (1 Mark)**
Covers navigation equipment (ECDIS, AIS, gyro-compass), routeing measures, weather services, and bridge procedures.

**(7) Chapter VI — Carriage of Cargoes (0.5 Mark)**
Covers stowage, securing, grain carriage, and incorporates the CSS Code.

**(8) Chapter VII — Carriage of Dangerous Goods (0.5 Mark)**
Incorporates the IMDG Code for safe packaging, marking, and stowage of dangerous goods.

**(9) Chapters VIII & X — Nuclear Ships & High-Speed Craft (0.5 Mark)**
Specialized craft regulations.

**(10) Chapters IX, XI-1, XI-2, XII — Management, Security, Bulk Carriers (0.5 Mark)**
ISM Code, ISPS Code, and bulk carrier safety measures.

#### Model Answer for Q (b) — ISM Code (7 Marks)

**Background (1 Mark):**
The **International Safety Management (ISM) Code** was made mandatory under Chapter IX of SOLAS 1974. It was developed in response to several major casualties in the late 1980s and early 1990s, including the **Herald of Free Enterprise** (1987) and the **Exxon Valdez** (1989).

**Objectives (2 Marks):**
- Establish a structured **Safety Management System (SMS)** for ship operators.
- Ensure safe practices in ship operation and a safe working environment.
- Establish safeguards against all identified risks.
- Continuously improve safety management skills of shore-based and shipboard personnel.

**Application (1 Mark):**
Mandatory for all ships of **500 GT and above** engaged in international voyages, including passenger ships, oil tankers, chemical tankers, gas carriers, bulk carriers, and cargo high-speed craft.

**Key Documents (1 Mark):**
- **Document of Compliance (DOC)** — issued to the shipping company
- **Safety Management Certificate (SMC)** — issued to each individual ship

**Designated Person Ashore (DPA) (2 Marks):**
The DPA is a shore-based senior person with direct access to the highest level of management. The DPA's responsibilities include:
- Monitoring the safety and pollution prevention aspects of each ship's operation.
- Ensuring adequate resources and shore-based support are applied to each ship.
- Acting as the **focal point** between ship and shore for SMS matters.
- Reviewing the SMS for effectiveness and reporting deficiencies to management.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students often lose marks by: (i) confusing the **ISM Code** (Chapter IX) with the **ISPS Code** (Chapter XI-2) — remember ISM is for **Safety Management**, ISPS is for **Security**; (ii) failing to mention the **DOC and SMC** distinctly; (iii) omitting the role of the **DPA** as the "bridge" between ship and shore; (iv) not linking the ISM Code to its historical triggers (Herald of Free Enterprise casualty).

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Adaptation]**

> **Q (a)** What is the **ISPS Code**? Explain its origin, objectives, and the three security levels. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Write a detailed note on the **Port State Control** mechanism under SOLAS Chapter I, Regulation 19. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Q (a) — ISPS Code (7 Marks)

**Origin (2 Marks):**
The **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** was adopted by a Diplomatic Conference in December 2002 in response to the **September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks** in the United States. It was incorporated into SOLAS as **Chapter XI-2** and entered into force on **1 July 2004**.

**Objectives (2 Marks):**
- Establish an international framework for **maritime security** involving ships, port facilities, and shipping companies.
- Enable early detection of security threats and implementation of preventive measures.
- Provide a methodology for security assessments, plans, and response procedures.

**Three Security Levels (3 Marks):**
- **Security Level 1 — Normal:** The level at which ships and port facilities operate **most of the time**. Basic security measures are maintained.
- **Security Level 2 — Heightened:** Applicable when there is a **heightened risk** of a security incident. Additional precautionary measures are implemented for a limited period.
- **Security Level 3 — Exceptional:** Applicable when a **security incident is probable or imminent**. Maximum security measures are maintained for a limited duration.

Key personnel include the **Company Security Officer (CSO)**, **Ship Security Officer (SSO)**, and **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)**.

#### Model Answer for Q (b) — Port State Control (7 Marks)

**Legal Basis (1 Mark):**
SOLAS Chapter I, **Regulation 19** — Control of Ships in Ports of Other Contracting States.

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Port State Control (PSC) is the inspection of foreign ships in national ports to verify that the ship and its crew comply with international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, MLC, Load Line, etc.).

**Authority (1 Mark):**
A **Port State Control Officer (PSCO)** is appointed by the government of the port State. They may inspect any ship calling at their port.

**Grounds for Inspection (2 Marks):**
- IMO Resolution A.1119(30) — Procedures for Port State Control
- Sub-standard ships can be identified via:
  - Absent or invalid certificates
  - Clear grounds that the ship or its crew does not conform to convention requirements
  - Reports or complaints from crew, passengers, or other parties

**Consequences (2 Marks):**
- Minor deficiencies — to be rectified within a specified time
- Detainable deficiencies — the ship is **detained** until rectified
- Detained ships appear in the **IMO database** and may face amplified inspections globally (Tokyo MoU, Paris MoU, etc.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Common mistakes: (i) confusing **Flag State** (where the ship is registered) with **Port State** (where the ship calls); (ii) stating that PSC officers can detain any ship — they can only detain **foreign** ships with non-conformities; (iii) omitting the **regional MoUs** (Memoranda of Understanding) like the Tokyo MoU, Paris MoU, Indian Ocean MoU, and Abuja MoU; (iv) mixing PSC under SOLAS with PSC under MARPOL Annex VI (air pollution).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Introduction to SOLAS**

- ✅ SOLAS = **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea** — most important IMO treaty on ship safety.
- ✅ First adopted in **1914** after the *Titanic* disaster; current version is **SOLAS 1974**, in force since **25 May 1980**.
- ✅ Administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, headquartered in **London, UK**.
- ✅ SOLAS 1974 is organized into **12 Chapters**, each with multiple Parts and Regulations.
- ✅ Key chapters for KTU: **I (General), II-1, II-2 (Fire), III (Life-saving), IV (GMDSS), V (Navigation), IX (ISM), XI-2 (ISPS), XII (Bulk carriers)**.
- ✅ **Tacit Acceptance Procedure** — amendments enter into force unless 1/3+ of parties object.
- ✅ **Flag State** issues certificates; **Port State** verifies via PSC inspections.
- ✅ **ISM Code** (Chapter IX) — Safety Management System, mandatory for ships ≥ 500 GT.
- ✅ **ISPS Code** (Chapter XI-2) — Maritime security, three security levels (1, 2, 3).
- ✅ **GMDSS** (Chapter IV) — 9 sea areas, automated distress communication.
- ✅ **DPA** (Designated Person Ashore) — the link between ship and shore SMS.
- ✅ **CSO, SSO, PFSO** — security officers under the ISPS Code.
- ✅ **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** — classification societies authorized by flag states.
- ✅ **Certificates** prescribed under Regulation I/12: Passenger Ship Safety, Cargo Ship Safety Construction, Equipment, Radio — all valid for **5 years** with annual surveys.
- ✅ Statutory surveys: **Initial, Annual, Periodical, Renewal** — keep the **chain of compliance unbroken**.
- ✅ Always link historical casualties to SOLAS amendments: *Titanic* → 1914 SOLAS; *Herald of Free Enterprise* → ISM Code; *9/11 attacks* → ISPS Code; *Bulk Jupiter* → Chapter XII.
- ✅ Remember the **14 major codes** annexed/referenced by SOLAS — at least 4–5 will appear in any KTU exam.
- ✅ **KTU favourite questions**: "Explain ISM/ISPS/GMDSS/PSC"; "Describe SOLAS chapter structure"; "Differentiate Flag State vs Port State"; "What is Tacit Acceptance?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subdivision and Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Subdivision and Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Subdivision and Stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Subdivision of a Ship** is the engineering and regulatory practice of dividing the hull of a vessel into a series of separate, bounded **watertight compartments** by means of transverse and longitudinal bulkheads that are constructed to withstand flooding pressure under SOLAS standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Damage Stability** is defined as the ability of a ship, after one or more compartments have been flooded due to hull damage or collision, to satisfy specific survival criteria — namely, the residual stability (positive righting lever), the residual freeboard above the **margin line**, and a limited equilibrium heel angle, as prescribed by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974, Chapter II-1**.

The combined discipline of **Subdivision and Stability** therefore ensures that, in the event of hull breach, water ingress is restricted to a controlled number of compartments, the ship does not capsize, and sufficient residual stability remains to allow orderly evacuation and safe passage to port.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Submarine-Like Apartment Building"**
> Imagine a 15-storey luxury cruise apartment building, but floating on the ocean. Each floor is sealed from the one above and below with **reinforced concrete firewalls**, and each apartment has a sealed door to its neighbours. If a giant axe chops a hole in apartment 7, the firewalls prevent water from spreading beyond that single apartment. The building may tilt slightly, but it does not collapse or sink. **Watertight bulkheads are the firewalls of a ship**, and **damage stability** is the science that decides how many apartments (compartments) can be flooded before the building (ship) loses balance and founders.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Plain Meaning | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bulkhead** | Vertical wall | Transverse / Longitudinal watertight partition |
| **Margin Line** | The "ceiling above floodwater" | A reference line drawn **76 mm (3 inches)** below the bulkhead deck at the side |
| **Permeability (μ)** | How "spongy" a room is | Ratio of floodable volume to gross volume of a compartment |
