> 1. **Cargo vs Passenger confusion:** Do NOT apply 125% liferaft rule to cargo ships — that is passenger-ship only. Many students lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. **Confusing liferaft and lifeboat launching times:** Liferafts inflate in 60 sec but are not "launched" in the conventional sense; lifeboat launch time is 10 min.
> 3. **Omitting the LSA Code reference** in your answer — examiners expect this verbatim (LSA Code Ch. 4, Ch. 4.4, etc.). **Lose 1 mark** if missing.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**"Explain the SOLAS requirements for personal lifesaving appliances and visual/radio signaling equipment. Include a list of minimum quantities for a 30-person cargo ship. (14 marks)"**

**Model Answer (Sub-Parts):**

#### (a) Personal Lifesaving Appliances (7 marks)

**1. Lifejackets (Reg. 7.2, 32):**
- **100% + spare** for crew.
- Buoyancy: **≥ 150 N** for adult sizes; **≥ 100 N** for child.
- Don time: **≤ 1 minute**.
- Equipped with **whistle, retro-reflective tape, buddy line, light (if bridge/control room)**.

**2. Immersion Suits (Reg. 7.3, 33):**
- **Mandatory for tankers and bulk carriers** in cold-water operations.
- Required for **all persons** in survival craft.
- Don time: **≤ 2 minutes**.
- Insulated to prevent hypothermia for **6 hours** in 5°C water.

**3. Ring Buoys (Life Buoys) (Reg. 7.1, 31):**
- Minimum 4 standard + 2 with **self-activating smoke** + 2 with **self-igniting light** (≥ 0.75 candela, 6-hour burn).
- **Liferaft capacity requirement met by distribution.**

#### (b) Visual & Radio Signaling — Inventory for 30-Person Cargo Ship (7 marks)

| Item | Quantity | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **EPIRB (406 MHz)** | 1 | Reg. 13, 48 hr battery |
| **SART (9 GHz)** | 1 | Reg. 14, 96 hr standby |
| **Rocket Parachute Flares** | 12 | Reg. 18, ≥ 300 m altitude |
| **Hand-Held Flares** | 12 | ≥ 15,000 candela, 60 s |
| **Buoyant Smoke Signals** | 4 | ≥ 15 min dense orange |
| **Line Thrower (4 charges + 4 lines)** | 1 | Range ≥ 230 m |
| **Portable GMDSS VHFs** | 3 | One per lifeboat typically |

> **Calculation of lifejackets (Reg. 7.2):**
> $N_{LJ} = 1.00 \times 30 = 30 \text{ lifejackets} + 4 \text{ spares (bridge/control room)} = 34 \text{ total}$
>
> **Calculation of ring buoys (Reg. 7.1):**
> $N_{RB} = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ (minimum)}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Students often forget to add the **bridge and ECR spares** to lifejackets — examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. Do NOT confuse **parachute rocket (12)** with **hand-held flare (12)** — both are required, separately, on a cargo ship. **Lose 2 marks** if conflated.
> 3. Always state the **frequency** (406 MHz for EPIRB, 9 GHz for SART) — examiners award partial credit only when units are given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SOLAS Chapter III** is the master chapter; **LSA Code (MSC.48(66))** gives engineering specs.
- [x] **Cargo ship** = 100% lifeboat + 100% liferaft; **Passenger** = 75% lifeboat + 125% liferaft.
- [x] **Liferaft distribution:** minimum **2 per side** of the ship.
- [x] **Lifeboat launch time:** ≤ 10 min; **Liferaft inflation:** ≤ 1 min; **Muster:** ≤ 5 min.
- [x] **Free-fall lifeboat** drop test: **1.3× the free-fall height** with full load.
- [x] **EPIRB** = 406 MHz, 48-hour battery, 5-year battery / 2-year HRU replacement.
- [x] **SART** = 9 GHz radar transponder, **12 dots on radar**, 96 hr standby / 8 hr active.
- [x] **Pyrotechnics:** 12 rocket parachute + 12 hand-held + 4 buoyant smoke + 1 line thrower (4 charges, 4 lines).
- [x] **Lifejacket** = 100% + 4 spares; **buoyancy ≥ 150 N**; don time ≤ 1 min.
- [x] **Immersion suit** = mandatory for tankers; don time ≤ 2 min; **6-hour** thermal protection.
- [x] **Ring buoys** = at least 4 + 2 smoke + 2 light = 8 minimum.
- [x] **Drills:** Abandon-ship monthly; Boat/Fire weekly (Reg. 19).
- [x] **Muster List** posted in conspicuous places (alleyways, mess rooms, bridge).
- [x] **GMDSS portable VHF** = 2-way survivor locating device in survival craft.
- [x] **Hydrostatic Release Unit (HRU)** automatically releases liferaft / EPIRB at < 4 m depth.
- [x] **Marking** of survival craft = **retro-reflective tape + SOLAS markings + stowage name**.
- [x] **Painting:** Lifeboats are often **orange with white markings** for high visibility.
- [x] **Anti-exposure suits** for use on workboats, offshore platforms.
- [x] **Recalls to remember:** SOLAS Reg. **6** (Capacity), Reg. **7** (Personal LSA), Reg. **13** (EPIRB), Reg. **14** (SART), Reg. **15** (Liferaft), Reg. **16** (Lifeboat), Reg. **18** (Pyrotechnics), Reg. **19** (Drills), Reg. **20** (Muster List).
- [x] **Number that examiners love to test:** **125%** (passenger liferaft), **100%** (cargo lifeboat/liferaft), **5 years** (EPIRB battery), **2 years** (HRU), **48 hours** (EPIRB operation), **96 hours** (SART standby), **1 minute** (liferaft inflation), **10 minutes** (lifeboat launch), **5 minutes** (muster), **20 knots** (FRB speed).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carriage of Grain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Carriage of Grain # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Carriage of Grain** under maritime law refers to the specialized legal and operational framework governing the transportation of bulk grain (wheat, corn, barley, rice, oats, rye, etc.) as cargo by sea. The carriage is primarily regulated by the **International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes Code (IMSBC Code)** under **SOLAS Chapter VI** and the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Chapter VII**, supplemented by national laws, charter parties, and international conventions such as the **Hague-Visby Rules**. Under KTU 2024 syllabus (PENST525), *grain* is defined per **SOLAS Regulation VI/2** as: > *"Wheat, maize (corn), oats, rye, barley, rice, pulses, seeds, and processed forms thereof (including pellets, flour, and meal) which behave in a manner similar to the original grain in terms of hazard."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** The IMSBC Code (effective 2011) **superseded** the earlier **International Grain Code (1991)**. Students must remember this transition — examiners frequently test this historical change. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine filling a **large rectangular water tank** on a moving ship with millions of small ball bearings (representing grain). If the ship tilts even slightly, the ball bearings **slip and slide** to the lower side, creating a sudden imbalance. This imbalance can capsize the ship. Unlike liquid cargo in proper tanks, grain has a **"free surface effect"** and can shift dangerously. **Analogy Breakdown:** - **Grain = millions of tiny balls** in a ship's hold - **Ship tilts = creating a "fluid-like" movement** of grain - **Grain slides to one side = catastrophic list (heel)** - **Solution = filling every void with grain** (feeds/feeder tables) so the grain **cannot shift** This is why grain carriage has **special regulations** that differ from ordinary bulk cargoes. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric used in Grain Carriage:** > - **Stowage Factor (SF):** **45–55 cubic feet per long ton (ft³/LT)** for bulk grain > - **Bulk Density:** **0.7–0.85 tonnes/m³** for wheat > - **Angle of Repose:** **30°–35°** for dry grain (critical stability parameter) ## 1.3 Why Grain is "Special" Cargo Grain is classified as a **"potentially dangerous" cargo** under SOLAS because of: | Hazard | Explanation | |--------|-------------| | **Cargo Shift** | Grain can flow like liquid when loose (fluidization) | | **Volumetric Expansion** | Grain swells when wet → exerts pressure on bulkheads | | **Spontaneous Heating** | Moist grain can combust (self-heating) | | **Toxic/Oxygen Depletion** | Some grains (e.g., linseed) emit gases | | **Dust Explosion** | Grain dust + ignition source = explosion risk | | **Reduced Stability** | Free-surface effect causes list → capsize risk | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write that grain is "dangerous" — the correct legal phrase is **"cargoes which may liquefy"** or **"cargoes with special hazards"** under SOLAS Chapter VI. ## 1.4 Regulatory Framework Hierarchy The legal framework for grain carriage follows a **pyramid structure**: 1. **International Level:** SOLAS Convention (Chapter VI & VII) → IMSBC Code 2. **Flag State Level:** National maritime authority (e.g., MCA UK, USCG, Indian DG Shipping) 3. **Classification Society Level:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK (approval of stability calculations) 4. **Contractual Level:** Charter Party clauses (GENCON, NYPE) + Bills of Lading 5. **Port State Level:** Inspection by port state control authorities --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes Code (IMSBC Code) The IMSBC Code is the **primary regulatory instrument** governing the carriage of grain. It was adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and became mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter VI** from **1 January 2011**. ### 2.1.1 Structure of the IMSBC Code The Code is divided into **four main sections**: - **Section 1:** General Provisions (Definitions, Classification, Documentation) - **Section 2:** General Loading, Carriage and Unloading Precautions - **Section 3:** Safety of Personnel and Ship - **Section 4:** Individual Schedules for specific cargoes (Appendices A, B, C) ### 2.1.2 Classification of Grain under IMSBC | Group | Description | Examples | Hazard Level | |-------|-------------|----------|--------------| | **Group A** | Cargoes which may liquefy | Iron ore fines, nickel ore, grain in some moisture conditions | **High** | | **Group B** | Cargoes with chemical hazards | Bulk chemicals, fertilizers | **High** | | **Group C** | Cargoes not classified above | Ordinary grain (wheat, corn, barley) | **Moderate** (still regulated) | > [!NOTE] > **Important:** Most dry grain falls under **Group C** (not inherently dangerous), but its handling is regulated under **SOLAS Chapter VII** for stability reasons. ## 2.2 SOLAS Chapter VII – Carriage of Dangerous Goods **Regulation 9 of SOLAS Chapter VII** specifically addresses grain: > *"The carriage of grain shall be in compliance with the requirements of the International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes Code (IMSBC Code) and the relevant sections of the Code of Safe Practice for Solid Bulk Cargoes (CSS Code). A Document of Authorization shall be issued for each ship loading grain."* ## 2.3 Document of Authorization (DoA) – The "Grain Loading Passport" A **Document of Authorization (DoA)** is a **mandatory certificate** issued by the **flag state administration** (or its authorized classification society) certifying that a ship is **fit to load grain** safely. ### 2.3.1 Contents of the Document of Authorization A DoA typically includes: 1. **Ship's particulars** (Name, IMO Number, GT, NT, DWT) 2. **Maximum permissible heeling moment** (in tonne-metres or foot-tonnes) 3. **Approved loading sequences and stowage plans** 4. **Approved trimming and feeding arrangements** 5. **Calculated stability data** (GM, free surface, angles of heel) 6. **Date of issue and validity period** 7. **Issuing authority details** ### 2.3.2 The Two Methods of Loading under DoA The DoA authorizes **two methods** of loading grain: | Method | Description | Use Case | |--------|-------------|----------| | **Method 1 (Stowed & Secured)** | Grain is loaded into all spaces completely filled & trimmed, with no free surface allowed | Standard bulk grain carriers | | **Method 2 (Partly Filled & Untrimmed with Feeds)** | Grain is loaded in some spaces untrimmed, but secured using **"feeds"** (trunks/feeder compartments) | Larger vessels with deep holds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The choice between Method 1 and Method 2 affects **heeling moment calculations** and is frequently examined. ## 2.4 Stability Requirements for Grain Ships The fundamental principle: **The angle of heel due to grain shift must not exceed 12°** at any stage of the voyage. ### 2.4.1 The 12-Degree Heel Rule $$\text{Maximum Permissible Heel Angle} = 12° \text{ (at any stage of voyage)}$$ This is the **absolute limit** under IMSBC Code Section 7. ### 2.4.2 Calculation of Heeling Moment The **heeling moment** caused by grain shift is calculated using the formula: $$M_h = W \times d \times \sin(\theta)$$ Where: - $M_h$ = Heeling moment (tonne-metres or ft-tons) - $W$ = Weight of grain that may shift (tonnes or long tons) - $d$ = Distance of shift of grain center of gravity (metres or feet) - $\theta$ = Angle of heel (degrees) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** Distance $d$ is **NOT the full breadth of the hold**. It is the **transverse distance** the grain surface can shift, typically calculated as $0.05 \times B$ (where B = beam of vessel) for trimmed grain, or $0.10 \times B$ for untrimmed grain. ### 2.4.3 Heeling Moment Tables (Reference Values) | Condition of Grain | Shift Distance (d) | Heel Angle (θ) | |-------------------|-------------------|----------------| | **Trimmed & Level** | $0.05 \times B$ | **12°** | | **Untrimmed (saucered)** | $0.10 \times B$ | **12°** | | **Loose untrimmed (Method 2)** | $0.20 \times B$ | **12°** | | **Trimming Lapse** | $0.10 \times B$ | **12°** | ## 2.5 Feeds, Feeder Tables & Trimming ### 2.5.1 The Concept of "Feeds" A **"feed"** is a compartment or trunk filled with grain to **prevent grain shift** in an adjacent hold. It acts as a counterweight — when the ship rolls, the grain in the feed shifts to **oppose** the grain shift in the main hold. ### 2.5.2 The Feeder Table Concept A **feeder table** is a triangular cross-section of grain (typically **45° angle of repose**) that connects the **bulk grain in a hold** to the **feed compartment**. As the ship rolls: $$\text{Volume of Feed Transferred} = \text{Volume of Grain Required to Level the Surface}$$ The feeder table ensures that the **net shift is ZERO** because any grain movement in the main hold is offset by an equal and opposite movement in the feed. ### 2.5.3 The 45° Angle of Repose $$\tan(\phi) = \frac{\text{Height of Cone}}{\text{Radius of Cone}}$$ For grain, the angle of repose $\phi = 30°–35°$ for natural untrimmed surface. For feeder table design, the **filling angle** used is **45°** (to provide safety margin). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $M_h = W \times d \times \sin(\theta)$ | Heeling moment calculation | t·m | | 2 | $d = 0.05 \times B$ (trimmed) | Shift distance for trimmed grain | m | | 3 | $d = 0.10 \times B$ (untrimmed) | Shift distance for untrimmed grain | m | | 4 | $\theta_{max} = 12°$ | Maximum permissible heel | degrees | | 5 | $SF = \frac{V}{W}$ | Stowage Factor = Volume/Weight | m³/t | | 6 | $V_{shift} = L \times B \times h_{shift}$ | Volume of grain that can shift | m³ | | 7 | $W_{shift} = \frac{V_{shift}}{SF}$ | Weight of grain that can shift | t | | 8 | $GM_{required} \geq \frac{M_h}{\Delta \times \sin(12°)}$ | Required metacentric height | m | | 9 | $FFS = \frac{i \times \rho}{T}$ | Free Surface Effect correction | m | | 10 | $\Delta_{max} = \frac{M_h}{BM \times \sin(12°)}$ | Maximum permissible displacement | t | Where: - $B$ = Beam of vessel (m) - $L$ = Length of hold (m) - $\Delta$ = Displacement (t) - $BM$ = Metacentric radius (m) - $GM$ = Metacentric height (m) - $FFS$ = Free Fluid Surface correction - $i$ = Transverse moment of inertia of free surface - $\rho$ = Density of liquid/fluid - $T$ = Tank/hold dimension > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Units must be **consistent**. Never mix metric (m, t) with imperial (ft, LT) in the same calculation. KTU questions typically specify the unit system — follow it strictly. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Maritime Operations The grain stability framework is critical in: - **Ship Design:** Bulk carrier designers must allocate hold volumes considering grain stowage factors - **Loading Software:** Modern vessels use **loading computers** (e.g., AMCS, BAPLIE) that auto-calculate DoA compliance - **Insurance & P&I Clubs:** Underwriters verify DoA validity before covering grain shipments - **Charter Party Disputes:** Grain stability failures lead to **average contributions** under marine insurance - **Port State Control:** PSCO inspectors verify DoA presence during routine inspections --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Permissible Heeling Moment The IMSBC Code specifies that the **angle of heel due to grain shift** at any stage of the voyage must **not exceed 12°**. The derivation of the corresponding heeling moment is shown below. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** At equilibrium, the **heeling moment** from grain shift equals the **righting moment** from the ship's stability. $$M_h = M_R$$ **Step 2:** The righting moment of a ship at small angles is given by: $$M_R = \Delta \times GM \times \sin(\theta)$$ **Step 3:** The heeling moment from grain shift (when grain moves a distance $d$ from centerline) is: $$M_h = W \times d \times \cos(\theta)$$ For small angles, $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$, so: $$M_h \approx W \times d$$ **Step 4:** Setting $M_h = M_R$ at the limiting angle $\theta = 12°$: $$W \times d = \Delta \times GM \times \sin(12°)$$ **Step 5:** Solving for the **Maximum Permissible Heeling Moment**: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{h,\max} &= \Delta \times GM \times \sin(12°) \\ &= \Delta \times GM \times 0.2079 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Solving for the **Maximum Permissible Shift** (when the heeling moment is known): $$ \begin{aligned} d_{max} &= \frac{M_{h,\max}}{W} \\ &= \frac{\Delta \times GM \times \sin(12°)}{W} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on the 12° limit:** This angle is not arbitrary. It represents the **angle at which the righting lever (GZ) begins to decrease rapidly** in a typical bulk carrier. Beyond 12°, the ship risks **capsize** if the heeling moment continues. ## 3.2 Work Problem — Calculating Maximum Grain Shift ### Problem Statement (KTU-style): A bulk carrier has the following particulars: - **Displacement ($\Delta$):** 25,000 tonnes - **Metacentric height (GM):** 1.5 m - **Beam (B):** 30 m - **Hold length (L):** 40 m - **Loaded grain weight in hold:** 8,000 tonnes - **Stowage factor (SF):** 1.25 m³/tonne The grain is to be loaded in trimmed condition. Find: 1. The **maximum permissible heeling moment** 2. The **maximum shift distance** 3. The **allowable angle of heel** due to shift ### Solution: **Step 1:** Calculate the Maximum Permissible Heeling Moment: $$M_{h,\max} = \Delta \times GM \times \sin(12°)$$ Substituting values: $$M_{h,\max} = 25{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 0.2079$$ $$ \begin{aligned} M_{h,\max} &= 25{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 0.2079 \\ &= 37{,}500 \times 0.2079 \\ &= 7{,}796.25 \text{ t·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the Shift Distance for Trimmed Grain (d = 0.05B): $$d = 0.05 \times B = 0.05 \times 30 = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the Heeling Moment from Grain Shift: $$M_h = W \times d = 8{,}000 \times 1.5 = 12{,}000 \text{ t·m}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with Maximum Permissible: $$M_h = 12{,}000 \text{ t·m} > M_{h,\max} = 7{,}796.25 \text{ t·m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **The proposed loading FAILS the IMSBC Code requirement!** The vessel must either: > (a) Reduce the grain in the hold to less than 5,197.5 tonnes, OR > (b) Use **Method 2 (Feeder Trunks)** to reduce the effective shift distance, OR > (c) Increase GM (e.g., by ballasting) before loading. **Step 5:** Calculate the **Maximum Allowable Grain Weight** to comply: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{max} &= \frac{M_{h,\max}}{d} \\ &= \frac{7{,}796.25}{1.5} \\ &= 5{,}197.5 \text{ tonnes} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Loading Methods Comparison | Parameter | Method 1 (Stowed & Secured) | Method 2 (Untrimmed with Feeds) | |-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------------| | **Grain Condition** | Fully trimmed & leveled | Partly filled, untrimmed | | **Shift Distance (d)** | $0.05 \times B$ | $0.10 \times B$ to $0.20 \times B$ | | **Heel Angle Limit** | **12°** | **12°** | | **Stability Margin** | Higher | Lower (compensated by feeds) | | **Loading Time** | Slower (trimming required) | Faster | | **Cost of Operation** | Higher (labor for trimming) | Lower | | **Risk Profile** | Lower risk | Higher risk if feeds fail | | **Document of Authorization** | Required | Required | ## 3.4 Liability Framework for Grain Carriage ### 3.4.1 Liability under International Conventions | Convention | Application to Grain | Key Articles | |-----------|---------------------|--------------| | **Hague-Visby Rules** | Bill of Lading carriage | Article III (1), III (2), III (6) | | **Hague Rules** | Historical (pre-Visby) | Articles I, II, III | | **Hamburg Rules** | Optional state adoption | Article 5 (Liability of Carrier) | | **Rotterdam Rules** | Modern multimodal | Chapter 5 (Liability of Carrier) | ### 3.4.2 Common Defenses in Grain Claims 1. **Inherent Vice (Article IV(2)(m) Hague-Visby):** Grain's natural tendency to shift/heat 2. **Faults in Navigation:** Master's decision in emergencies 3. **Fire (Article IV(2)(b)):** Limited carrier liability 4. **Force Majeure:** Weather, war, strikes 5. **Improper Loading by Shipper:** Charterer loaded improperly > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** Under the Hague-Visby Rules, the carrier is **not liable** for loss caused by **"inherent vice"** of grain. But the carrier **must** show the ship was **seaworthy** and properly **stabilized for grain carriage** (DoA compliance). --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Regulatory Framework Flow (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[IMO - International Maritime Organization] --> B[SOLAS Convention
