[KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Answer:** Under **SOLAS Regulation VI/2**, **'Grain'** is defined as: *"Wheat, maize (corn), oats, rye, barley, rice, pulses, seeds, and processed forms thereof (including pellets, flour, and meal) which behave in a manner similar to the original grain in terms of hazard."* **Key Elements:** 1. **Specific commodities listed** — wheat, maize, oats, rye, barley, rice, pulses, seeds 2. **Processed forms included** — pellets, flour, meal 3. **"Similar hazard" test** — processed forms must behave like the original grain > [!NOTE] > The IMSBC Code Section 1.2 expands this definition to include **other cereals** that exhibit similar bulk behavior. --- ### Question 2: What is a Document of Authorization (DoA) in grain carriage? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** A **Document of Authorization (DoA)** is a **mandatory certificate** issued by the **flag state administration** (or an authorized classification society) to a vessel prior to loading grain, certifying that: 1. The **ship is suitable** to carry grain 2. The **stability calculations** have been approved 3. The **loading sequences** comply with IMSBC Code requirements 4. The **maximum permissible heeling moments** for each hold are specified 5. **Trimming arrangements** are documented **Legal Basis:** **SOLAS Chapter VII, Regulation 9** (as amended). **Validity:** The DoA is valid for the **specific voyage** or for a **period not exceeding 5 years** (subject to conditions). > [!IMPORTANT] > A vessel **without a valid DoA** must **NOT load grain** — doing so is a **detainable deficiency** under Port State Control. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice ### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** > *"Discuss the International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes (IMSBC) Code in detail. Explain its structure, scope, and application to grain carriage. Describe the stability requirements under the Code and the method of calculating heeling moments."* (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — IMSBC Code: Structure, Scope, Application (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. Genesis of the IMSBC Code:** The IMSBC Code was adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **2008** and became **mandatory under SOLAS Chapter VI** on **1 January 2011**. It **superseded** the earlier **Code of Safe Practice for Solid Bulk Cargoes (BC Code)**, which was voluntary, and the **International Grain Code (1991)**. **2. Structure of the IMSBC Code:** The Code is organized into the following sections: - **Section 1:** General Provisions — Definitions, Classification of cargoes, Documentation - **Section 2:** General Loading, Carriage and Unloading Precautions - **Section 3:** Safety of Personnel and Ship (ventilation, fire-fighting, personal protection) - **Section 4:** Individual Cargo Schedules (Appendices A, B, C) - **Appendix A:** Schedules for cargoes that may liquefy (Group A) and chemical hazards (Group B) - **Appendix B:** Schedules for cargoes not liquefying (Group C) - **Appendix C:** Bulk cargoes not listed in A or B (refer to authorities) **3. Scope and Application to Grain:** The IMSBC Code applies to **all vessels** engaged in the carriage of **solid bulk cargoes** as defined in SOLAS Chapter VI. For **grain**, the Code provides: - **Definitions** of grain and related cargoes - **Stability requirements** to prevent capsize due to grain shift - **Document of Authorization** (DoA) requirements - **Loading and stowage** instructions - **Ventilation, moisture control, and trimming** guidelines **4. Classification of Grain under IMSBC:** Most dry grain is classified as **Group C** (cargoes not liable to liquefy or pose chemical hazard). However, certain grains under specific moisture conditions may fall into **Group A** (liable to liquefy), making proper moisture control essential. #### Part (b) — Stability Requirements and Heeling Moment Calculation (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. The 12-Degree Rule:** The fundamental stability requirement is that the **angle of heel** resulting from **adverse shift of grain** shall **not exceed 12°** at any stage of the voyage. This applies to: - **Departure condition** (fully loaded) - **Intermediate stages** (during voyage, partial discharge) - **Arrival condition** (after discharge) **2. Calculation of Heeling Moment:** The heeling moment caused by grain shift is calculated as: $$M_h = W \times d \times \sin(\theta)$$ Where: - $M_h$ = Heeling moment (tonne-metres) - $W$ = Weight of grain that may shift (tonnes) - $d$ = Distance of transverse shift (metres) - $\theta$ = Angle of heel (degrees) **3. Shift Distance Values (IMSBC Code Section 7.2):** | Condition | Shift Distance | |-----------|----------------| | Trimmed and level | $d = 0.05B$ | | Untrimmed (saucered) | $d = 0.10B$ | | Loose untrimmed | $d = 0.20B$ | Where $B$ = beam of vessel. **4. Maximum Permissible Heeling Moment:** $$M_{h,\max} = \Delta \times GM \times \sin(12°)$$ Where: - $\Delta$ = Displacement - $GM$ = Metacentric height **5. Methods of Compliance:** - **Method 1:** Trim and level all grain to eliminate shift - **Method 2:** Use **feeder tables/feeds** to counterbalance shift **6. Worked Example:** For a vessel with $\Delta = 20{,}000$ t, $GM = 1.2$ m, $B = 28$ m, $W_{hold} = 7{,}000$ t: $$M_{h,\max} = 20{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 0.2079 = 4{,}990 \text{ t·m}$$ $$d_{trimmed} = 0.05 \times 28 = 1.4 \text{ m}$$ $$M_h = 7{,}000 \times 1.4 = 9{,}800 \text{ t·m}$$ Since $M_h = 9{,}800 > M_{h,\max} = 4{,}990$, **loading is NON-COMPLIANT**. The vessel must: - Reduce cargo weight, OR - Use feeder trunks (Method 2), OR - Increase GM by ballasting --- ### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *"Explain the legal framework for the carriage of grain under international maritime law. Discuss the role of the Hague-Visby Rules, the concept of inherent vice, and the carrier's obligations regarding seaworthiness and proper stowage of grain cargo."* (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — Legal Framework and Hague-Visby Rules Application (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. The Multi-Layered Legal Framework:** The carriage of grain by sea is governed by a combination of: - **International Conventions** (SOLAS, Hague-Visby Rules) - **Codes** (IMSBC Code, CSS Code) - **National Laws** (Flag state and port state regulations) - **Contractual Terms** (Charter parties, Bills of Lading) **2. SOLAS Convention — Primary Safety Framework:** - **SOLAS Chapter VI** — Carriage of Solid Bulk Cargoes - **SOLAS Chapter VII** — Carriage of Dangerous Goods - **SOLAS Regulation VII/9** — Specific requirements for grain The **IMSBC Code** is mandatory under SOLAS Chapter VI from 1 January 2011. **3. Hague-Visby Rules — Liability Framework:** The **Hague-Visby Rules** (as incorporated into the **UK Carriage of Goods by Sea Act 1971**, **US Carriage of Goods by Sea Act 1936**, and **Indian Bills of Lading Act 1855**) govern the contractual liability of the carrier for loss/damage to cargo. **Key Articles Applicable to Grain Carriage:** | Article | Provision | Application to Grain | |---------|-----------|---------------------| | **Article III(1)** | Carrier's duty of seaworthiness | Vessel must be stable for grain carriage (DoA) | | **Article III(2)** | Duty to properly load, stow, carry, and discharge | Proper trimming, ventilation, feeds | | **Article III(6)** | Bill of Lading as prima facie evidence | Receipt of grain quantity | | **Article IV(2)(m)** | Inherent vice defense | Grain's natural tendency to shift/heat | | **Article IV(2)(a)** | Act, neglect, default of master | Defense for nautical errors | | **Article IV(2)(b)** | Fire | Limited liability for fire damage | **4. Hamburg Rules and Rotterdam Rules (Optional State Adoption):** - **Hamburg Rules (1978):** Broader carrier liability, longer limitation period (2 years) - **Rotterdam Rules (2009):** Multimodal coverage, modernized liability framework (not yet in force as of 2024) #### Part (b) — Inherent Vice, Seaworthiness, and Carrier's Obligations (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. The Doctrine of Inherent Vice (Article IV(2)(m)):** > *"Neither the carrier nor the ship shall be responsible for loss or damage arising or resulting from… inherent vice… of the goods carried."* **Application to Grain:** "Inherent vice" in grain cargo includes: - **Natural moisture migration** leading to self-heating - **Weevil and insect infestation** (pre-existing) - **Spontaneous combustion** in damp grain - **Fungal growth** in moisture-laden cargo - **Natural deterioration** over long voyages **Conditions for Successful Defense:** The carrier must prove: 1. The damage was due **solely** to inherent vice 2. The carrier was **not negligent** in any contributing manner 3. The vessel was **seaworthy** for grain carriage (DoA valid) 4. **Proper care** was taken in stowage, ventilation, and carriage > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** The carrier **cannot** rely on inherent vice if the damage was **caused or exacerbated** by carrier negligence (e.g., poor ventilation, inadequate trimming, unseaworthy vessel). **2. Seaworthiness Obligation (Article III(1)):** The carrier has an **absolute duty** (subject to due diligence) to provide a **seaworthy vessel** at the start of the voyage. For grain, seaworthiness includes: - **DoA validity** for the specific voyage - **Stability calculations** approved by classification society - **Holds clean, dry, and ready** for grain (free from residues, odours) - **Ventilation systems** functional - **Moisture control equipment** operational - **Hatch covers** weathertight and tested > [!IMPORTANT] > **Case Law — The "Muncaster Castle" (1961):** A barge carrying grain foundered due to **incorrect stowage** by the master. The House of Lords held the carrier **liable** for unseaworthiness because the vessel was not properly stabilized for grain. **3. Stowage Obligations (Article III(2)):** The carrier must: - **Load** grain using approved procedures - **Stow** grain to prevent shift - **Carry** grain with proper ventilation - **Discharge** grain without unnecessary damage For grain, proper stowage means: - **Trimming** in accordance with the DoA - **Use of feeds** (where applicable) - **Avoidance of contamination** with other cargoes - **Temperature and moisture monitoring** **4. The Owner's vs. Charterer's Liability:** When grain is loaded by a **time charterer** under a charter party: - The **charterer** may be liable for improper loading - The **owner** retains liability for seaworthiness - The **P&I Club** typically indemnifies the owner **5. Practical Example:** A shipment of wheat arrives at the discharge port with: - 15% of cargo **wet and mouldy** - Bags **caked and consolidated** - 5% **shortage** in weight **Carrier's Defense:** Inherent vice — natural moisture migration. **Counter-argument:** The vessel's ventilation system was malfunctioning; the carrier **failed to exercise due diligence** in ensuring proper carriage. The defense **fails**, and the carrier is **liable** for the damage. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Areas in Grain Carriage Questions:** 1. **Forgetting the 12° rule** — Always state the angle of heel limit before calculations. 2. **Mixing units** — Stay in either metric (m, t) or imperial (ft, LT). KTU questions specify — follow exactly. 3. **Misapplying shift distance** — Use $0.05B$ for trimmed, NOT $0.20B$ (which is for untrimmed/loose grain). 4. **Confusing DoA with Loading Certificate** — DoA is issued **before loading**; Loading Certificate is **after**. 5. **Inherent vice is not absolute** — Always mention that the carrier must show **no negligence**. 6. **IMSBC Code vs. Grain Code** — IMSBC Code (2011) **superseded** the 1991 Grain Code. 7. **Forgetting Feeder Tables** — When discussing Method 2, mention the **45° filling angle**. 8. **Missing case law** — "Muncaster Castle" and "Snia Amicizia" are common KTU references. 9. **Stability units** — GM is in metres (not centimetres); always convert. 10. **Discharge procedures** — Many students forget that grain must be **discharged carefully** to avoid final-stage list. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ☐ **Grain is a "potentially dangerous" cargo** under SOLAS due to its free-surface effect and shifting behavior. - ☐ **SOLAS Chapter VI/Regulation 9** requires grain to be carried per the **IMSBC Code**. - ☐ **IMSBC Code became mandatory on 1 January 2011**, replacing the 1991 International Grain Code. - ☐ **Document of Authorization (DoA)** is mandatory before loading grain — issued by flag state/classification society. - ☐ **Maximum permissible heel angle = 12°** at every stage of the voyage. - ☐ **Two loading methods:** Method 1 (Stowed & Secured) and Method 2 (Untrimmed with Feeds). - ☐ **Shift distance (d):** $0.05B$ (trimmed), $0.10B$ (untrimmed), $0.20B$ (loose). - ☐ **Heeling moment formula:** $M_h = W \times d \times \sin(\theta)$. - ☐ **Maximum permissible heeling moment:** $M_{h,\max} = \Delta \times GM \times \sin(12°) \approx 0.208 \times \Delta \times GM$. - ☐ **Feeder tables** use a **45° filling angle** for design purposes. - ☐ **Grain stowage factor:** typically **45–55 ft³/LT** or **1.25–1.4 m³/t**. - ☐ **Inherent vice (Article IV(2)(m))** is the primary carrier defense for grain damage. - ☐ **Inherent vice is NOT absolute** — carrier must show **no negligence**. - ☐ **Seaworthiness for grain** includes valid DoA, proper holds, functional ventilation. - ☐ **Case law references:** *Muncaster Castle* (1961), *Snia Amicizia* (1967), *Gosse Millard* (1927). - ☐ **Hague-Visby Rules** apply to bills of lading; **Article III** (carrier obligations) and **Article IV** (defenses) are the key provisions. - ☐ **Hague-Visby limitation:** **666.67 SDR per package** or **2 SDR per kg**, whichever is higher. - ☐ **P&I Clubs** typically indemnify owners in grain claims; subrogation may follow. - ☐ **Charterer's liability** arises when the charterer is responsible for loading/stability failures. - ☐ **Port State Control** can detain vessels without valid DoA — a **detainable deficiency**. - ☐ **Group C classification** applies to most dry grain; **Group A** (liquefying) requires moisture control. - ☐ **Ventilation requirements:** Minimum **6 air changes per hour** for grain holds. - ☐ **Moisture content limit:** Typically **15%** for safe grain carriage. - ☐ **Trimming standards:** Surface must be level to within **±5° of horizontal**. - ☐ **IMSBC Code Appendices:** A (liquefying/chemical), B (non-liquefying), C (unlisted). - ☐ **Solid bulk cargoes** with moisture > **TML (Transportable Moisture Limit)** are prohibited. - ☐ **The "8 percent rule"** — A common term in grain charter parties for stowage/loading. --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — Carriage of Grain** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education framework*

topics.title: Carriage of Dangerous Goods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Carriage of Dangerous Goods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

**Dangerous Goods (Hazardous Cargo)** in maritime commerce are defined under **SOLAS Chapter VII** and the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code** (published by the International Maritime Organization, IMO) as substances, materials, or articles which, when transported by sea, may pose an unreasonable risk to **health, safety, property, or the marine environment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (SOLAS Reg. VII/1):** *Dangerous goods mean goods classified in the IMDG Code as explosive, gases, flammable liquids, flammable solids, oxidizing substances, organic peroxides, toxic substances, infectious substances, radioactive materials, corrosives, or miscellaneous dangerous substances.*

The legal and operational regime governing the **Carriage of Dangerous Goods by Sea** is a composite of:
- **SOLAS Convention, 1974** (Chapter VII)
- **IMDG Code** (updated biennially)
- **MARPOL 73/78** (Annex III — Harmful Substances in Packaged Form)
- **Hague-Visby Rules** / **Rotterdam Rules** (Carrier liability)
- **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** (UNRTDG)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a flight. Some items (perfume, batteries, lighters) are flagged at check-in because they could leak, explode, or poison the cabin. The airline has a **strict checklist** of what is allowed, how it must be packed, labeled, and where it must be placed. The same logic applies to ships — but scaled up by **a factor of 10,000**. A single misdeclared container of lithium batteries on a containership can cause a fire that sinks the vessel, endangers the crew, and pollutes oceans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Dangerous goods regulation is essentially a **three-tier risk-management system**:
> 1. **Classify** — What is the hazard?
> 2. **Communicate** — Tell the carrier (labels, DGD).
> 3. **Contain & Separate** — How to pack and stow it safely.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Nomenclature

- **9 Hazard Classes** as per the IMDG Code (Class 1 through Class 9).
- **UN Number** — a 4-digit identifier (e.g., UN 1090 = Acetone).
- **Proper Shipping Name (PSN)** — the legally recognized name of the substance.
- **Packing Group (PG)** — I (high danger), II (medium), III (low).
- **Marine Pollutant (MP)** designation under MARPOL Annex III.
- **Flash Point** — minimum temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor that ignites; critical for **Class 3** goods.

> [!NOTE]
> The **EmS Guide** (Emergency Response Procedures for Ships Carrying Dangerous Goods) and the **MFAG** (Medical First Aid Guide) accompany the IMDG Code and are mandatory on board under SOLAS Reg. VII/14.2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The IMDG Classification System (9 Classes)

The IMDG Code categorizes dangerous goods into **nine hazard classes**, with sub-divisions for compatibility:

| Class | Hazard Type | Examples | Sub-Divisions |
|:-----:|:-----------|:---------|:--------------|
| **1** | Explosives | Fireworks, ammunition, TNT | 1.1 to 1.6 (mass explosion to extremely insensitive) |
| **2** | Gases | Propane, oxygen, chlorine | 2.1 Flammable / 2.2 Non-flammable / 2.3 Toxic |
| **3** | Flammable Liquids | Petrol, acetone, paints | Flash point < 60°C (closed cup) |
| **4** | Flammable Solids | Matches, sodium, phosphorus | 4.1 / 4.2 (spont. combust.) / 4.3 (water-reactive) |
| **5** | Oxidizers & Organic Peroxides | Hydrogen peroxide, ammonium nitrate | 5.1 Oxidizer / 5.2 Peroxide |
| **6** | Toxic & Infectious | Pesticides, medical waste | 6.1 Toxic / 6.2 Infectious |
| **7** | Radioactive | Uranium, medical isotopes | I (white), II (yellow), III (yellow) — based on transport index |
| **8** | Corrosives | Sulphuric acid, caustic soda | Acid / Alkali |
| **9** | Miscellaneous | Lithium batteries, asbestos, dry ice | Environmentally hazardous, elevated temperature |

## 2.2 The "Chain of Responsibility" (Duties of Stakeholders)

### 2.2.1 The Shipper (Consignor)
- **Duty to Classify** accurately per IMDG criteria.
- **Duty to Pack** in UN-specification packaging (marked with the UN packaging symbol).
- **Duty to Mark & Label** each package with class diamond, UN number, and PSN.
- **Duty to Provide Documentation** — namely the **Multimodal Dangerous Goods Form (MDGF)** or **Dangerous Goods Declaration (DGD)**.
- **Duty to Ensure** the goods are in proper condition for transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under **SOLAS Reg. VII/4**, the shipper is **primarily liable** for misdeclaration. Penalties include criminal prosecution, fines, and unlimited civil liability for losses.

### 2.2.2 The Carrier (Shipowner / Master)
- **Right to Refuse** dangerous goods not properly declared (Hague-Visby Art. IV(6)).
- **Duty to Exercise Due Diligence** to make the ship seaworthy for the specific cargo (Hague-Visby Art. III(1)).
- **Duty to Stow** per the IMDG Code's **Stowage and Segregation** requirements (segregation codes like "SG35", "SW1").
- **Duty to Carry** the IMDG Code, EmS, and MFAG on board.

### 2.2.3 The Master
- **Ultimate Authority** to jettison or destroy dangerous goods in emergencies (common law, Hague-Visby Art. IV(2)).
- **Duty to Notify** the coastal state (under SOLAS Reg. VII/7-1) when carrying certain high-consequence dangerous goods.

### 2.2.4 The Packer & Filler
- Must use **UN-certified packaging** and follow the **packing instructions** in the IMDG Code.

## 2.3 Legal Framework — Key Conventions & Articles

| Instrument | Article / Reg. | Core Provision | Liability Implication |
|:-----------|:---------------|:---------------|:---------------------|
| **SOLAS Ch. VII** | Reg. VII/2–VII/7 | Transport, classification, stowage, notification | Mandatory global |
| **IMDG Code** | All volumes | Detailed classification, packing, marking | Soft law adopted via SOLAS |
| **Hague-Visby Rules** | Art. IV Rule 6 | Carrier may unload/ destroy dangerous goods; no liability if hazard not disclosed | Limits carrier liability |
| **Hamburg Rules** | Art. 13 | Special rules for dangerous goods; carrier can take measures | Applies only to contracting states |
| **Rotterdam Rules** | Art. 32 | Detailed "Specific Obligations" for shipper re. dangerous goods | Modern global standard (not yet in force) |
| **MARPOL Annex III** | Reg. 1–8 | Packing, marking, documentation, stowage of harmful substances | Prevents marine pollution |

## 2.4 The "Five-Pillar Compliance Test"

For an examiner or compliance officer, every consignment must satisfy all five:

1. **Classification** — Correct UN number, class, PG, MP status.
2. **Packaging** — UN-spec packagings, performance-tested (drop, stack, leakproof).
3. **Marking & Labelling** — Class labels (diamond), UN number, PSN, marine pollutant mark, orientation arrows.
4. **Documentation** — DGD, MDGF, Container/ Vehicle Packing Certificate (CTU Code).
5. **Stowage & Segregation** — Per IMDG Code column 16b of the Dangerous Goods List (DGL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Utility:** This five-pillar framework is the operational backbone of port state control inspections (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian MoU). Detention of vessels under SOLAS Ch. VII is a daily occurrence in major hubs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Procedural Flow for Consigning Dangerous Goods

This section presents the **exhaustive procedural flow** a shipper must follow to legally consign dangerous goods by sea.

## 3.1 The "Five-Stage Procedural Algorithm"

### Stage 1 — Hazard Identification
1. Identify the substance's physical-chemical properties (flash point, LD50, reactivity, radioactivity).
2. Match the substance to the **UNRTDG** list of dangerous goods.
3. Assign the **UN Number**, **Proper Shipping Name (PSN)**, **Class**, and **Packing Group (PG)**.

### Stage 2 — Packaging Selection
1. Consult the IMDG Code **Dangerous Goods List (DGL)** entry for the substance.
2. Identify the allowed **Packing Instruction (PI)** — e.g., P001, P200, IBC02.
3. Select a **UN-certified packaging** bearing the UN packaging mark (e.g., `4G/Y15/S/25/USA/MM-1234`).
4. Perform or verify the **performance tests**: drop, stack, leakproofness, hydraulic pressure (for gases).

### Stage 3 — Marking & Labelling
1. Affix the **class label** (diamond, 100×100 mm minimum, color-coded per class).
2. Mark the **UN number** below the class label in characters at least **12 mm high**.
3. Print the **Proper Shipping Name** on the package.
4. Add the **Marine Pollutant mark** (fish-and-tree symbol) if applicable.
5. Add **orientation arrows** for liquids and for packages > 60 L.
6. For radioactive materials, also display the **Transport Index (TI)**.

### Stage 4 — Documentation Chain
1. Prepare the **Multimodal Dangerous Goods Form (MDGF)** — signed by the shipper.
2. Provide the **Container Packing Certificate / Vehicle Packing Certificate** (signed by the packer/loader).
3. Issue the **Bill of Lading (B/L)** with a description of the goods, flagging dangerous cargo (often "DANGEROUS GOODS — Class X, UN 1090").
4. Retain copies for **at least 2 years** (some flag states require 5 years).

### Stage 5 — Stowage & Segregation
1. Consult the IMDG Code **DGL columns 16a and 16b** for stowage codes (e.g., "SW1" = protected from sources of heat) and segregation codes (e.g., "SG17", "SG35", "SG69").
2. Apply the **segregation table** in IMDG Volume 1, Chapter 7.2.4.
3. Special categories: **"Away from"**, **"Separated from"**, **"Separated by a complete compartment or hold from"**.

## 3.2 Liability Allocation Matrix

| Event | Hague-Visby Liability | Hamburg Rules Liability | Rotterdam Rules Liability |
|:------|:----------------------|:------------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Shipper fails to declare DG** | Carrier not liable (Art. IV(6)) | Shipper fully liable | Shipper strictly liable (Art. 32) |
| **Carrier fails to stow correctly** | Carrier liable (Art. III(1) & III(2)) | Carrier liable | Carrier liable (Art. 13) |
| **Hidden danger unknown to shipper** | Carrier can land/destroy goods, no liability | Similar | Carrier may take "reasonable measures" |
| **Master jettisons DG in emergency** | Carrier protected if public necessity | Allowed | Allowed under Art. 15 |
| **Fire / Explosion due to latent defect** | Carrier not liable (Art. IV(2)(q)) | Burden on carrier to prove | Carrier must prove all defenses |

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Determining Stowage for a Mixed Cargo

**Scenario:** A vessel is to load:
- **20 drums of Acetone** (UN 1090, Class 3, PG II)
- **20 cylinders of Oxygen, compressed** (UN 1072, Class 2.2(5.1))
- **10 boxes of Lithium Batteries** (UN 3480, Class 9)

**Analytical Process:**

**Step 1 — Identify Segregation Requirements from IMDG DGL**

$$ \text{Acetone (Class 3)} \; \xleftrightarrow{\text{Segregate from}} \; \text{Oxygen (Class 2.2 subsidiary 5.1)} $$

According to IMDG Code segregation table, **Class 3 vs Class 2.2 (5.1)** requires **"Separated from"** (i.e., different compartments or holds; not adjacent).

**Step 2 — Evaluate Lithium Battery Stowage**

$$ \text{Lithium Batteries (Class 9)} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Stowage Code "SW1"} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Protected from heat, on deck or under deck, cool and dry} $$

**Step 3 — Apply "Separation Logic"**

$$ \text{Segregation Distance} = \begin{cases} \text{"Away from"} \rightarrow \text{Different packages, no shared compartment} \\ \text{"Separated from"} \rightarrow \text{Different holds or compartments with intervening bulkhead} \\ \text{"Separated by a complete compartment or hold from"} \rightarrow \text{Two bulkheads apart} \end{cases} $$

**Step 4 — Final Stowage Plan**
- **Acetone** — Under deck, segregated from oxygen by a full bulkhead.
- **Oxygen cylinders** — On deck (preferred for gases), away from heat sources, segregated from acetone.
- **Lithium batteries** — On deck, in a cool ventilated space, away from both.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical "segregation logic" question worth **7 marks** in a KTU Part B paper. The examiner looks for: (i) correct identification of segregation codes, (ii) correct application of segregation table, (iii) consideration of marine pollutant status, (iv) safety of crew access.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Lifecycle of a Dangerous Goods Consignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Shipper Identifies Hazard]) --> stepB{Classify per IMDG}
    stepB --> stepC[Assign UN Number, Class, PSN, PG]
    stepC --> stepD[Select UN Packaging per Packing Instruction]
    stepD --> stepE[Mark, Label, Placard Packages]
    stepE --> stepF[Prepare MDGF / DGD and CTU Certificate]
    stepF --> stepG[Hand Over to Carrier with B/L]
    stepG --> stepH{Carrier Verifies Documents}
    stepH -- Incomplete --> stepI[Refuse Cargo - Hague-Visby Art. IV 6]
    stepH -- Accept --> stepJ[Load Plan per Stowage and Segregation Codes]
    stepJ --> stepK[Onboard Carriage with IMDG Code and EmS]
    stepK --> stepL{Voyage Incident}
    stepL -- Spill / Fire --> stepM[Activate EmS and MFAG]
    stepL -- No Incident --> stepN[Safe Discharge at Port of Destination]
    stepM --> stepO[Incident Report to Flag State and Coastal State]
    stepN --> stepP([Cargo Delivered, Documentation Retained 2 Years])
    stepO --> stepP
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three-Tier Legal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    tier1[Tier 1 - International Conventions] --> intl1[SOLAS Chapter VII]
    tier1 --> intl2[IMDG Code IMO]
    tier1 --> intl3[MARPOL Annex III]
    tier1 --> intl4[Hague-Visby Rules]

    tier2[Tier 2 - National Legislation] --> nat1[Flag State Law e.g. UK MSA 1995]
    tier2 --> nat2[Coastal State Law e.g. India MSA 2025]
    tier2 --> nat3[Port State Control MOUs Paris MoU Tokyo MoU Indian MoU]

    tier3[Tier 3 - Contractual Layer] --> cont1[Bill of Lading incorporating Hague-Visby]
    cont3 --> cont2[Charter Party including Inter-Club Agreement]
    cont3 --> cont3[Dangerous Goods Declaration MDGF]
    cont3 --> cont4[CTU Code Packing Certificate]

    tier1 --> tier2
    tier2 --> tier3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Segregation Logic Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Cargo A - Class X] --> nodeQ{Segregation Code?}
    nodeQ -- Away From --> nodeR[Different packages, no contact]
    nodeQ -- Separated From --> nodeS[Different holds bulkhead apart]
    nodeQ -- Separated By Complete Compartment --> nodeT[Minimum two bulkheads apart]
    nodeQ -- No Segregation Code --> nodeU[No restriction, can be stowed together]

    nodeB[Cargo B - Class Y] --> nodeQ
    nodeC[Cargo C - Class Z] --> nodeQ

    nodeR --> nodeV([Approved Stowage Plan])
    nodeS --> nodeV
    nodeT --> nodeV
    nodeU --> nodeV
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams map the **operational, legal, and procedural architecture** that examiners expect students to reproduce in part (a) of a 14-mark question on dangerous goods.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Define "Dangerous Goods" under SOLAS Chapter VII. Name the international code that governs the transport of such goods by sea.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Under **SOLAS Regulation VII/1**, dangerous goods are substances, materials, or articles which, when transported by sea, may pose an unreasonable risk to health, safety, property, or the marine environment. These include explosives, gases, flammable liquids, oxidizers, toxic substances, radioactive materials, and corrosives. The transport of dangerous goods by sea is governed by the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code**, published by the International Maritime Organization (IMO), which is made mandatory under SOLAS Chapter VII. *(3 marks — Definition 1, Examples 1, Code name 1)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is a "Marine Pollutant" under MARPOL Annex III? How is it marked on a package?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A **Marine Pollutant (MP)** is a substance classified under MARPOL Annex III as harmful to the marine environment when carried in packaged form. Such substances are identified in the IMDG Code Dangerous Goods List with the letter "P" in the marine pollutant column. Packages containing marine pollutants must display the **marine pollutant mark** — a black fish-and-tree symbol inside a black-bordered square, placed adjacent to the class label. *(3 marks — Definition 1, Identification 1, Mark description 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: "The carriage of dangerous goods by sea involves a shared responsibility between the shipper, carrier, and master." Discuss the duties and liabilities of each stakeholder with reference to SOLAS, the IMDG Code, and the Hague-Visby Rules.**

#### Part (a) — Duties of the Shipper and the Carrier (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**A. Duties of the Shipper (4 marks):**
1. **Classification** — The shipper must correctly classify the goods per the IMDG Code, identifying the UN number, class, packing group, and marine pollutant status. Misclassification is a criminal offence under most national laws.
2. **Packaging** — Must use UN-certified packagings that have passed the IMDG performance tests (drop, stack, leakproofness). The packaging must bear the UN packaging mark.
3. **Marking & Labelling** — Packages must display the class label (hazard diamond), UN number, proper shipping name, and the marine pollutant mark where applicable.
4. **Documentation** — The shipper must provide a signed **Multimodal Dangerous Goods Form (MDGF)** and a **Container/ Vehicle Packing Certificate** to the carrier before loading.
5. **Duty to Inform** — Under **Hague-Visby Rules Art. IV Rule 6**, the shipper must inform the carrier of the dangerous nature of the goods; failure absolves the carrier of liability.

**B. Duties of the Carrier (3 marks):**
1. **Seaworthiness** — Under **Hague-Visby Art. III(1)**, the carrier must exercise due diligence to make the ship seaworthy, including fitness to carry the specific dangerous cargo.
2. **Proper Stowage** — The carrier must stow the goods in accordance with IMDG Code segregation and stowage codes.
3. **Right to Refuse** — Under **Hague-Visby Art. IV Rule 6**, the carrier may refuse undeclared or improperly declared dangerous goods and may land, destroy, or render them innocuous without liability.

#### Part (b) — Duties of the Master, Liability, and Emergency Powers (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**A. Duties of the Master (3 marks):**
1. **Carriage of IMDG Code, EmS, and MFAG** on board per SOLAS Reg. VII/14.2.
2. **Notification to coastal states** under SOLAS Reg. VII/7-1 for vessels carrying certain high-consequence dangerous goods.
3. **Emergency Powers** — The master has the authority to **jettison, destroy, or neutralize** dangerous goods in cases of imminent danger to the vessel, crew, or environment (Hague-Visby Art. IV(2); common law doctrine of necessity).

**B. Liability Allocation (3 marks):**
1. **Shipper's strict liability** for misdeclaration (SOLAS Reg. VII/4; Hague-Visby Art. IV Rule 6).
2. **Carrier's limited liability** under Hague-Visby (typically 666.67 SDR/package or 2 SDR/kg).
3. **Hamburg Rules and Rotterdam Rules** extend shipper liability to negligence and impose broader carrier obligations regarding dangerous goods.

**C. Conclusion (1 mark):**
The maritime regime for dangerous goods is a layered system: **SOLAS/IMDG prescribe what must be done; Hague-Visby/Rotterdam Rules prescribe who pays when things go wrong.** The shared-responsibility model ensures that the risk is borne by the party best placed to control it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Past Paper]**
**Q: Explain the IMDG classification system. Discuss the segregation principles and stowage requirements for dangerous goods on board ships with suitable examples.**

#### Part (a) — The IMDG Classification System (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The IMDG Code classifies dangerous goods into **9 classes** based on the type of hazard:

1. **Class 1 — Explosives** (1.1 mass-explosion to 1.6 extremely insensitive): e.g., TNT, fireworks.
2. **Class 2 — Gases**: 2.1 flammable (propane), 2.2 non-flammable (oxygen), 2.3 toxic (chlorine).
3. **Class 3 — Flammable Liquids** (flash point < 60°C): e.g., petrol, acetone.
4. **Class 4 — Flammable Solids**: 4.1 (matches), 4.2 (spont. combustible, e.g., phosphorus), 4.3 (water-reactive, e.g., sodium).
5. **Class 5 — Oxidizers & Peroxides**: 5.1 (hydrogen peroxide), 5.2 (organic peroxides).
6. **Class 6 — Toxic & Infectious**: 6.1 pesticides, 6.2 medical waste.
7. **Class 7 — Radioactive Materials**: categorized I, II, III by transport index.
8. **Class 8 — Corrosives**: acids (sulphuric) and alkalis (caustic soda).
9. **Class 9 — Miscellaneous**: lithium batteries, asbestos, environmentally hazardous substances.

Each entry is identified by a **UN Number** (4 digits) and **Proper Shipping Name (PSN)**.

#### Part (b) — Segregation & Stowage Principles (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**A. Segregation Principles (4 marks):**

The IMDG Code specifies **three levels of segregation** in Volume 1, Chapter 7.2.4:
- **"Away from"** — Different packages; cannot be in the same outer packaging.
- **"Separated from"** — In different holds/compartments; not adjacent.
- **"Separated by a complete compartment or hold from"** — Minimum two bulkheads apart.

**Example:** Class 3 (Acetone) and Class 2.2 with subsidiary 5.1 (Oxygen) must be "separated from" each other. Class 5.1 (oxidizers) must be "separated from" Class 8 (corrosives) and Class 4.1 (flammable solids).

**B. Stowage Requirements (2 marks):**

Stowage codes (e.g., **SW1** = protected from heat; **SW5** = protected from sunlight) appear in the IMDG DGL Column 16a. Special requirements apply to:
- **On-deck stowage** for gases and certain toxic substances.
- **Under-deck stowage** for flammable liquids in vented spaces.
- **Away from heat sources** for self-heating substances.

**C. Example (1 mark):**
- **Lithium batteries (UN 3480, Class 9):** Stowage code "SW1" — on deck or under deck in a cool, dry, ventilated space, away from heat and ignition sources.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "Dangerous Goods" definition** — Students often quote the IMDG Code definition instead of SOLAS Reg. VII/1. The **2 marks allocated for definition** require the SOLAS reference.
> 2. **Forgetting the Carrier's Right to Refuse** — Hague-Visby Art. IV Rule 6 is the most-tested provision in dangerous goods questions. Omitting it loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "Subsidiary Risk" with "Primary Risk"** — Substances like Oxygen (2.2) carrying a 5.1 subsidiary risk must be segregated per both rules. Students often cite only the primary class.
> 4. **Missing the Marine Pollutant angle** — Under MARPOL Annex III, additional marking and documentation are required. Worth 2 marks in any cargo-law question.
> 5. **Ignoring the Master's emergency powers** — The master's right to jettison under common law and Hague-Visby Art. IV(2) is a **favourite 3-mark sub-question** in KTU papers.
> 6. **Confusing Packing Groups with Hazard Classes** — PG I = high danger; PG III = low danger. This distinction often appears in Part A and is heavily tested.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Dangerous Goods** = defined in **SOLAS Reg. VII/1**; regulated by the **IMDG Code**.
- ✅ **9 Classes** of dangerous goods (Class 1 Explosives → Class 9 Miscellaneous).
- ✅ **Five-Pillar Compliance**: Classify → Pack → Mark/Label → Document → Stow/Separate.
- ✅ **UN Number** is the unique 4-digit identifier; **PSN** is the legally recognized name; **PG** indicates degree of danger (I, II, III).
- ✅ **Shipper is primarily liable** for misdeclaration under SOLAS Reg. VII/4 and Hague-Visby Art. IV Rule 6.
- ✅ **Carrier's Right to Refuse** dangerous goods not properly declared.
- ✅ **Master's Emergency Powers** to jettison or destroy dangerous goods in peril.
- ✅ **Segregation levels**: "Away from", "Separated from", "Separated by a complete compartment from".
- ✅ **Stowage codes** (Column 16a) and **Segregation codes** (Column 16b) of the IMDG DGL are mandatory operational references.
- ✅ **Marine Pollutants** under MARPOL Annex III require additional **fish-and-tree mark** and special documentation.
- ✅ **EmS** (Emergency Response) and **MFAG** (Medical First Aid) must be carried on board.
- ✅ **Hague-Visby** — carrier's liability limit; **Hamburg Rules** Art. 13; **Rotterdam Rules** Art. 32 (shipper's specific obligations).
- ✅ **Liability Caps**: 666.67 SDR per package or 2 SDR per kg (Hague-Visby) — but undeclared DG often voids the cap.
- ✅ **Notification to coastal state** under SOLAS Reg. VII/7-1 for high-consequence dangerous goods.
- ✅ **CTU Code** governs container packing; combined with IMDG, it is the global standard for safe packing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic for Quick Recall — "S-S-D-C-M":**
> **S**hipper classifies, **S**egregates, **S**tows; **D**ocuments via DGD;
> **C**arrier checks, **C**arries, **C**overs liability;
> **M**aster **M**onitors, **M**anifests, **M**anages emergencies.
> *(Use this 5-second recall in the exam hall to structure your answer.)*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Line Convention, Important Definitions and Markings, Survey and Certification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Line Convention, Important Definitions and Markings, Survey and Certification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966** is an International Maritime Organization (IMO) treaty adopted on **5 April 1966** and entered into force on **21 July 1968**. It establishes the **minimum permissible freeboard** to which ships may be loaded, thereby ensuring adequate **reserve buoyancy** and stability, preventing overloading, and safeguarding life, property, and the marine environment.

The Convention was developed in the aftermath of the tragic sinking of the **SS Mermaid (1860)** and the **SS Daphne (1883)**, where investigations revealed that overloading and improperly marked vessels were the principal causes of casualties. The earlier British **Merchant Shipping Act of 1890** and the resultant **Load Line Regulations** by **Samuel Plimsoll MP** (the "Sailors' Friend") became the foundation of the modern Convention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plimsoll's Contribution:** In **1875–1876**, Samuel Plimsoll campaigned in the British Parliament against the practice of shipowners overloading unseaworthy "coffin ships" to claim insurance money. His efforts led to the **Merchant Shipping Act of 1876**, making the **Plimsoll Mark** mandatory on British vessels.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **drinking glass filled with water up to the brim**. If you place even a single ice cube inside, the water overflows. Now imagine marking a line **slightly below the rim** — this is your **"safety line"**. As long as the water (the ship's cargo/load) does not cross this safety line, the glass (the ship) remains stable and won't "spill" (capsize or sink).

In maritime engineering:
- The **glass** = Ship's Hull
- The **Water (cargo)** = Deadweight, Cargo, Bunkers, Fresh Water
- The **Safety Line** = The **Load Line** (Plimsoll Mark)
- The **Air gap above the line** = **Freeboard** (reserve buoyancy + safety margin)

The deeper the ship sits in the water, the less reserve buoyancy remains. The Load Line Convention dictates the **maximum permissible draft** under varying sea conditions, ensuring that the ship always has sufficient buoyancy to survive waves, rough seas, and partial flooding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Multiple Lines?** A ship sailing in the **tranquil tropical waters of Mumbai in summer** can be loaded **deeper** than the same ship sailing in the **stormy North Atlantic in winter**. Hence, the Load Line Convention prescribes **multiple horizontal lines** corresponding to different **zones, seasons, and water types**.

---

## 1.3 Why the Load Line Convention is Critical for KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Maritime Safety** | Prevents loss of life and property by limiting overloading. |
| **Legal Compliance** | Non-compliance leads to detention, fines, and insurance invalidation. |
| **Insurance Validity** | P&I Clubs and Hull insurers require valid Load Line Certificates. |
| **Port State Control** | Deficiencies in load line markings lead to PSC detentions. |
| **Stability Reserve** | Guarantees minimum reserve buoyancy across operating conditions. |
| **Inter-jurisdictional Uniformity** | Provides global technical standard (except USA waters where USCG rules apply). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Definitions (As Per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> The following definitions are **verbatim from the ICLL 1966, as amended by the 1988 Protocol**. Examiners expect precise language.

### 2.1.1 Load Line
The **maximum permissible draft** to which a ship may be submerged in **salt water of specific gravity 1.025** at the **deepest operating condition** assigned by the Convention for a particular zone, season, and water type.

### 2.1.2 Freeboard
The **vertical distance measured from the waterline to the upper edge of the deck line** (i.e., the top of the freeboard deck plating at the side). Mathematically:

$$
\text{Freeboard} = \text{Distance from waterline to top of freeboard deck (at side)}
$$

### 2.1.3 Plimsoll Mark (Load Line Mark)
The **circular disc of diameter 300 mm**, bisected by a **horizontal line 450 mm long and 25 mm thick**, with the upper edge passing through the center. It is placed **amidships on each side of the ship** and is the **universal symbol** of the load line.

### 2.1.4 Freeboard Deck
The **uppermost complete deck exposed to weather and sea**, which has permanent means of closing all openings.

### 2.1.5 Superstructure Deck
Any deck above the freeboard deck extending at least **0.07L (where L = rule length)** longitudinally.

### 2.1.6 Reserve Buoyancy
The **displacement above the waterline** — i.e., the volume of the hull above the waterline that ensures the ship remains afloat even if some compartments are flooded.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Key Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **standard mathematical relationships** defined in Annex I of the ICLL 1966. Memorize these for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
F_{\min} &= D_{\text{summer}} - d_{\text{summer}} \\
F_{\text{seasonal}} &= F_{\text{min}} + \Delta F_{\text{season}} \\
d_{\text{fresh}} &= d_{\text{salt}} - \left(\frac{\text{Freeboard} \times \rho_{\text{sea}}}{40}\right) \\
\text{Reserve Buoyancy} &= \frac{V_{\text{above WL}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
}
$$

### 2.2.1 Standard Specific Gravity Reference

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Sea Water Density | **1.025 t/m³** | Tropical vs Polar adjustment |
| Standard Fresh Water Density | **1.000 t/m³** | For river/lake service |
| **Freeboard Correction** | **Freeboard ÷ 40** (in cm per 0.001 density) | Standard formula |
| Standard Plimsoll Diameter | **300 mm** | Centre of disc at L/2 |
| Standard Horizontal Line Length | **450 mm × 25 mm** | Bisects disc |

### 2.2.2 Zone and Seasonal Markings (Critical for KTU)

| Line Notation | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **TF** | Tropical Fresh Water | Tropical zone, rivers/lakes |
| **F** | Fresh Water | Fresh water outside tropical |
| **T** | Tropical (Salt Water) | Tropical zone, salt water |
| **S** | Summer (Salt Water) | Default summer condition |
| **W** | Winter | North Atlantic winter (Nov–Mar) |
| **WNA** | Winter North Atlantic | Severe winter conditions |
| **LS** | Lumut Seasonal (Special — Malaysia only) | Discontinued in 2014 |
| **ST** | Summer Tropical — outside tropical zone | Discontinued in 2014 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** The **WNA line is ALWAYS higher** (less loading allowed) than the Winter (W) line because the North Atlantic experiences the worst sea conditions. Students often confuse this.

---

## 2.3 Computation of Minimum Freeboard — Type "B" Ship (Single Deck Bulk Carrier Style)

The tabular freeboard $F$ (in mm) is computed using:

$$
\boxed{
F = K \cdot \left( A_{\text{reg}} - L \right) \cdot \left( L \right)^{-0.5} \cdot C
}
$$

Where:
- $F$ = Tabular freeboard (mm)
- $K$ = Displacement coefficient constant
- $A_{\text{reg}}$ = Regulatory parameter
- $L$ = Rule Length (m)
- $C$ = Block coefficient correction

> [!NOTE]
> For the **K value** reference and exact tabulated values, KTU examiners accept both the **Type A (tanker)** and **Type B (general cargo)** assignments. The two principal block coefficient families and their tabular values are derived from IMO Resolution **A.972(24)**.

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| **Ship Design Stage** | Determines hull form, depth, and bulkhead positions. |
| **Operational Planning** | Stowage plans and stevedore loading limits. |
| **Draft Survey** | Verifies that loaded draft complies with the mark. |
| **Stability Booklet** | Cross-references with intact stability calculations. |
| **Damage Stability** | Reserve buoyancy guarantees survivability. |
| **Weather Routing** | Voyage planning considers zone/season restrictions. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Freeboard Correction for Fresh Water

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A ship has a summer salt water draft of **9.50 m** and a corresponding freeboard of **1.80 m**. Compute the maximum permissible draft when the vessel is to load in **fresh water** (specific gravity 1.000) during summer conditions.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

Per ICLL Annex II, Regulation 32, the fresh water allowance is:

$$
\text{FA} = \frac{\text{Freeboard} \times (s - 1)}{40 \times s} \times 1000
$$

Where:
- $s$ = Specific gravity of sea water at the load line = 1.025
- Freeboard = 1.80 m
- Result FA is in **millimetres**

**Step 2 — Substitute the values into the equation.**

$$
\text{FA} = \frac{1.80 \times (1.025 - 1.000)}{40 \times 1.025} \times 1000
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator and denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 1.80 \times 0.025 = 0.045 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 40 \times 1.025 = 41.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio and convert to mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FA in m} &= \frac{0.045}{41.000} = 0.00109756 \text{ m} \\
\text{FA in mm} &= 0.00109756 \times 1000 = 1.098 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the correction to the salt water draft.**

The maximum permissible draft in fresh water is:

$$
d_{\text{fresh}} = d_{\text{salt}} - \text{FA (in m)}
$$

$$
d_{\text{fresh}} = 9.50 - 0.001098 = 9.4989 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Practical Loading Draft.**

$$
\boxed{d_{\text{fresh}} \approx 9.499 \text{ m (1.1 mm LESS than salt water draft)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A ship that can load to **9.500 m** in salt water can only load to **9.499 m** in fresh water. This is because **fresh water provides less buoyancy** (lower density), so the ship must ride slightly higher.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Zone and Seasonal Allowance for Tropical Loading

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** The same ship has a summer freeboard of 1.80 m. Compute the **tropical freeboard** using ICLL Regulation 40 (Tropical Load Line).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the ICLL Allowance for Tropical Zone.**

Per Regulation 40, the **tropical freeboard is obtained by deducting 1/48 of the summer salt water draft** from the summer freeboard. The tropical freeboard $F_T$ is:

$$
F_T = F_S - \left( \frac{d_S}{48} \right)
$$

Where:
- $F_S$ = Summer freeboard = 1.80 m
- $d_S$ = Summer draft = 9.50 m

**Step 2 — Compute the tropical allowance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tropical Allowance} &= \frac{d_S}{48} = \frac{9.50}{48} \\
&= 0.19792 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 198 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the tropical freeboard.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_T &= 1.80 - 0.19792 \\
&= 1.60208 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 1.602 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the corresponding tropical draft.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_T &= D - F_T \\
\text{(where } D = \text{moulded depth, but here we use the relationship)} \\
d_T &= 9.50 + (1.80 - 1.602) \\
&= 9.50 + 0.198 \\
&= 9.698 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Tropical Loading Draft.**

$$
\boxed{d_T \approx 9.698 \text{ m (198 mm MORE than summer draft)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The tropical load line is **deeper** than summer, allowing heavier loading in calm tropical waters.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Line Markings on a Typical Vessel

| Line Type | Symbol | Vertical Position Relative to S | Permitted Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Summer (Salt) | **S** | Baseline | Default (zones outside tropical/winter) |
| Winter | **W** | **Above S** (less draft) | North Sea, Baltic, N. Atlantic (Nov–Mar) |
| Winter North Atlantic | **WNA** | **Above W** (least draft) | Specific NA zones in winter |
| Tropical | **T** | **Below S** (more draft) | Tropics, year-round |
| Fresh Water | **F** | **Below S** (more draft) | Fresh water (rivers, lakes) |
| Tropical Fresh | **TF** | **Below T and F** (max draft) | Tropical rivers (Amazon, Mekong) |

### 3.3.1 Derivation Logic for Vertical Spacing

For each pair of adjacent lines, the **vertical spacing** is a fixed proportion of the summer draft:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spacing S–W} &= \frac{d_S}{48} \\
\text{Spacing S–T} &= \frac{d_S}{48} \\
\text{Spacing W–WNA} &= 50 \text{ mm (constant)} \\
\text{Spacing S–F} &= \Delta_{\text{fresh}} \approx \frac{d_S \times 0.025}{40}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The W–WNA spacing is **fixed at 50 mm** and is **independent of draft**. All other spacings are draft-dependent. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 3.4 Survey and Certification Framework — ICLL Annex I

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ICLL prescribes **three mandatory surveys** under Article 14. Each survey has a specific scope, periodicity, and certificate.

### 3.4.1 The Three Mandatory Surveys

| Survey Type | Frequency | Scope | Certificate Issued |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Survey** | Once, before entry into service | Comprehensive — verifies full compliance | **International Load Line Certificate** |
| **Annual Survey** | Within 3 months before/after anniversary date | Visual inspection, general condition, markings | Endorsement on existing certificate |
| **Renewal Survey** | Every 5 years | Comprehensive — same as initial | New Certificate valid 5 years |

### 3.4.2 Survey Items Checked

1. **Plimsoll Mark visibility, accuracy, and position** (must be amidships, paint condition).
2. **Deck line** — straight, level, properly painted.
3. **Watertight integrity** of hull, hatch covers, and closing appliances.
4. **Freeboard marks** — letter notations and grid lines.
5. **Superstructure openings** — doors, portlights, ventilators fitted with weathertight covers.
6. **Guard rails, freeing ports** — minimum heights and openings.
7. **Stability information** — Loading and stability booklet.
8. **Draft marks** — at bow, stern, and amidships, properly marked.

### 3.4.3 International Load Line Certificate (ILLC) — Validity

- Initial validity: **5 years** from completion of survey.
- May be **extended for 5 months** if renewal cannot be completed at the proper time.
- **Becomes void** if material alterations are made without notification.
- **May be withdrawn** by the Administration if the ship is found unsafe.

### 3.4.4 Exemption Certificate

Ships of < 24 m length may be granted an **Exemption Certificate** if their voyages are in sheltered waters only, subject to flag Administration approval.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Plimsoll Mark Anatomy and Adjacent Line Markings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Ship Hull Side View Amidships] --> node2[Plimsoll Disc Center]
    node2 --> node3[Disc Diameter 300 mm]
    node2 --> node4[Horizontal Line 450 mm]
    node2 --> node5[Deck Line 300 mm Long 25 mm Thick]
    node1 --> node6[Load Line Mark S Baseline]
    node1 --> node7[Load Line Mark W Above S]
    node1 --> node8[Load Line Mark WNA Above W]
    node1 --> node9[Load Line Mark T Below S]
    node1 --> node10[Load Line Mark F Below S]
    node1 --> node11[Load Line Mark TF Lowest of All]
    node7 --> node12[Spacing S to W equals d S divided by 48]
    node8 --> node13[WNA to W fixed at 50 mm]
    node9 --> node14[Tropical Allowance equals d S divided by 48]
    node10 --> node15[Fresh Water Allowance from density correction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Node `node1` represents the side profile of the hull at amidships. The disc sits at the **summer salt water line (S)**. Above it are `node7` (Winter) and `node8` (WNA) — both *less* draft, *more* freeboard. Below it are `node9` (Tropical) and `node10/11` (Fresh variants) — *more* draft.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — ICLL Survey & Certification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Ship Construction Complete] --> stepB[Initial Survey by Class or RO]
    stepB --> stepC{All Items Satisfactory?}
    stepC -- Yes --> stepD[Issue International Load Line Certificate ILLC]
    stepC -- No --> stepE[Rectify Deficiencies and Resurvey]
    stepE --> stepC
    stepD --> stepF[Annual Anniversary Survey Every Year]
    stepF --> stepG{Defects Found?}
    stepG -- No --> stepH[Endorsement on ILLC]
    stepG -- Yes --> stepI[Condition of Class or Rectification]
    stepI --> stepJ[Verification Survey]
    stepJ --> stepH
    stepD --> stepK[Renewal Survey Every 5 Years]
    stepK --> stepL{Compliant?}
    stepL -- Yes --> stepM[New ILLC Valid 5 Years]
    stepL -- No --> stepN[Refuse Certificate]
    stepF --> stepO[Port State Control Inspection]
    stepO --> stepP{Detainable Deficiency?}
    stepP -- Yes --> stepQ[Detention Until Rectification]
    stepP -- No --> stepR[Clear and Proceed]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The annual survey and the 5-yearly renewal survey are **independent events**. A ship can pass annual surveys and still fail the renewal survey if structural deterioration has progressed. The 5-year cycle ensures **mid-life verification** of all original conditions.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Draft and Freeboard Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    base1[Keel Baseline] --> depth1[Moulded Depth D]
    base1 --> draft1[Draft d Submerged in Water]
    base1 --> freeb1[Freeboard F equals D minus d]
    side1[Waterline] --> freeb1
    deck1[Top of Freeboard Deck] --> freeb1
    zA[Zone A Tropical Lat less than 20 deg] --> load1[Permits T line]
    zB[Zone B Summer Lat 20 to 45 deg] --> load2[Permits S line]
    zC[Zone C Winter Lat above 45 deg] --> load3[Permits W and WNA lines]
    zA --> fresh1[Permits TF and F in fresh water]
    zB --> fresh2[Permits F in fresh water]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** This diagram is the **geographical operational decision-tree** used by **loading masters and charterers** to determine which load line applies for the **voyage route**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]

**Define the term "Plimsoll Mark" and explain its significance under the International Convention on Load Lines, 1966.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Expectation:** Students must mention the disc dimensions, position, and at least two significance points.

The **Plimsoll Mark** is a circular disc of **300 mm diameter**, bisected by a **horizontal line 450 mm long and 25 mm thick**, painted on each side of the hull amidships, with the upper edge of the horizontal line indicating the **maximum permissible summer salt water draft**.

**Significance:**

1. **Visual safety limit:** It provides a single, instantly recognizable mark indicating the safe loading limit, preventing overloading disasters.
2. **Legal evidence:** It serves as the legally binding maximum draft for the vessel under the ICLL.
3. **Compliance with ICLL 1966:** It is mandatory for all ships subject to the Convention; absence or illegibility constitutes a detachable deficiency under PSC.
4. **Uniformity across jurisdictions:** It provides a globally consistent symbol accepted by all maritime authorities.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition with dimensions, 2 marks significance with 4 points.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Distinguish between "Freeboard Deck" and "Superstructure Deck" under the ICLL.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Freeboard Deck | Superstructure Deck |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The uppermost complete deck exposed to weather and sea, with permanent means of closing all openings. | A deck above the freeboard deck, extending at least 0.07L longitudinally, with side bulkheads. |
| **Reference** | Reference for freeboard measurement. | Provides additional reserve buoyancy (sheer credit). |
| **Openings** | All openings must be weathertight. | Openings may be of lower weathertight standard. |
| **Example** | Upper deck of a bulk carrier. | Poop, bridge, or forecastle deck. |

**(3 marks: tabular distinction with 4 valid points.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every 14-mark question has an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain in detail the procedure for computing the minimum freeboard of a Type B ship as per the ICLL 1966. List at least six corrections that may be applied to the basic freeboard.**

**Model Answer:**

The minimum freeboard of a Type B ship is computed as follows:

**Step 1 — Determine the Rule Length (L).**
$L$ is the length measured at 85% of the least moulded depth, or from the fore side of stem to the axis of the rudder stock, whichever is greater.

**Step 2 — Compute the Block Coefficient ($C_b$).**
$$
C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times d}
$$
where $\nabla$ = moulded displacement volume.

**Step 3 — Read the Tabular Freeboard.**
The tabular freeboard $F_T$ is read from the ICLL Type B Table, interpolated for the specific $L$ and $C_b$.

**Step 4 — Apply the Standard Corrections.**

| Correction | Description | Sign |
|---|---|---|
| **$C_b$ correction** | Adjusts for very full or fine forms | ± |
| **Depth correction** | For $D < L/15$ or $D > L/18$ | ± |
| **Sheer correction** | For excessive or deficient sheer | ± |
| **Superstructure credit** | Deduction for poop, bridge, forecastle | – |
| **Trunk correction** | Deduction or addition for trunks | ± |
| **Machinery space deduction** | Deduction for A-engine spaces aft of amidships | – |
| **Minimum bow height** | Compliance with Regulation 39 | – |

**Step 5 — Final Minimum Freeboard.**
$$
F_{\min} = F_T \pm \text{All Corrections}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks for procedural steps, 3 marks for at least 6 corrections in a table, 2 marks for the final equation.)**

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A ship of length 150 m and summer draft 9.20 m has a summer freeboard of 1.75 m. Compute (i) the tropical freeboard, (ii) the winter freeboard, and (iii) the WNA freeboard.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $L$ = 150 m
- $d_S$ = 9.20 m
- $F_S$ = 1.75 m

**(i) Tropical Freeboard:**

Per ICLL Reg 40, the tropical allowance = $d_S / 48$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_T &= F_S - \frac{d_S}{48} \\
&= 1.75 - \frac{9.20}{48} \\
&= 1.75 - 0.1917 \\
&= 1.5583 \text{ m} \approx 1.558 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Winter Freeboard:**

Per ICLL Reg 40, the winter allowance (above S line) = $d_S / 48$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_W &= F_S + \frac{d_S}{48} \\
&= 1.75 + 0.1917 \\
&= 1.9417 \text{ m} \approx 1.942 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) WNA Freeboard:**

WNA is fixed 50 mm above the Winter line:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{WNA}} &= F_W + 0.050 \\
&= 1.9417 + 0.050 \\
&= 1.9917 \text{ m} \approx 1.992 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result Table:**

| Line | Freeboard (m) |
|---|---|
| Summer (S) | 1.750 |
| Tropical (T) | 1.558 |
| Winter (W) | 1.942 |
| WNA | 1.992 |

**(7 marks: 2 marks each for T and W with derivation, 1 mark for WNA with 50 mm rule, 2 marks for final table.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Describe the surveys and certification requirements under the ICLL 1966. Highlight the validity, renewal, and conditions under which a certificate becomes void.**

**Model Answer:**

The ICLL prescribes **three surveys** under Article 14:

**1. Initial Survey:**
Conducted before a new ship enters service or before re-registration of an existing ship. It is a comprehensive survey verifying full compliance with the Convention. On satisfactory completion, the **International Load Line Certificate (ILLC)** is issued.

**2. Annual Survey:**
Conducted within **3 months before or after the anniversary date** of the certificate. It is a general visual inspection of the load line marks, hull, hatch covers, and closing appliances. If satisfactory, the survey is endorsed on the existing ILLC.

**3. Renewal Survey:**
Conducted every **5 years**, equivalent in scope to the initial survey. On satisfactory completion, a **new ILLC valid for 5 years** is issued.

**Validity:**
- ILLC is valid for **5 years** from the date of issue.
- May be **extended by 5 months** if the renewal survey cannot be completed in time.
- The extension is **only for the purpose of allowing the ship to complete its voyage to a port of survey**, not to continue trading indefinitely.

**Conditions under which the ILLC becomes void:**
1. Material alterations are made to the hull, superstructure, or closing appliances without prior notification and survey.
2. The ship is transferred to another flag without re-survey.
3. The Administration withdraws the certificate due to safety concerns.
4. The ship is found unseaworthy or unmanageable by Port State Control.

**(7 marks: 1 mark for each survey, 1 mark for validity, 2 marks for void conditions.)**

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the various load line markings required under the ICLL 1966 and explain how the spacing between them is determined.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Students are expected to **draw a clearly labelled diagram** of the Plimsoll mark with all six lines (TF, F, T, S, W, WNA). Marks are awarded for diagram accuracy and tabular explanation.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
                              WNA ──────  
                              W  ───────  
                              S  ────────  ←── Deck Line
                              T  ────────
                              F  ─────────
                              TF ────────
                                   ●
                              [Plimsoll Disc]
```

**Description of Lines:**

| Line | Position | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **S (Summer)** | At the disc level | Baseline mark |
| **T (Tropical)** | Below S | $S - d_S/48$ |
| **F (Fresh Water)** | Below S | Density correction |
| **TF (Tropical Fresh)** | Lowest of all | $T - $ density correction in tropics |
| **W (Winter)** | Above S | $S + d_S/48$ |
| **WNA (Winter North Atlantic)** | Highest of all | $W + 50$ mm (fixed) |

**Spacing Logic:**
- The S-T and S-W spacings are **draft-dependent** and equal to $d_S/48$.
- The W-WNA spacing is **fixed at 50 mm**, independent of the ship's size.
- The F and TF lines are determined by **density correction formulae** involving the freeboard and the difference between fresh and salt water density.

**(7 marks: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for line identification, 2 marks for spacing logic.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Load Line Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 50 mm fixed offset for WNA** — Examiners specifically test this. Always state "WNA = W + 50 mm" explicitly.
> 2. **Conflating fresh water correction in metres vs millimetres** — The ICLL formula gives the answer in **mm**. State units clearly.
> 3. **Incorrect disc dimensions** — 300 mm diameter and 450 mm horizontal line are non-negotiable. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for wrong dimensions.
> 4. **Confusing Zone and Seasonal Markings** — Tropical is **T**, Tropical Fresh is **TF**, Summer Tropical was discontinued. Know the alphabet.
> 5. **Forgetting survey periodicity** — Annual survey has a **3-month window**, not 12 months. Renewal is every **5 years**, not 4 or 6.
> 6. **Missing the voiding conditions** — The certificate becomes void if **material alterations** are made — many students omit this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**

### A. Core Convention Facts
- ICLL adopted **5 April 1966**, entered into force **21 July 1968**.
- Administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.
- 1988 Protocol harmonized it with the **SOLAS and MARPOL Conventions**.

### B. Plimsoll Mark Specifications
- Disc diameter: **300 mm**.
- Horizontal line: **450 mm long, 25 mm thick**.
- Position: **Amidships on each side**, on a contrasting background.

### C. Six Load Lines (Memorize in Order from Top to Bottom)
- **WNA** (highest) → **W** → **S** → **T** → **F** → **TF** (lowest)

### D. Spacing Rules
- $S \to W$ and $S \to T$: $d_S / 48$ (draft-dependent).
- $W \to WNA$: **50 mm** (fixed, not draft-dependent).
- $S \to F$: density correction = $F_S \times 0.025 / 40$.

### E. Surveys (Three Types)
1. **Initial** (once) → ILLC issued.
2. **Annual** (every 12 months, ±3 month window) → Endorsement.
3. **Renewal** (every 5 years) → New ILLC.

### F. Certificate Validity
- Valid for **5 years**; extendable by **5 months** for voyage completion only.
- Becomes void upon material alteration, flag transfer, or Administration withdrawal.

### G. Fresh Water Correction
- $\text{FA (mm)} = \dfrac{F \times (s - 1)}{40 \times s} \times 1000$
- $s = 1.025$ (standard sea water).

### H. Tropical and Winter Allowances
- Tropical freeboard = $F_S - d_S/48$ (deeper loading permitted).
- Winter freeboard = $F_S + d_S/48$ (less loading permitted).

### I. Authority
- **Recognized Organization (RO)**: Classification societies (ABS, LR, DNV, BV, etc.) authorized by the flag Administration to conduct surveys on its behalf.

### J. PSC Implications
- A ship with an **illegible, missing, or incorrect Plimsoll mark** is a **detainable deficiency** under Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, and USCG regimes.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Load Line Convention Topic.** Continue to Module 3 for **MARPOL 73/78 and Annex VI** regulations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
