topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ISPS Code, Ship Security Plan, Security Levels, Declaration of Security, Ship Security Alert System, Piracy, Best Management Practices to Deter Piracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The ISPS Code — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** The **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** is a comprehensive set of mandatory measures adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** through **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**, which came into force on **1 July 2004**. It establishes an international framework involving co-operation between Contracting Governments, port authorities, and shipping companies to detect security threats and take preventive measures against security incidents affecting ships or port facilities used in international trade.

The ISPS Code is essentially the maritime world's response to the post-9/11 security environment, supplementing the long-standing safety-focused SOLAS Convention with a parallel security architecture.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of the ISPS Code as the **"security ID-card and CCTV system"** of a large office complex. Just as a corporate office building requires every employee to carry an ID badge, every visitor to be logged, every entry to be monitored, and emergency drills to be conducted at different threat levels, the ISPS Code requires every ship and port to have a designated Security Officer, a written Security Plan, identification systems, monitoring equipment, and a graduated response framework. The "threat colour codes" (green/yellow/red) used in government buildings are directly analogous to the **Security Levels 1, 2, and 3** under the ISPS Code.

The trigger for the ISPS Code was the recognition that approximately **90% of world trade by volume** moves through ships and ports, making maritime infrastructure a high-value target for terrorism, piracy, smuggling, and sabotage.

---

## 1.2 Structural Anatomy of the ISPS Code

The ISPS Code is divided into two complementary parts:

| Part | Designation | Nature | Key Content |
|------|-------------|--------|-------------|
| **Part A** | Mandatory | **Binding** on all Contracting Governments | Detailed operational requirements, implementation rules, certification procedures |
| **Part B** | Guidance/Advisory | **Non-binding** (recommendatory) | Best-practice guidance on how to comply with Part A requirements |

### What Does the ISPS Code Apply To?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope of Application:** The Code applies to the following categories of ships engaged on international voyages:
> - **Passenger ships** (including high-speed passenger craft)
> - **Cargo ships** of **500 gross tonnage (GT) and above** — the same threshold as SOLAS Chapter V
> - **Mobile offshore drilling units (MODUs)** and other port-facility-servicing vessels
> - All **port facilities** serving such ships (e.g., container terminals, oil/gas terminals, passenger terminals)

Ships below 500 GT are exempt, but the Code encourages voluntary compliance.

---

## 1.3 Three Pillars of ISPS Compliance

The ISPS Code rests on **three primary obligation pillars** which are assessed in KTU examinations:

1. **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** — written, vessel-specific security document
2. **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** — designated competent person on board every ship
3. **Company Security Officer (CSO)** — designated competent person in the shipping company

These three pillars are supported by:
- **Security Levels 1, 2, 3** (graduated threat response)
- **Declaration of Security (DoS)** — interface tool between ship and port
- **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** — covert distress signalling
- **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)** — vessel security history
- **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)** — flag-state compliance proof

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Date for Examinations:** The ISPS Code was **adopted on 12 December 2002** at IMO Diplomatic Conference and **entered into force on 1 July 2004**. Remember this date for KTU questions.

---

## 1.4 Definitions of Critical Terminology (KTU Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ISPS Definitions (Must Memorise for KTU 2024 Exams):**

- **Ship Security Plan (SSP):** A plan developed to ensure the application of measures on board the ship designed to protect persons on board, cargo, cargo transport units, ship's stores, and the ship itself from the risks of a security incident.

- **Ship Security Officer (SSO):** The person on board the ship, accountable to the Master, designated by the Company as responsible for the security of the ship, including implementation and maintenance of the SSP and liaison with the CSO and PFSO.

- **Company Security Officer (CSO):** The person designated by the Company for ensuring that a ship security assessment is carried out; that a ship security plan is developed, submitted for approval, and thereafter implemented and maintained.

- **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO):** The person responsible for the security of a port facility, including implementation and maintenance of the port facility security plan (PFSP), and liaison with ship security officers and company security officers.

- **Declaration of Security (DoS):** An agreement reached between a ship and a port facility (or another ship) that specifies the security measures each will undertake in respect of their interface.

- **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS):** A system that, when activated, transmits a ship-to-shore security alert to a competent authority designated by the Administration, identifying the ship, its location, and indicating that the security of the ship is under threat or has been compromised.

- **Security Level 1:** Normal operation — the level at which ships and port facilities normally operate (baseline).
- **Security Level 2:** Heightened risk — appropriate security measures must be maintained for a period of time as a result of heightened risk of a security incident.
- **Security Level 3:** Exceptional/imminent threat — further specific protective security measures must be maintained for a limited period when a security incident is probable or imminent.

---

## 1.5 Piracy — Legal and Operational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (UN Convention on the Law of the Sea – UNCLOS, Article 101):**
> **Piracy** consists of any of the following acts:
> (a) any **illegal acts of violence or detention**, or any act of **depredation**, committed by the **crew or passengers** of a private ship or aircraft, and directed against:
>   (i) another ship or aircraft, or
>   (ii) against persons or property on board such ship or aircraft;
> (b) any act of **voluntary participation** in the operation of a ship or aircraft with knowledge of facts making it a pirate ship or aircraft;
> (c) any act of **inciting or of intentionally facilitating** an act described in subparagraph (a) or (b).

> [!NOTE]
> **Jurisdictional Rule:** For an act to qualify as piracy under UNCLOS, it must occur **outside the territorial sea** of any State (i.e., on the high seas or in the Exclusive Economic Zone, EEZ). Acts of armed robbery within a country's territorial waters are termed **"armed robbery against ships"** under the **SUA Convention and IMO Resolution A.1025(26)** — they are NOT piracy in the strict UNCLOS sense, though both are prosecutable.

### Real-World Significance

- The global hotspot regions for piracy include the **Gulf of Aden / Horn of Africa (declining since 2012)**, the **Gulf of Guinea (West Africa — major current threat)**, the **Strait of Malacca / Singapore (declining)**, the **Caribbean**, and the **Indian Ocean**.
- Annual economic cost of piracy (insurance, rerouting, security, ransoms) has historically exceeded **USD 7–8 billion**.

---

## 1.6 Best Management Practices (BMP) to Deter Piracy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Best Management Practices (BMP)** are a set of non-binding, industry-developed, risk-based recommendations published by a coalition of maritime organisations (notably **BIMCO, INTERTANKO, ICS, INTERCARGO, OCIMF, Witherby Publishing, and the Marshall Islands/ Liberian flags**) to assist ship operators and masters in preventing and suppressing piracy and armed robbery. The most current version is **BMP 5 (released in 2018, applicable 2018–present)**.

The BMP complements the ISPS Code by providing **practical, operational, and tactical guidance** that translates legal obligations into bridge-level actions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT SHEET

## 2.1 The Ship Security Plan (SSP) — Detailed Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Basis:** ISPS Code Part A, Section 9 (Regulation 9, SOLAS Chapter XI-2).

The SSP is the **central operational document** that translates the ISPS Code's principles into vessel-specific procedures. It is a **confidential, ship-specific, and company-specific** plan.

### 2.1.1 Mandatory Contents of the SSP (KTU High-Yield List)

> [!NOTE]
> **An SSP MUST address all the following eleven elements** (per ISPS Code Part A, Section 9):

1. **Measures designed to prevent weapons, dangerous substances, and devices** intended for use against persons, ships, or ports from being taken on board the ship.
2. **Identification of the restricted areas** and measures for the control of access to them.
3. **Measures for the prevention of unauthorized access** to the ship, its cargo spaces, and to persons on board.
4. **Procedures for responding to security threats** or breaches of security, including provisions for maintaining critical operations of the ship or ship/port interface.
5. **Procedures for responding to any security instruction** Contracting Governments may give at Security Level 3.
6. **Procedures for evacuation** in case of security threats or breaches of security.
7. **Duties of shipboard personnel** assigned security responsibilities and of other shipboard personnel with security-related duties.
8. **Procedures for auditing** the security activities described in the plan.
9. **Procedures for the training, drills, and exercises** associated with the plan.
10. **Procedures for interfacing with port facility security activities**.
11. **Procedures for the periodic review** of the plan and for updating it.

### 2.1.2 SSP Approval and Custody

| Aspect | Standard |
|--------|----------|
| **Drafted by** | CSO (Company Security Officer) |
| **Approved by** | Administration (Flag State) or Recognized Security Organization (RSO) |
| **Stored on board** | Master, SSO, and a sealed copy held by CSO |
| **Confidentiality** | Treated as **RESTRICTED/SECURITY-SENSITIVE** — no general crew access |
| **Periodic Review** | Required, especially after any security incident or change in vessel layout |
| **Language** | Working language of the ship (typically English) |

---

## 2.2 Security Levels — The Three-Tier Threat Response System

The ISPS Code adopts a **traffic-light conceptual framework**, similar to national terror-threat advisory systems. The flag-state Administration, port-state authorities, or contracting governments set the level.

### 2.2.1 Comparative Matrix of Security Levels

| Parameter | **Level 1 (Normal)** | **Level 2 (Heightened)** | **Level 3 (Exceptional)** |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Threat Posture** | Baseline routine | Risk of incident; no specific target | Incident probable or imminent; specific target possibly identified |
| **Trigger Source** | Default operating level | Intelligence / heightened risk | Specific threat intel / direct attack warning |
| **Access Control** | Standard checks, ID verification | Enhanced, additional screening | Full lockdown, no non-essential access |
| **Restricted Areas** | Defined but normally accessed | Reinforced monitoring, frequent patrols | Fully sealed, continuous guarding |
| **Cargo Handling** | Routine | Additional checks on stores/cargo | Suspended or supervised by armed response |
| **Monitoring** | Standard CCTV, logs | Increased surveillance, foot patrols | 24/7 armed watch, real-time monitoring |
| **Drills** | Periodic (at least once per quarter) | Frequency increased | Reactive exercises simulating threat |
| **Declaration of Security (DoS)** | Routine baseline | Updated and re-acknowledged | Re-executed with new protective clauses |
| **Examples of Actions** | ID badges, gangway watch, SSP in force | Extra lookouts, AIS verification, restricted-area sealing | Embark armed personnel, abandon interface, sail away |

### 2.2.2 Who Sets the Security Level?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three authorities can set or modify Security Levels:
> 1. **Flag State Administration** — for its own ships anywhere in the world.
> 2. **Port State / Contracting Government** — for ships in its territorial waters or port facilities.
> 3. **Ship's Master** — in special cases, can unilaterally raise the level and report to CSO/Administration (Master's overriding authority under SOLAS).

The Master retains the **overriding authority** to make decisions for the safety and security of the ship — this overrides commercial instructions.

---

## 2.3 Declaration of Security (DoS) — Ship-Port Interface Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Declaration of Security (DoS)** is a written, signed agreement between a ship and a port facility (or between two ships in ship-to-ship activity) that records the security measures each party commits to apply during their interface. It is essentially a "shared security contract."

### 2.3.1 When is a DoS Required?

> [!NOTE]
> A DoS shall be completed when:
> - The ship is operating at **Security Level 2 or 3**; or
> - There is a **ship-to-ship activity**; or
> - There is a **ship-to-port-facility interface**; or
> - The **port facility or the ship** has a justified reason to request one (e.g., unusual cargo, history of incidents).

At **Security Level 1**, a DoS is not mandatory but is recommended for high-risk interfaces.

### 2.3.2 Key Information Recorded in a DoS

- Identification of the ship and the port facility
- Security Level at which the ship and the port are operating
- Names and 24-hour contact details of SSO and PFSO
- Specific security measures each party will apply
- Confirmation that the SSP and PFSP are in compliance
- Duration and location of the interface
- Circumstances under which the DoS ceases to be valid
- Acknowledgement that any false statement may invalidate the DoS

### 2.3.3 KTU Conceptual Hook

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Analogy:** A DoS is like a **handshake with shared responsibilities** between two parties entering a business relationship — like a contractor's site-safety agreement in construction. Each side lists what it will do, signs, and the document governs the working relationship.

---

## 2.4 Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) — Covert Distress Signalling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Basis:** ISPS Code Part A, Section 9.4, and SOLAS Regulation XI-2/6.

The SSAS is a **silent alarm** — a system that allows the Master (or SSO) to send a covert alert to a shore-based competent authority if the ship is under attack, threat, or has been compromised. The system is designed to be **invisible to attackers**.

### 2.4.1 Operational Features of SSAS

| Feature | Specification |
|---------|---------------|
| **Activation** | Manual, from at least two different locations on the ship (one of which is the navigation bridge) |
| **Alert Recipients** | Flag-state designated Competent Authority (and optionally the shipping company) |
| **Alert Content** | Ship's identity, position (GPS), and a security alert message |
| **Method of Transmission** | **Long Range Identification and Tracking (LRIT)** infrastructure or other dedicated secure communication |
| **Status on Bridges** | **Covert** — no audible or visual indication on the bridge; only designated personnel know |
| **Reset / Cancellation** | Only by the Administration, never by the attackers |
| **Testing** | At least annually; results recorded |

### 2.4.2 Why SSAS Matters — KTU Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> The SSAS is the **silent, automatic radio burglar-alarm** of a ship. The attackers should NEVER know the alert has been raised, because if they do, they may kill the crew, scuttle the ship, or accelerate their attack. This stealth principle is the heart of SSAS design.

The SSAS is integrated with the ship's **GMDSS** (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System) and **LRIT** (Long Range Identification and Tracking) systems.

---

## 2.5 Piracy — Extended Legal Framework

### 2.5.1 The UNCLOS Definition (Article 101) — KTU Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Conditions Must Be Satisfied for Piracy Under UNCLOS:**
> 1. **Illegal acts of violence, detention, or depredation** (must involve force or threat thereof);
> 2. **Committed by crew/passengers of a private ship** (i.e., not a warship or government vessel acting for the state — that would be a "warship" act);
> 3. **Directed against another ship, persons, or property on the high seas or outside the jurisdiction of any state** (i.e., **outside territorial seas**).

If these three conditions are met, the act qualifies as **piracy *jure gentium*** (piracy under international law).

### 2.5.2 Universal Jurisdiction Over Pirates

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Any State may **seize pirate ships, arrest the persons, and seize the property on board** — this is **universal jurisdiction**. No State has to claim the pirates as its nationals, no need for the flag state of the victim ship to authorise. This is one of the oldest principles of international law, going back to the 17th century.

### 2.5.3 Distinguishing Piracy From Other Maritime Crimes (High-Yield Table)

| Crime | Definition | Jurisdiction | UNCLOS Article |
|-------|------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Piracy** | Violent illegal act by private ship vs. another ship, **on high seas / EEZ** | Universal | Art. 101–107 |
| **Armed Robbery Against Ships** | Similar acts **within territorial sea** of a state | That coastal state | SUA Convention, IMO Res. A.1025(26) |
| **Maritime Terrorism** | Acts to intimidate population, compel government, for political/ideological motives | SUA Convention 2005, national laws | SUA 1988/2005 |
| **Hijacking (Aircraft)** | Air piracy | The Hague Convention 1970, Beijing 2010 | N/A (separate regime) |

### 2.5.4 ReCAAP — Regional Cooperation

> [!NOTE]
> The **Regional Cooperation Agreement on Combating Piracy and Armed Robbery against Ships in Asia (ReCAAP Information Sharing Centre)** is based in **Singapore** and operational since 2006. It is a model for regional information-sharing centres. Similar centres exist in **Djibouti (ReCAAP for Africa analogue, e.g., the Djibouti Code of Conduct 2009)** and **Yaoundé (Gulf of Guinea Code of Conduct 2013)**.

---

## 2.6 Best Management Practices (BMP) — The Tactical Layer

### 2.6.1 Genesis of BMP

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Background:** BMP originated in 2005 as **"Best Management Practices for Protection Against Somalia Based Piracy"** following the surge in Gulf of Aden piracy. The current global version is **BMP 5 (2018)**, which broadened the scope to cover all known piracy hotspots worldwide. BMP is published jointly by **Witherby Publishing Group** and supported by **BIMCO, ICS, INTERTANKO, INTERCARGO, OCIMF, and the Marshall Islands/Liberian flags**.

### 2.6.2 The Five-Pillar Framework of BMP 5 (KTU High-Yield)

| Pillar | Title | Core Action |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| **1** | **Plan** | Conduct thorough **voyage planning and risk assessment**; identify threats; consult maritime security centres (MSCHOA, UKMTO) |
| **2** | **Harden** | Apply **citadel, citadel-equivalent, or hardening measures**; razor wire; covered bridge windows; secure doors |
| **3** | **Brief** | **Master to brief the entire crew** on threats, plans, rules for use of force, reporting |
| **4** | **Watch** | Continuous **visual and radar watch** for small craft, mother ships; use of LRIT/AIS |
| **5** | **Defend** | Apply **self-defence measures** consistent with flag-state rules of engagement; activate SSAS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Five additional supporting measures** accompany the five pillars: register with MSCHOA, comply with reporting (UKMTO), maintain communications, implement self-protection equipment (SPE), and follow naval convoy/group transit systems.

### 2.6.3 BMP and the "Citadel" Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **citadel** is a pre-designated, **fortified, secure room on the ship** (usually in the engine room or accommodation block) where the crew can retreat if pirates board. The citadel is equipped with:
> - Reinforced steel doors
> - Independent communications (SSAS, satellite phone)
> - Provisions (food, water, medical) for at least 5–7 days
> - Controlled ventilation
> - Hardened access control

The citadel is a **passive self-protection** measure, not a weapons platform.

---

## 2.7 KTU Quick-Reference Formula/Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 2):**

| Concept | Key Numbers / Rules |
|---------|---------------------|
| ISPS Code entry into force | **1 July 2004** |
| Adoption date | **12 December 2002** |
| SOLAS Chapter applied | **Chapter XI-2** |
| ISPS Code Parts | **Part A (mandatory) + Part B (advisory)** |
| ISPS ship threshold | **500 GT and above** (cargo) + all passenger ships |
| Number of Security Levels | **3 (1, 2, 3)** |
| DoS required at Security Level | **2 or 3** (or any time the port/ship requests) |
| SSAS activation points | **At least 2 locations** (incl. bridge) |
| SSAS indication on bridge | **Covert (silent)** |
| ReCAAP HQ | **Singapore** (since 2006) |
| Djibouti Code of Conduct | **2009** (East Africa / Western Indian Ocean) |
| Yaoundé Code of Conduct | **2013** (Gulf of Guinea) |
| BMP current version | **BMP 5 (2018)** |
| Piracy definition source | **UNCLOS Article 101** |
| Universal jurisdiction | **Yes, for piracy** |
| Threshold for territorial sea | **12 nautical miles from baselines** |
| EEZ extent | **200 nautical miles** |
| Master's overriding authority | **SOLAS — safety & security** |
| Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR) — entry periods | **On delivery, on flag change, on company change, on decommissioning** |
| ISSC validity | **5 years maximum** (subject to intermediate verifications) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Deriving the ISPS Ship Security Plan: Step-by-Step Creation Logic

The SSP is created through a structured process, often called the **SSA → SSP → ISSC chain**. Below is the full logical flow:

### Step 1: Ship Security Assessment (SSA)

$$\text{Step 1: Identify Threats} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Identify Vulnerabilities} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Calculate Risk}$$

**Detailed logic:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Identify Threats (T)} &= \{\text{Piracy, Terrorism, Smuggling, Stowaways, Drug Trafficking, Cargo Theft, ...}\} \\
\text{Identify Vulnerabilities (V)} &= \{\text{Age of vessel, crew training, layout, route, ...}\} \\
\text{Calculate Risk (R)} &= f(T, V, C)
\end{aligned}

where $C$ is the consequence severity. The output of the SSA is a **documented assessment of security risks and vulnerabilities specific to the ship, its cargo, its trade route, and its personnel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the SSA first?** Because the SSP is a *prescription* — it cannot be written before the *diagnosis* (SSA) is complete. This mirrors the **doctor-first, then prescription** model in medicine. KTU questions often test the order: SSA → SSP → Approval → ISSC.

### Step 2: Draft the SSP

Based on the SSA findings, the CSO drafts the SSP. The plan addresses the **eleven mandatory elements** listed in Section 2.1.1 above.

### Step 3: Approval

$$\text{SSP Drafted} \xrightarrow{\text{Submitted to}} \text{Flag State Admin / RSO} \xrightarrow{\text{Verification}} \text{Approved SSP}$$

The Administration may delegate the approval to a **Recognized Security Organization (RSO)** (e.g., classification societies like Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas).

### Step 4: Implementation

After approval, the SSO implements the SSP on board, with support from the CSO shore-side.

### Step 5: Certification

$$\text{Approved SSP} + \text{ISSC Audit} \rightarrow \text{ISSC Issued} \rightarrow \text{Continuous Compliance via Verification Surveys}$$

The **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)** is the formal proof of compliance. It is valid for **5 years**, with intermediate verifications.

---

## 3.2 Deriving the Activation Logic of SSAS

The SSAS has a precise operational logic that KTU frequently tests. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Threat Detection

$$\text{Threat Detected} \xrightarrow{\text{Notification to Master / SSO}} \text{Threat Confirmed}$$

### Step 2: Activation Decision

The Master, in consultation with SSO, decides whether to activate SSAS. **Note:** the system can be activated even if attackers are present and watching, because:

$$\text{SSAS Activation} \rightarrow \text{No Bridge Indication} \rightarrow \text{Attackers Unaware} \rightarrow \text{Alert Sent Silently}$$

### Step 3: Alert Transmission

\begin{aligned}
\text{SSAS Alert Generated} &\rightarrow \text{Satellite Communication} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Flag-State Competent Authority (and optionally Company)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Content: \{Ship ID, GPS Position, Security Status\}}
\end{aligned}

### Step 4: Shore-Side Response

The receiving authority activates a response protocol — naval task force, anti-piracy forces (e.g., EU NAVFOR, Combined Task Force 151), and law-enforcement coordination.

### Step 5: Deactivation

Only the Administration (or designated authority) can **reset** the alert. The attackers **cannot silence** it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logic Check:** Why is bridge indication forbidden? — Because if attackers see the alert has been sent, they may **hasten their attack, harm crew, scuttle the vessel, or ransom the ship** before help arrives. The covert nature preserves the response window.

---

## 3.3 Deriving the Security Level Escalation Logic

The Security Level escalates through a structured decision flow:

### Decision Tree

\begin{aligned}
\text{Intelligence Input} \rightarrow \text{Threat Assessment} \rightarrow \text{Decision Authority} \rightarrow \text{Level Set}
\end{aligned}

**Detailed logic:**

1. **Level 1** = Default, no specific threat indicator. Routine ISPS implementation.

2. **Level 2** triggers when:
   - There is credible intelligence of a generic threat
   - A recent incident has occurred in the area
   - Port state raises level for vessels in its waters
   - Vessel's flag state raises level for its fleet

3. **Level 3** triggers when:
   - Specific targeting of the ship or its cargo is detected
   - An attack is imminent (e.g., a suspect vessel is tracking)
   - The vessel is in a war zone or near an active conflict

### Escalation-Response Math

\begin{aligned}
\text{Response Intensity} \propto \text{Security Level}
\end{aligned}

In other words, **higher security level → more restrictive measures, more resources, more frequent reporting, more drills, and (typically) more restricted ship-port interface**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that Security Levels are **not an exact scale**; they are **discrete states** with **mandatory minimum measures** (per ISPS Code Annex 1) and **additional protective measures** that the CSO/SSO/flag state may add. The ISPS Code does NOT prescribe a strict numerical formula — it prescribes a *menu* of measures per level.

---

## 3.4 Deriving the BMP Risk-Based Decision Logic

BMP 5 requires the Master to make a structured threat-response decision. Below is the KTU-friendly analytical matrix:

### BMP 5 Risk Decision Matrix

\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Score (R)} = P_{\text{attack}} \times C_{\text{consequence}}
\end{aligned}

where $P_{\text{attack}}$ is the probability of a piracy attack (assessed from UKMTO/MSCHOA reports, intelligence, and historical data) and $C_{\text{consequence}}$ is the consequence (hijack, ransom, casualties, cargo loss, environmental damage).

| Risk Score | BMP 5 Recommendation |
|------------|----------------------|
| **Low (R ≤ 25)** | Standard BMP 5 implementation, normal watch, register with MSCHOA |
| **Medium (25 < R ≤ 50)** | Enhanced watch, all crew briefed, additional hardening, prepare citadel |
| **High (50 < R ≤ 75)** | Consider armed guards (if flag state permits), group transit, maximum vigilance |
| **Critical (R > 75)** | Pre-position crew in citadel, lock all access points, SSAS ready, request naval escort |

### Step-by-Step Decision Walk-through

\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Voyage Risk Assessment} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Plan (route, speed, transit windows)} \\
\text{Step 3: Harden ship} \rightarrow \text{Step 4: Brief crew} \rightarrow \text{Step 5: Watch (continuous)} \\
\text{Step 6: Defend (if attack)} \rightarrow \text{Step 7: Report} \rightarrow \text{Step 8: Recover}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **BMP 5 emphasises the "Five-Pillar" Plan-Harden-Brief-Watch-Defend model — but the actual deployment of arms (Privately Contracted Armed Security Personnel — PCASP) is a SEPARATE question** governed by flag-state and coastal-state laws. BMP 5 is neutral on whether to use PCASP — it focuses on self-protection through non-lethal means.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SSP vs PFSP vs DoS

| Feature | **SSP (Ship Security Plan)** | **PFSP (Port Facility Security Plan)** | **DoS (Declaration of Security)** |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Issued by** | CSO (draft) + Flag State (approval) | PFSO (draft) + Port State (approval) | Jointly by SSO and PFSO (or ship-to-ship) |
| **Scope** | Ship-specific security procedures | Port-specific security procedures | Records interface security measures |
| **Lifetime** | Continuous; reviewed periodically | Continuous; reviewed periodically | Per interface (or for a defined voyage) |
| **Confidentiality** | Restricted | Restricted | Signed copy retained by both parties |
| **Triggers review** | Change in vessel, route, ownership | Change in port, threat landscape | Change in security level, incident, etc. |
| **Compulsory under ISPS?** | Yes (for every applicable ship) | Yes (for every applicable port) | Mandatory at Level 2/3 or on request |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to compare, emphasise that **SSP and PFSP are "internal" plans**, while **DoS is a "bridge" document** between the two. The DoS does not replace the SSP or PFSP — it supplements them at the interface.

---

## 3.6 Master-Chart: ISPS Code Compliance Workflow

\begin{aligned}
\text{Company} &\rightarrow \text{Appoint CSO} \rightarrow \text{Conduct SSA} \rightarrow \text{Draft SSP} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Submit to Flag State/RSO} \rightarrow \text{Approval} \rightarrow \text{ISSC Issued} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Operations} \rightarrow \text{Periodic Verification & Review}
\end{aligned}

In parallel, the **CSR (Continuous Synopsis Record)** is maintained, recording the vessel's security history throughout its operational life. The CSR must be kept on board and a copy held by the flag state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
