# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the term "Shipping Market Cycle" and list its four phases as per Stopford's model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A shipping market cycle is the recurring pattern of fluctuations in freight rates, charter hire rates, and asset prices caused by the structural imbalance between tonnage supply and maritime trade demand. The four phases are: **1) Trough, 2) Recovery, 3) Peak, and 4) Recession**. (3 marks for complete definition + phase listing)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the significance of the "Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio" in shipping market analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio is the percentage of existing fleet tonnage that has been ordered for future delivery. A ratio above **25%** typically signals impending oversupply and a future market downturn, while a ratio below **10%** indicates tight future supply and potential rate strength. It is the most reliable **forward-looking supply indicator** used by shipowners and analysts. (3 marks for definition + threshold values + significance)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **four phases of the shipping market cycle** as proposed by Stopford. Describe the characteristics of freight rates, orderbook, and shipowner profitability in each phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Panamax bulk carrier has the following voyage economics:
- Freight earned: $800,000
- Bunker cost: $250,000
- Port and canal charges: $90,000
- Cargo carried: 120,000 tons
- Voyage duration: 30 days

Calculate: (i) the net voyage income, (ii) the daily net earnings, and (iii) the required freight rate (RFR). Based on the market rate of $5.50/ton, identify the **current phase of the market cycle** with justification.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Stopford's Four Phases (7 Marks)**

The four phases are described as a continuous loop:

**Phase 1: Trough (1.5 marks)**
- Freight rates fall **below operating costs**; shipowners operate at a loss.
- Orderbook is at multi-year lows; shipyards face layoffs and cancellations.
- **Shipowner profitability:** Negative or marginal; widespread bankruptcies.
- **Example:** 2015–2016 dry bulk market, 1998 tanker market.

**Phase 2: Recovery (1.5 marks)**
- Demand gradually outpaces slowly growing supply.
- Freight rates rise above operating cost; first profits emerge.
- **Shipowner profitability:** Positive but cautious; new orders begin.
- **Orderbook:** Begins to rise modestly; bank financing eases.

**Phase 3: Peak (2 marks)**
- Demand surges (often from commodity super-cycles).
- Freight rates reach **extreme highs**; massive cash flows.
- Orderbook balloons; speculative ordering; newbuilding prices peak.
- **Shipowner profitability:** Record profits; "irrational exuberance."

**Phase 4: Recession (2 marks)**
- New deliveries flood the market as demand weakens.
- Freight rates crash; **scrapping accelerates**; banks tighten lending.
- **Shipowner profitability:** Sharp decline; many firms exit.
- The cycle returns to the Trough.

> **Diagrammatic representation (in answer sheet):** Draw a circular flow diagram with 4 quadrants labeled Trough → Recovery → Peak → Recession, with arrows showing the direction of cyclical flow. (Award 1 mark for diagram clarity.)

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Freight = $800,000
- Bunker = $250,000
- Port/Canal = $90,000
- Cargo = 120,000 tons
- Voyage = 30 days
- Market Rate = $5.50/ton

**Step (i): Net Voyage Income (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Net Voyage Income} = 800{,}000 - 250{,}000 - 90{,}000
$$

$$
= 800{,}000 - 340{,}000 = \mathbf{\$460{,}000}
$$

**Step (ii): Daily Net Earnings (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Daily Net Earnings} = \frac{460{,}000}{30} = \mathbf{\$15{,}333.33 \text{ per day}}
$$

**Step (iii): Required Freight Rate (RFR) (2 marks)**

$$
\text{RFR} = \frac{\text{Total Voyage Costs}}{\text{Cargo}} = \frac{340{,}000}{120{,}000} = \mathbf{\$2.83 \text{ per ton}}
$$

**Step (iv): Cycle Phase Identification (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Market Rate} (\$5.50) > \text{RFR} (\$2.83) \rightarrow \text{Significant Margin}
$$

$$
\text{Margin Ratio} = \frac{5.50}{2.83} = 1.94
$$

A margin of nearly **2× the RFR** indicates the vessel is generating strong profits — consistent with the **Recovery or Peak phase** of the shipping cycle.

> **Cycle Phase Verdict: Recovery → Peak** (justification: freight rate is ~94% above the breakeven level, indicating strong market conditions.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **causes of shipping market cycles**, emphasizing the role of the **2–3 year shipbuilding lead time** and the **pro-cyclical investment behavior** of shipowners. Use suitable examples from the **2003–2008 dry bulk super-cycle** to support your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **time charter market** and the **voyage charter market** with respect to: (i) risk allocation between owner and charterer, (ii) sensitivity to shipping market cycles, and (iii) suitability during different cycle phases. Provide a recommendation for a shipowner's strategy during the **Peak phase**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Causes of Shipping Market Cycles (7 Marks)**

**Cause 1: Long Shipbuilding Lead Time (3 marks)**
- New vessels take **24–36 months** to design, finance, and build.
- Shipowners order during current high-rate environments, not in anticipation of future rates.
- The supply that arrives is therefore **misaligned with** current demand — guaranteeing a future overshoot.
- **2008 Example:** Bulk carrier newbuild orders placed in **2003–2005** (at peak rates) were delivered into the 2008 demand collapse, deepening the recession.

**Cause 2: Pro-Cyclical Investment Behavior (2 marks)**
- Human and institutional psychology drives owners to **chase the previous peak** ("irrational exuberance").
- Banks loosen lending standards during booms, amplifying orderbook growth.
- During busts, credit tightens, worsening the downturn.
- This **positive feedback loop** is self-reinforcing and explains why cycles overshoot in both directions.

**Cause 3: Demand Volatility (1 mark)**
- Maritime trade is a derived demand from global GDP.
- Economic shocks (2008 financial crisis, 2020 COVID) can shift demand by 10–20% within months.

**Cause 4: Asset Price Volatility (1 mark)**
- Second-hand ship prices amplify the cycle by 3–5× compared to freight rate volatility, encouraging speculative buying and selling.

---

#### **Part (b) — Time vs Voyage Charter Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Time Charter | Voyage Charter |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| **Risk Allocation** | Charterer bears voyage cost (bunker, port); owner bears only operating cost. | Owner bears full voyage cost and market risk. |
| **Cycle Sensitivity** | Less sensitive (revenue is fixed per day). | Highly sensitive (revenue depends on fluctuating freight rates). |
| **Suitability in Trough** | Preferred — stable income. | Risky — may not cover costs. |
| **Suitability in Peak** | Owner loses upside potential. | Owner captures full market upside. |

**Strategy Recommendation During Peak (1 mark):**
During a **Peak phase**, a shipowner should adopt a **mixed strategy**:
1. **Lock in long-term time charters** (e.g., 2–3 years) at premium rates to hedge against the inevitable post-peak downturn.
2. Allocate a **smaller portion of the fleet to voyage charters** to capture the high spot market rates.
3. **Avoid speculative newbuilding orders** at peak prices — the typical late-cycle mistake.

> **Strategic Justification:** This balanced approach locks in high earnings while preserving some upside, and crucially, **protects the owner from the coming recession** that historically follows every peak.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Shipping Market Cycles**

1. **Mistake 1: Confusing the cycle phases with random market volatility.** Cycles are **multi-year structural patterns**, not short-term rate swings. Students who use phrases like *"the market is volatile therefore it's in recession"* will lose **2–3 marks** for conceptual imprecision.

2. **Mistake 2: Failing to cite numerical thresholds.** Stating *"orderbook is high"* without specifying **> 25% of fleet** is a half-answer. Always cite the **quantitative benchmark** (20%, 25%, 2.0×, etc.).

3. **Mistake 3: Ignoring the 2–3 year construction lag.** Many students describe the cycle but fail to explain **why** supply is sticky. The lead-time is the **single most important structural cause** and must be mentioned in any 7-mark explanation question.

4. **Mistake 4: Misidentifying current market phase.** A common error is labeling every high-rate period as a "Peak." A true Peak is identified by an **orderbook ratio > 25%** *combined* with **extreme freight rates**. Use the **Analytical Matrix** (Section 3.2) to justify your identification.

5. **Mistake 5: Skipping the diagram in 7-mark cycle questions.** Examiners award **at least 1 mark** specifically for a clean phase-flow diagram. Never omit it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Shipping Market Cycles**

- **Definition:** Recurring fluctuations in freight rates and asset prices driven by supply-demand imbalance in tonnage.
- **Four Phases:** Trough → Recovery → Peak → Recession (Stopford's Model).
- **Cycle Duration:** Typically **5–7 years**; can extend to 10+ years for extreme cycles.
- **Key Cause #1:** **2–3 year shipbuilding lead time** creates supply stickiness.
- **Key Cause #2:** **Pro-cyclical investment behavior** amplifies the boom-bust pattern.
- **Key Cause #3:** Demand is **derived from global GDP** and is highly volatile.
- **Critical Threshold:** Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio of **> 25%** signals future oversupply.
- **Healthy Indicator:** Time Charter Rate ≥ **1.3× Operating Cost**.
- **Volatility Multiplier:** Asset prices swing 3–5× more than freight rates.
- **Key Indices:** BDI (Dry Bulk), BCTI (Tankers), CCFI/SCFI (Containers).
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Net Fleet Growth = Deliveries − Scrapping − Losses
  - Orderbook-to-Fleet = (Order GT / Fleet GT) × 100
  - RFR = Total Voyage Cost / Cargo Carried
  - Daily Net Income = (Revenue − Costs) / Voyage Days
- **Strategic Insight:** **Buy in Trough, Hold in Recovery, Hedge in Peak, Survive in Recession.**
- **2008 Lesson:** Orders placed at peak rates always deliver into the next downturn.
- **2021–2022 Anomaly:** Container shipping experienced an extreme peak driven by COVID-related supply chain disruption — a reminder that **exogenous shocks** can dramatically alter the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Identify the current cycle phase,"* always use the **3-indicator rule** — cite the orderbook ratio, the freight rate vs operating cost, and at least one qualitative factor (e.g., shipyard activity or bank lending). This guarantees full marks in 7-mark and 14-mark analytical questions.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipping Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shipping Risk** refers to the spectrum of perils, hazards, and uncertainties that threaten a maritime venture — encompassing the vessel (hull), its cargo, freight earnings, and third-party liabilities — arising from the unique physical, legal, and commercial environment of ocean-going trade. In the legal-commercial sense, shipping risk is the **quantifiable and insurable exposure** to loss that arises during the international carriage of goods by sea, and it is governed by an interlocking framework of **statute (e.g., Marine Insurance Act 1906), common law principles, international conventions (Hague-Visby Rules, York-Antwerp Rules), and standard market contracts (Institute Clauses, Lloyd's SG Policy, charter parties).**

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats shipping risk under two principal heads:

1. **Physical / Fortuitous Risks** — perils of the sea, fire, collision, stranding, sinking, war, strikes, jettison, etc.
2. **Legal / Commercial Risks** — liability to third parties, cargo claims, general average contributions, salvage, and breach of contract risks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Shipping Risk is studied *not* as an actuarial subject but as a **legal-contractual allocation problem** — i.e., *who bears which risk, under what contract, and how is the loss ultimately distributed via marine insurance and contribution mechanisms.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Seafaring Family"

Imagine a joint family running a long-distance *house-moving* business across rivers and mountains:

- The **truck** (the **ship**) can break down, tip over, or be stolen — that is **hull risk**.
- The **furniture inside** can be damaged, lost, or destroyed — that is **cargo risk**.
- If the truck accidentally damages a neighbour's house, somebody has to pay — that is **third-party liability risk**.
- The family takes a **loan** to replace the truck — if the truck is gone forever, the bank must still be paid; this is the **freight / financial risk**.

In shipping, this family is the **shipowner, the charterer, the cargo owner, the financier, and the insurer** — each "family member" holds a *legally defined* share of risk, and the entire global maritime insurance industry exists to *price, transfer, and share* these risks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:** The *whole purpose* of maritime law is to answer one question for each risk — **"Who pays?"** The instruments that answer it are: the **charter party** (private contract), the **bill of lading** (cargo contract), the **marine insurance policy** (risk transfer), and the **P&I Club** (liability pool).

## 1.3 Standard Risk Metrics Used in Shipping

| Risk Parameter | Industry Standard Metric | Governing Document |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel valuation | **Scrap value** or **Market value** | Institute Clauses – Hulls (ITCH) |
| Insurable interest | **Proximate cause** test | Marine Insurance Act 1906, s.55 |
| General average sacrifice | **Y/A Rules 2016** contribution in *proportion to values saved* | York-Antwerp Rules 2016 |
| War risk premium | **Additional premium (AP)** over standard | Institute War & Strikes Clauses (Hulls) 1/10/83 |
| P&I cover | **Calls** (supplementary premium) | P&I Club rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember:** The **Marine Insurance Act 1906** remains the foundational statute for *all* English-law shipping risk disputes and is implicitly tested in KTU answers through its four defining sections — **Sections 3, 4, 5, and 55**.
>
> - **Section 3** – definition of marine insurance
> - **Section 4** – prohibition of wagering / insurable interest
> - **Section 5** – insurable interest defined
> - **Section 55** – causation and proximate cause

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Shipping Risks

Shipping risks are classified along **three orthogonal axes** that examiners frequently test:

### Axis A — *Subject Matter of the Loss*

1. **Hull & Machinery Risk** – loss of or damage to the vessel itself.
2. **Cargo Risk** – loss of or damage to goods carried.
3. **Freight Risk** – loss of expected freight earnings.
4. **Liability Risk** – third-party claims (collision, pollution, cargo, injury).
5. **Disbursements / Increased Value Risk** – the *war-risk-increased-value* cover taken by the mortgagee.

### Axis B — *Cause of the Loss*

The classic enumeration comes from **s.6(2) of the Marine Insurance Act 1906**:

- Perils of the sea
- Fire
- War perils
- Pirates, rovers, thieves
- Captures, seizures, restraints
- Detainments of all kinds
- Barratry (master's wilful wrongdoing)
- Any other peril of a similar kind

### Axis C — *Extent / Magnitude of the Loss*

1. **Total Loss**
   - **Actual Total Loss (ATL)** — *complete destruction or damage rendering the subject-matter irretrievably lost to the assured*.
   - **Constructive Total Loss (CTL)** — *the subject-matter is reasonably abandoned because an actual total loss appears unavoidable, or the cost of recovery would exceed the value of the recovered thing*.
2. **Partial Loss**
   - **Particular Average** — *partial loss fortuitously sustained by the assured and not forming part of a general-average sacrifice*.
  . **General Average** — *extraordinary sacrifice or expenditure deliberately made for the common safety of the vessel, cargo, and freight, to avert a peril common to all*.

## 2.2 The "Risk Allocation Chain" — How Risk Flows

Risk is *not* a single event but a **chain of contractual and legal transfers** that occurs whenever a ship is fixed. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate this chain in order:

1. **Owner builds / buys ship** → risk lies with owner → owner insures hull (ITCH).
2. **Owner charters ship to charterer** (voyage / time / bareboat) → *charter party* re-allocates operational risks.
3. **Goods loaded** → *bill of lading* issued → cargo risk shifts to carrier, but cargo owner insures goods (ICC A/B/C).
4. **Vessel is mortgaged** → mortgagee insures *mortgagee's interest* (MII) and *increased value* (IV).
5. **Liability emerges** → owner's P&I club covers third-party liability.
6. **Loss occurs** → insurer pays → insurer *subrogates* into the assured's rights.
7. **Multiple parties suffer** → general average declared → *adjusters* compute contributions under York-Antwerp Rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subrogation (s.79 MIA 1906):** After indemnifying the assured, the insurer "steps into the shoes" of the assured and may pursue any third party who caused the loss. This is a *favourite* 3-mark Part A question.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Actual Total Loss (ATL)** | $\text{ATL} = \text{total destruction} \lor \text{irretrievable loss} \lor \text{damage to owner}$ | Vessel sunk beyond recovery |
| 2 | **Constructive Total Loss (CTL)** | $\text{CTL} \iff \text{cost of recovery} > \text{value after recovery}$ | Damaged ship repair > 100% insured value |
| 3 | **Notice of Abandonment (NoA)** | $\text{NoA required} \iff \text{CTL claim}$ | Failure to give NoA = loss treated as partial |
| 4 | **General Average Contribution** | $\text{Contribution} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Value of interest saved}}{\sum \text{All values saved}}\right) \times \text{GA sacrifice / expenditure}$ | Per **Y/A Rule XVII & G** |
| 5 | **Proximate Cause Test (s.55 MIA)** | $\text{Cover} \iff \text{peril insured against} \in \text{proximate cause chain}$ | *Leyland Shipping* v *Norwich Union* [1918] |
| 6 | **Insurable Interest (s.5 MIA)** | $\text{II} = \text{legal or equitable relation to the adventure}$ | Test: assured *stands to benefit* or *suffers loss* |
| 7 | **P&I Call** | $\text{Total Call} = \text{Advance Call} + \text{Supplementary Call} + \text{Reserve Call}$ | Annual contribution by shipowner to P&I Club |
| 8 | **Subrogation Recovery (s.79)** | $\text{Recovery} = \text{Loss paid} - \text{Expenses}$ | Insurer recoups from negligent third party |
| 9 | **General Average Bond** | $\text{Parties} \rightarrow \text{Average Bond + Average Guarantee}$ | Required before cargo released after GA |
| 10 | **Particular Average Franchise** | $\text{Pay} \iff \text{loss} \geq \text{stated \% of insured value}$ | Common: 3% or 5% franchise |

> [!WARNING]
> **No Pipes in Tables:** Where mathematical vertical-bar notation (e.g., absolute value $\vert x \vert$) appears, it is written as $\vert x \vert$ — never with the raw ASCII `|` character which would break the markdown table renderer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Commercial Utility

- **Hull insurance premiums** are calibrated to vessel **age, flag state, classification society record, trading pattern, and IACS compliance** — students pursuing naval architecture or marine engineering careers will use these classifications when conducting *risk-based inspection planning*.
- **General average adjusters** (e.g., Richards Hogg Lindley, McLarens) use the *Y/A formulas* to apportion multi-million-dollar losses in a matter of weeks after a casualty.
- **P&I Clubs** (Gard, Skuld, UK P&I, Steamship Mutual) function as *mutual insurers* and are the unseen backbone of global trade — without them, no major commodity trader would load a single bulk carrier.
- **War risk premiums** spiked from ~0.05% to ~1% of hull value post-2022 due to Red Sea / Black Sea incidents — a current real-world application of the *Institute War Clauses*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The General Average Contribution Formula

The York-Antwerp Rules 2016 govern how a General Average (GA) loss is distributed. The cardinal principle is enshrined in **Rule XVII**:

> *Contributions to a general average shall be made upon the actual values of the property at the termination of the adventure.*

### Step 1 — Identify the *Common Maritime Adventure (CMA)*

A common maritime adventure exists when vessel, cargo, and freight share a common peril. We must first *declare* general average (a lawful act of the master, ratified by the shipowner).

### Step 2 — Classify the Loss

The loss is one of two types:

- **GA Sacrifice** — a deliberate loss (e.g., cargo jettisoned, ship scuttled intentionally on a beach to save the rest).
- **GA Expenditure** — money spent (e.g., port of refuge costs, salvage rewards, wages during the deviation).

### Step 3 — Compute *Contributory Values*

For each interest, we compute the value *saved* at the **destination port**:

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{ship}} = \text{arrived value of ship at destination}
$$

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{cargo}} = \text{net market value at destination}
$$

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{freight}} = \text{gross freight earned less expense}
$$

The **total contributory value (TCV)** is the sum:

$$
\text{TCV} = \text{CV}_{\text{ship}} + \text{CV}_{\text{cargo}} + \text{CV}_{\text{freight}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the *General Average Loss (GAL)*

If the sacrifice was, say, cargo jettisoned worth ₹40 lakh, the GA loss is simply that figure, plus allowable expenditure. Let:

$$
\text{GAL} = \text{Sacrifice} + \text{Allowable Expenditure}
$$

For this worked example, suppose:
- Sacrifice (jettisoned cargo) = ₹40,00,000
- Port of refuge expenses = ₹10,00,000
- Salvage remuneration = ₹5,00,000

So the *total* general average loss is:

$$
\text{GAL} = 40{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 55{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Each Party's *Proportionate Share*

For each interest, the contribution payable is:

$$
\text{Contribution}_i = \left( \dfrac{\text{CV}_i}{\text{TCV}} \right) \times \text{GAL}
$$

Suppose the contributory values are:

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{ship}} = 60{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{cargo}} = 80{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{freight}} = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

Then the total contributory value is:

$$
\text{TCV} = 60{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 150{,}00{,}000
$$

The ship's contribution is:

$$
\text{Contribution}_{\text{ship}} = \left( \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{150{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 55{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
= \left( \dfrac{60}{150} \right) \times 55{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
= 0.4 \times 55{,}00{,}000 = 22{,}00{,}000
$$

The cargo's contribution is:

$$
\text{Contribution}_{\text{cargo}} = \left( \dfrac{80}{150} \right) \times 55{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
= 0.5333\ldots \times 55{,}00{,}000 = 29{,}33{,}333.33
$$

The freight's contribution is:

$$
\text{Contribution}_{\text{freight}} = \left( \dfrac{10}{150} \right) \times 55{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
= 0.0666\ldots \times 55{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}66{,}666.67
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Closure

$$
22{,}00{,}000 + 29{,}33{,}333.33 + 3{,}66{,}666.67 = 55{,}00{,}000.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

The general average account balances — the loss is fully distributed.

## 3.2 The Constructive Total Loss (CTL) Decision Tree

CTL is governed by **s.60(2) of the Marine Insurance Act 1906**. The assured has a *right* to claim CTL (and give notice of abandonment) if **any one** of three conditions is met:

$$
\text{CTL} = \underbrace{\mathbb{1}\left[\,C_{\text{recovery}} > V_{\text{recovered}}\,\right]}_{\text{Cost test}}
\quad \lor \quad
\underbrace{\mathbb{1}\left[\,\text{ATL appears unavoidable}\,\right]}_{\text{Imminence test}}
\quad \lor \quad
\underbrace{\mathbb{1}\left[\,\text{Vessel lost to its owner}\,\right]}_{\text{Deprivation test}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{recovery}}$ = cost of recovery / repair / forwarding
- $V_{\text{recovered}}$ = value of the subject-matter *after* recovery
- $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ = indicator function (returns 1 if condition true, else 0)

### Numerical Illustration of CTL — Cost Test

A bulk carrier is insured for **₹100 crore** (insured value = $V_{\text{insured}}$). After a grounding off Chennai:

- Estimated repair cost: **₹105 crore**
- Vessel's value *after* repair (salvaged, but damaged): **₹98 crore**

The cost test is:

$$
C_{\text{recovery}} > V_{\text{recovered}} \quad \iff \quad 105 > 98 \quad \text{→ TRUE}
$$

The assured may treat the loss as a **Constructive Total Loss**, give a valid **Notice of Abandonment** to the insurer, and recover the *full insured value* of ₹100 crore (less any subsequent salvage proceeds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** If the assured claims CTL but fails to give a **Notice of Abandonment**, the loss is **deemed to be a partial loss only** (s.62(1) MIA 1906). This is one of the *most-tested* pitfalls in KTU answers.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Which Risk Is Borne by Whom?

| Risk Event | Bill of Lading (Hague-Visby) | Voyage Charter Party | Time Charter Party | Hull Policy (ITCH) | Cargo Policy (ICC A) | P&I Club |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Perils of the sea damaging cargo | **Carrier excepted** (Art IV(2)(c)) → cargo bears it | Owner (carrier) excepted | Owner (carrier) excepted | n/a | **Covered** | n/a |
| Ship sinks (ATL) | n/a | n/a | n/a | **Covered (ATL)** | n/a | n/a |
| Fire on board damaging cargo | **Carrier excepted** (Art IV(2)(b)) | Owner excepted | Owner excepted | **Covered** | **Covered** | n/a |
| Collision with another ship (3rd-party) | n/a | n/a | n/a | **3/4 collision liability** | n/a | **1/4 collision liability** |
| Crew injury / illness | n/a | Charterer (during TC) | Time charterer | n/a | n/a | **Covered** |
| Oil pollution | n/a | n/a | n/a | Limited | n/a | **Covered up to club limit** |
| Cargo shortage (short delivery) | **Carrier liable** (Art III) | n/a | n/a | n/a | n/a | **Covered** |
| General average contribution | All parties share | All parties share | All parties share | Insurer pays (suing & labour) | Insurer pays | n/a |
| War risk — vessel bombed | n/a | n/a | n/a | Excluded from ITCH, **covered by Institute War Clauses** | Excluded from ICC A | n/a |
| Bareboat charterer neglects maintenance | n/a | n/a | **Bareboat charterer bears** | Owner still insures | n/a | n/a |

## 3.4 The "Proximate Cause" Logical Flow

Under **s.55(1) MIA 1906**, the insurer is liable only for losses *proximately caused* by a peril insured against. The classic test (*Leyland Shipping* v *Norwich Union Fire Insurance Society* [1918] AC 350):

$$
\text{Loss paid} \iff \text{peril A} \in \text{chain of causation} \land \neg \text{new proximate cause B intervenes}
$$

**Worked Example:** A ship is insured against *perils of the sea*. A storm (peril of the sea) causes the ship to capsize. The cargo is damaged by seawater entering through the hatches. The hatches were defective due to *inherent vice* (not a peril).

- The *storm* is the proximate cause → **insurer pays**.
- Even though the *inherent vice* contributed, the storm is the **dominant effective cause** → insurer liable.

Conversely, if the *inherent vice* alone had caused the water ingress, the loss would be **unrecoverable** under the *perils of the sea* clause.

## 4.1 The Shipping Risk Allocation Architecture

The diagram below maps the *flow of risk* from the moment a ship is conceived through to the final settlement of a casualty. Each block is a contractual / legal instrument; each arrow indicates the direction in which **risk is transferred**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Shipowner] -->|owns hull risk| A2[Hull Insurance ITCH 1/10/83]
    A1 -->|liability exposure| A3[Protection and Indemnity P&I Club]
    A2 -->|reinsures| A4[Marine Reinsurance Market]
    A3 -->|pool| A5[International Group of P&I Clubs]
    A1 -->|charters vessel| B1[Charter Party Contract]
    B1 -->|voyage charter| B2[Voyage Charterer]
    B1 -->|time charter| B3[Time Charterer]
    B1 -->|bareboat charter| B4[Bareboat Charterer]
    C1[Cargo Owner] -->|insures goods| C2[Cargo Insurance ICC A 1/1/09]
    C1 -->|receives B/L| C3[Bill of Lading Hague Visby]
    A1 -->|issues B/L| C3
    D1[Bank / Mortgagee] -->|insures interest| D2[MII Mortgagees Interest Insurance]
    D1 -->|insures extra| D3[IV Increased Value Insurance]
    E1[Master declares GA] --> E2[Average Adjuster]
    E2 -->|Y/A Rules 2016| E3[General Average Contribution]
    F1[Insurer pays loss] -->|subrogation s.79| F2[Insurer sues third party]
    G1[War Strikes peril] --> G2[Institute War Strikes Clauses 1/10/83]

    A1 -.- A3
    B1 -.- C3
    C2 -.- C3
```

## 4.2 The Decision Tree for "Total Loss vs. Partial Loss"

A KTU-favourite is asking the student to *classify* a given loss scenario. The decision tree below encodes the **Marine Insurance Act 1906** logic:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Loss Event] --> q1{Is the subject matter totally destroyed or irretrievably damaged?}
    q1 -- Yes --> ATL[Actual Total Loss s.57 MIA]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is the owner irrevocably deprived of possession?}
    q2 -- Yes --> ATL
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is the cost of recovery greater than the value recovered?}
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is there a valid Notice of Abandonment?}
    q4 -- Yes --> CTL[Constructive Total Loss s.60 MIA]
    q4 -- No --> PL1[Treated as Partial Loss s.62 MIA]
    q3 -- No --> PL2[Partial Loss Particular Average]
    PL2 --> q5{Is it a deliberate sacrifice for common safety?}
    q5 -- Yes --> GA[General Average Y/A Rules]
    q5 -- No --> PA[Particular Average]
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define "Constructive Total Loss" under the Marine Insurance Act 1906. When is a Notice of Abandonment necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Constructive Total Loss (CTL) is defined under **Section 60(2) of the Marine Insurance Act 1906** as a loss where the subject-matter insured is *reasonably abandoned* on account of its actual total loss appearing to be *unavoidable*, or because it could not be preserved from actual total loss *without an expenditure which would exceed its value when preserved*.

A **Notice of Abandonment (NoA)** is a formal written intimation by the assured to the insurer that he treats the loss as a constructive total loss. It is **mandatory** if the assured wishes to claim a total loss on a CTL situation. Under **s.62(1) MIA 1906**, failure to give a valid NoA means the loss is treated as a *partial loss* only, and the assured can recover only the *cost of partial damage*, not the full insured value. The NoA must be given *with reasonable diligence* after being *informed of the loss*, and may be *waived* by the insurer's conduct.

> **Marking key:** Definition (2 marks) + necessity of NoA (1 mark).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between "General Average" and "Particular Average" with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | General Average (GA) | Particular Average (PA) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Deliberate sacrifice for *common safety* | Fortuitous partial loss |
| Borne by | All interests in the adventure (vessel, cargo, freight) in proportion to values saved | The party suffering the loss alone |
| Trigger | Peril threatening the *whole* adventure | Peril affecting *one* interest |
| Legal basis | **York-Antwerp Rules 2016** | **Marine Insurance Act 1906, s.64** |
| Example | Cargo jettisoned to lighten a grounded vessel | Cargo damaged by seawater during the voyage |

**Example for GA:** A vessel runs aground; to refloat her, the master jettisons 200 tonnes of grain into the sea. The grain's value is *deliberately sacrificed* and is shared by ship, cargo, and freight.

**Example for PA:** A storm damages 50 bags of cement in the lower hold due to seawater entry. Only the cement owner bears the loss; the ship and other cargo do not contribute.

> **Marking key:** Two clear distinctions (1.5 marks) + one example of each (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (both sub-parts) from the choice of two.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the different types of marine insurance policies covering shipping risk. Explain the role of the Protection and Indemnity (P&I) Club. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Marine insurance is the principal mechanism by which shipping risk is **transferred** from the commercial operator to a risk-bearer. The principal types are:

1. **Hull & Machinery Insurance** – Governed by the *Institute Time Clauses – Hulls 1/10/83* (ITCH) or *Institute Voyage Clauses – Hulls 1/10/83*. It covers loss of or damage to the vessel, including **actual total loss, constructive total loss, partial loss, general average, salvage, sue and labour**, and **3/4 collision liability**. The remaining 1/4 of collision liability falls to the P&I Club.

2. **Cargo Insurance** – Governed by the *Institute Cargo Clauses (A), (B), or (C) 1/1/09*. ICC A is "all-risks" (subject to exclusions), ICC B is named-perils (fire, sinking, stranding etc.), ICC C is minimal cover. Plus **Institute War Clauses (Cargo)** and **Institute Strikes Clauses (Cargo)** for war/strike extensions.

3. **Freight Insurance** – Covers loss of freight earnings; common in voyage charterparties.

4. **Protection and Indemnity (P&I) Insurance** – Operated as **mutual insurance clubs** (not commercial insurance). The *International Group of P&I Clubs* (13 member clubs) covers third-party liabilities that the hull policy excludes:
   - 1/4 collision liability
   - Cargo claims (except under Hague-Visby exceptions)
   - Crew injury, illness, death, repatriation
   - Oil pollution liability (subject to limits)
   - Wreck removal
   - Fines and penalties
   - Persons other than crew (passengers, stevedores)

5. **War Risk Insurance** – *Institute War & Strikes Clauses – Hulls 1/10/83* and *Time Clauses – Hulls 1/10/83 – Limited Conditions* (used when transiting war-risk zones like the Persian Gulf or Black Sea).

6. **Mortgagees' Interest Insurance (MII)** – Protects the bank/lender in case the hull policy is voided due to owner's breach of utmost good faith (uberrima fides).

**Role of P&I Clubs:**

- Operate on a **mutual basis** – members (shipowners) pay *calls* (advance + supplementary + release) to cover losses.
- **Cover the liability "gap"** left by the hull policy.
- Provide **security** (e.g., P&I Letters of Undertaking) to release vessels from arrest in foreign ports.
- Conduct **loss prevention** and **claims handling** expertise.
- Influence maritime legislation through the **International Group**.

> **Marking key:** Types of policies (4 marks) + P&I role explained (2 marks) + P&I example / mechanism (1 mark).

#### (b) The vessel 'MV Bharath' is insured for ₹100 crore under a hull policy on ITCH terms. The vessel ran aground off Mumbai. The estimated cost of repair is ₹95 crore, and the vessel's value after repair would be ₹92 crore. The vessel's estimated cost of refloating and towing to a safe port is ₹15 crore. The assured wants to claim total loss. Advise the assured on whether they can claim Actual Total Loss (ATL), Constructive Total Loss (CTL), or Partial Loss, citing the relevant legal provisions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Is it an Actual Total Loss (ATL)?**

Under **s.57 of the Marine Insurance Act 1906**, an ATL occurs where the subject-matter is *destroyed, or so damaged as to cease to be a thing of the kind insured*, or where the assured is *irrevocably deprived* of the vessel. Here, the vessel is grounded, not destroyed. The assured is not irrevocably deprived; the vessel can be refloated. **→ NOT an ATL.**

**Step 2 — Is it a Constructive Total Loss (CTL)?**

Under **s.60(2)(i) MIA 1906**, the assured may treat the loss as constructive if *the cost of recovery would exceed the value of the thing recovered*.

$$
C_{\text{recovery}} = \text{Refloat} + \text{Tow} + \text{Repair} = 15 + 95 = 110 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
V_{\text{recovered}} = 92 \text{ crore (post-repair value)}
$$

$$
C_{\text{recovery}} > V_{\text{recovered}} \iff 110 > 92 \quad \text{→ TRUE}
$$

The cost test is **satisfied**. Additionally, the *expenditure test* is met.

**Step 3 — Notice of Abandonment (NoA)**

Under **s.62(1) MIA 1906**, the assured must give a **valid Notice of Abandonment** to claim CTL. The assured must serve the NoA on the insurer **with reasonable diligence after being informed of the casualty**. If NoA is not given, the loss is treated as a *partial loss* and the assured can recover only the repair cost (₹95 crore) — not the full insured value.

**Step 4 — Recovery**

Upon valid NoA, the assured can recover the **full insured value of ₹100 crore** (less any subsequent salvage or proceeds of the vessel recovered by the insurer). The insurer may then exercise **subrogation** under **s.79 MIA 1906** to recover from any third party or to sell the salved vessel.

**Conclusion:** MV Bharath is a **Constructive Total Loss**; the assured should give a **valid Notice of Abandonment** to recover the full insured value.

> **Marking key:** ATL test (1.5 marks) + CTL cost-test calculation (3 marks) + NoA requirement (1.5 marks) + conclusion (1 mark).

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(INTERNAL CHOICE — answer either A or B)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

#### (a) Explain the doctrine of "Proximate Cause" in marine insurance with reference to Section 55 of the Marine Insurance Act 1906 and the leading case of *Leyland Shipping Co Ltd v Norwich Union Fire Insurance Society Ltd* [1918] AC 350. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Doctrine of Proximate Cause**

Under **Section 55(1) of the Marine Insurance Act 1906**:

> *"Subject to the provisions of this Act, and unless the policy otherwise provides, the insurer is liable for any loss proximately caused by a peril insured against, but, subject as aforesaid, he is not liable for any loss which is not proximately caused by a peril insured against."*

**Meaning:** Of the many causes that contribute to a loss, the law identifies the *dominant* or *effective* cause — that cause is treated as the *proximate* cause. The insurer pays only if the proximate cause is a peril covered by the policy.

**The Leyland Case Facts:**

- The vessel *Leyland* was insured against *perils of the sea* but not against *barratry* (master's wilful misconduct).
- During WWI, the master took the vessel into a war-risk zone to earn a freight premium.
- The vessel was torpedoed and sunk by a German submarine.
- The assured claimed the loss was due to *perils of the sea*.
- The House of Lords held: the *proximate cause* was the **wilful deviation into a war zone** (an uninsured peril — barratry), not the perils of the sea.

**Legal Principle Established:**

The proximate cause is **the cause which is the *dominant* and *effective* cause** — not the last cause, not the cause in time, but the cause that *sets the other causes in motion* and *predominates in efficiency*.

**Application to a Shipping Risk Scenario:**

If a vessel is insured against "perils of the sea" and a storm (peril of the sea) causes the ship to list, the cargo shifts, the ship capsizes, and the cargo is lost — the *storm* is the proximate cause, and the insurer pays. However, if the *cargo shift* was due to *inherent vice* (unstable stowage of grain), and the storm merely *triggered* the shift, then the proximate cause may be the *inherent vice* (an excluded peril) — the insurer does not pay.

**Key Takeaway:** The proximate-cause rule prevents insurers from being made liable for losses where the *real* operative cause lies outside the policy cover.

> **Marking key:** s.55 quote (2 marks) + Leyland facts (2 marks) + ratio / holding (2 marks) + application (1 mark).

#### (b) The vessel 'MV Kerala' is on a voyage from Kochi to Rotterdam carrying 50,000 MT of rice. Due to a severe storm in the Bay of Biscay, the vessel listed dangerously. The master, in order to save the vessel and remaining cargo, ordered 5,000 MT of rice to be jettisoned. The vessel was subsequently towed to Lisbon. The general average loss and expenditure amounted to ₹8 crore. The contributory values at the port of destination were: Ship ₹60 crore, Cargo ₹90 crore, Freight ₹10 crore. Compute the general average contribution payable by each interest as per the York-Antwerp Rules 2016. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Common Maritime Adventure & Declare General Average**

The vessel, cargo, and freight were all subject to a *common peril* (storm). The jettison of 5,000 MT of rice was a *deliberate sacrifice* made by the master for the *common safety* of all interests. This is a textbook **General Average Sacrifice** under **Rule II of the Y/A Rules 2016**.

**Step 2 — Compute the Total Contributory Value (TCV)**

$$
\text{TCV} = \text{CV}_{\text{ship}} + \text{CV}_{\text{cargo}} + \text{CV}_{\text{freight}}
$$

$$
\text{TCV} = 60 + 90 + 10 = 160 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the General Average Loss (GAL)**

$$
\text{GAL} = \text{Sacrifice} + \text{Allowable Expenditure} = 8 \text{ crore (given)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Each Party's Contribution**

**Ship's contribution:**

$$
\text{Contribution}_{\text{ship}} = \left( \dfrac{60}{160} \right) \times 8 = 0.375 \times 8 = 3 \text{ crore}
$$

**Cargo's contribution:**

$$
\text{Contribution}_{\text{cargo}} = \left( \dfrac{90}{160} \right) \times 8 = 0.5625 \times 8 = 4.5 \text{ crore}
$$

**Freight's contribution:**

$$
\text{Contribution}_{\text{freight}} = \left( \dfrac{10}{160} \right) \times 8 = 0.0625 \times 8 = 0.5 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Closure)**

$$
3 + 4.5 + 0.5 = 8 \text{ crore} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:**

| Interest | Contributory Value (₹ crore) | Contribution (₹ crore) |
|---|---|---|
| Ship | 60 | **3.00** |
| Cargo | 90 | **4.50** |
| Freight | 10 | **0.50** |
| **Total** | **160** | **8.00** |

The cargo owner, having the highest contributory value, pays the largest share (₹4.5 crore). The loss is fully distributed among all three interests per the **Rule of Equal Sacrifice** embodied in Y/A Rule XVII.

> **Marking key:** TCV computation (1.5 marks) + Ship contribution (1.5 marks) + Cargo contribution (1.5 marks) + Freight contribution (1.5 marks) + Closure verification (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Shipping-Risk Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Missing the Notice of Abandonment (NoA) in CTL questions (–2 marks typical):** Examiners *deliberately* test this. If your CTL answer does not mention the NoA requirement under **s.62 MIA 1906**, you lose at least 2 marks. Always end CTL answers with: *"The assured must give a valid Notice of Abandonment to the insurer with reasonable diligence; failure renders the loss a partial loss."*
>
> 2. **Confusing General Average with Particular Average (–1.5 marks):** Remember — GA is *deliberate sacrifice for common safety*; PA is *fortuitous partial loss*. If you write "cargo was lost in a storm — that is general average," you are wrong. The cargo damage in a storm is *particular average*; GA is when the master *throws cargo overboard* to save the ship.
>
> 3. **Forgetting subrogation in insurance answers (–1 mark):** After the insurer pays, the insurer *steps into the shoes* of the assured under **s.79 MIA 1906**. This is a *favourite* 1-mark addition that examiners expect.
>
> 4. **P&I Club answers missing the "1/4 collision" point (–1 mark):** The hull policy covers 3/4 of collision liability, and the P&I covers the remaining 1/4. This *asymmetry* is repeatedly tested.
>
> 5. **In General Average numericals, forgetting to add the *expenditure* (port of refuge, salvage) to the sacrifice (–1 to –2 marks):** Always show: $\text{GAL} = \text{Sacrifice} + \text{Allowable Expenditure}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shipping Risk**

- **Shipping risk = allocation problem** of who bears the loss among owner, charterer, cargo, and insurer.
- **Marine Insurance Act 1906** is the foundational statute (esp. **ss.3, 5, 6, 55, 57, 60, 62, 64, 79**).
- **Two types of total loss** — ATL (s.57) and CTL (s.60). CTL requires **valid Notice of Abandonment** (s.62).
- **Proximate cause** (s.55) is the *dominant effective cause* — *Leyland* is the locus classicus.
- **General Average** is governed by the **York-Antwerp Rules 2016** — contribution in proportion to *values saved*.
- **Particular Average** is the assured's *own* loss under **s.64 MIA**.
- **Subrogation (s.79)** — insurer "steps into the shoes" of the assured after indemnity.
- **Insurable interest (s.5)** must exist at the time of loss; wagering policies are void (s.4).
- **Perils of the sea (s.6(2)(c))** — fortuitous, external, violent accidents of the sea.
- **Hull insurance** = ITCH 1/10/83; covers 3/4 collision; the remaining 1/4 is the P&I's job.
- **Cargo insurance** = ICC A (all-risks), ICC B, ICC C — progressively narrower cover.
- **P&I Clubs** are *mutual*, not commercial — cover liability gaps (pollution, crew, cargo, 1/4 collision, wreck removal).
- **War risk** is *excluded* from standard hull and cargo policies — covered by **Institute War & Strikes Clauses 1/10/83**.
- **General Average contribution formula:** $\text{Contribution}_i = \left( \dfrac{\text{CV}_i}{\text{TCV}} \right) \times \text{GAL}$
- **CTL cost test:** $C_{\text{recovery}} > V_{\text{recovered}}$ → claim CTL + NoA.
- **Charter parties** re-allocate risk between owner and charterer; time charter places operational risk on charterer, navigational risk on owner.
- **Bills of Lading** (Hague-Visby) define carrier's *exceptions* — Art IV(2) lists the 17 excepted perils; cargo owner insures self against these.
- **Mortgagees' Interest Insurance (MII)** protects the bank if the hull policy is voided for non-disclosure by the shipowner (uberrima fides).
- **P&I Calls** = Advance Call + Supplementary Call + Release Call.
- **Salvage** is the reward for saving maritime property from peril — distinct from GA, but both governed by Y/A 2016.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipping Market Supply and Demand Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipping Market Supply and Demand Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipping Market Supply and Demand Model** is an economic framework used in maritime economics to analyze the interaction between the **availability of seaborne transport capacity (tonnage supply)** and the **requirement for ship transport services (tonnage demand)**, in order to determine the prevailing **freight rates**, **charter party terms**, and **asset prices** (newbuilding and secondhand vessels) prevailing at any point in time.

The shipping market is a **derived demand market** because demand for shipping services is not driven by a direct consumer wish for transport itself, but by the underlying demand for the **commodities** (crude oil, iron ore, coal, grain, containers, LNG) that require movement between geographically separated production and consumption centers. Consequently, the freight market is essentially a **barometer of world trade** and global economic activity.

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the shipping market as a **four-tier interdependent market system**:

1. **The Newbuilding Market** — where demand for and supply of newly constructed vessels meets.
2. **The Secondhand Market** — where existing operational vessels are bought and sold.
3. **The Freight (Charter) Market** — where shipowners lease capacity to cargo interests.
4. **The Demolition (Scrapping) Market** — where obsolete tonnage is sold for breaking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition — Freight Rate:** The price paid per unit of cargo (e.g., USD per metric ton) or per unit of vessel capacity per unit of time (e.g., USD per day — the **Time Charter Equivalent** or **TCE rate**) for the transportation of goods by sea.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the shipping market as a **giant taxi service for the world economy**, but with three peculiar features that ordinary taxi markets do not have:

- **The vehicles (ships) take 1–3 years to build.** You cannot instantly add taxis to your fleet when demand spikes on a Monday morning. If you ordered a Capesize bulker today during a coal boom, it would only enter the water when the boom is most likely already over.
- **The vehicles can be sold internationally in seconds.** A Greek owner can sell a Panamax to a Chinese operator while the ship is mid-Pacific.
- **The vehicles can be permanently retired and sold for scrap metal.** When too many taxis exist and fares fall, owners send them to the breakers' yard in Alang (India) or Chittagong (Bangladesh) to be cut up for steel.

**Intuitive Summary:**
- When world trade expands → more goods need moving → freight rates rise → shipowners earn more → they order new ships.
- When too many new ships eventually arrive → capacity glut → freight rates crash → ships are sold cheap → old ships scrapped.
- This self-correcting loop is the **shipping cycle (boom and bust)**, and the supply–demand model is the analytical lens used to study it.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** The shipping market is a **"barometer with a lag."** Demand responds fast (weeks to months) to trade shocks, but supply responds slowly (years) because of construction lead time. This lag is the single most important feature of the model.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics Used in the Model

| Metric | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **DWT** (Deadweight Tonnage) | Total weight a ship can carry (cargo + fuel + stores + water) | Metric Tonnes (MT) |
| **GT** (Gross Tonnage) | Total enclosed volume of a ship | Volume Units (1 GT = 100 ft³) |
| **TEU** (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit) | Standard container measure | Containers |
| **BDI** (Baltic Dry Index) | Composite of Capesize, Panamax, Supramax, Handysize time-charter routes | Index points |
| **TCE** (Time Charter Equivalent) | Net daily earnings after voyage costs | USD/day |
| **Bunker Price** | Cost of marine fuel (IFO 380, VLSFO, MGO) | USD/MT |
| **NBP** (Newbuilding Price) | Contract price of a new vessel at a yard | USD Million |
| **Port Congestion Index** | Indicator of waiting time at ports | Days / % |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Demand Side of the Shipping Market

> [!NOTE]
> **Tonnage demand** is the quantity of shipping capacity (measured in ton-miles, tonne-miles, or TEU-miles) that cargo shippers are willing to hire at a given freight rate, ceteris paribus.

The demand curve in shipping is conventionally drawn as **downward-sloping** (from upper-left to lower-right) because, like any well-behaved demand curve, higher freight rates reduce the quantity of shipping services demanded and lower freight rates increase the quantity demanded.

### 2.1.1 Determinants of Shipping Demand

The demand function can be expressed structurally as:

$$D = f(W, Y, P_c, P_s, T, S, I)$$

Where:
- $W$ = World seaborne trade volume (in tonnes)
- $Y$ = World GDP / industrial production index
- $P_c$ = Commodity price level (especially for dry bulk and tankers)
- $P_s$ = Substitutes' price (e.g., pipelines for crude oil, rail for bulk)
- $T$ = Average transport distance (trade route structure)
- $S$ = Seasonal/cyclical factors
- $I$ = Inventory adjustments by shippers

### 2.1.2 Decomposition of Demand — The Ton-Mile Identity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stopford's Ton-Mile Equation** (a foundational formula in maritime economics, derived from basic supply-chain identity):

$$\text{Seaborne Demand (ton-miles)} = \text{World Trade (tonnes)} \times \text{Average Distance (miles)}$$

This identity is critical because it shows that shipping demand can rise even when trade volumes are flat — simply because the **average distance goods travel** has increased (e.g., when Brazilian iron ore replaces Australian iron ore for Chinese steel mills after a cyclone, distance rises by ~6,000 nautical miles).

### 2.1.3 Cargo Segments Drive Different Sub-Markets

The shipping market is **not a single homogeneous market**; it is segmented by cargo type, and each segment has its own supply–demand dynamics:

| Segment | Cargo Type | Demand Driver | Vessel Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crude Oil Tankers** | Crude petroleum | OPEC output, refinery margins, strategic reserves | VLCC, Suezmax, Aframax |
| **Product Tankers** | Refined products (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel) | Refinery throughput, regional imbalances | LR2, LR1, MR |
| **Dry Bulk** | Iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite | Steel production, energy generation, food | Capesize, Panamax, Supramax, Handysize |
| **Containers** | Manufactured & consumer goods | Consumer spending, retail inventories | ULCS, Post-Panamax, Panamax, Feeder |
| **LNG / LPG** | Liquefied gas | Energy transition, gas-vs-coal switching | LNG carrier, VLGC |
| **Specialized / Project** | Heavy lifts, vehicles, reefer | Infrastructure investment, auto production | Multi-purpose, RoRo, Reefer |

### 2.1.4 Demand Elasticity in Shipping

- **Price Elasticity of Demand** is generally **inelastic in the short run** ($E_p < 1$) because cargoes must be moved regardless of freight cost — oil, grain, and ore cannot wait. A 10% rise in freight rate may reduce quantity demanded by only 2–3%.
- In the **long run**, demand is more elastic ($E_p > 1$) because industrial buyers can relocate mines, switch fuels, or invest in pipelines.
- **Cross-price elasticity** between shipping and pipelines is significant for crude oil — a rise in pipeline tariff shifts demand back to tankers.

## 2.2 The Supply Side of the Shipping Market

> [!NOTE]
> **Tonnage supply** is the total carrying capacity (in DWT or TEU) of the operational world fleet, adjusted for efficiency, speed, and availability (i.e., not in port, not in repair, not laid up).

The supply function can be written as:

$$S = f(P, C, P_{nb}, R, A, \eta)$$

Where:
- $P$ = Freight rate (the price shipowners receive)
- $C$ = Operating costs (bunker, crew, port charges, insurance)
- $P_{nb}$ = Newbuilding price (cost of expanding capacity)
- $R$ = Scrap price (alternative revenue from demolition)
- $A$ = Availability factor (% of fleet actively trading)
- $\eta$ = Technical efficiency (ton-miles per DWT per day)

### 2.2.1 The Net Fleet Equation (Stopford's Identity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental supply identity of shipping economics:**

$$S_{t+1} = S_t + \text{Newbuildings}_t - \text{Scrapping}_t - \text{Losses}_t + \Delta \text{Efficiency}_t$$

Where:
- $S_{t+1}$ = World fleet size at end of period $t+1$ (in DWT)
- $S_t$ = World fleet size at end of period $t$ (in DWT)
- $\text{Newbuildings}_t$ = DWT delivered by shipyards during period $t$
- $\text{Scrapping}_t$ = DWT sold for demolition during period $t$
- $\text{Losses}_t$ = DWT removed by casualties (sinkings, total losses, condemnations)
- $\Delta \text{Efficiency}_t$ = Net effect of slow steaming, port congestion, redesigns

### 2.2.2 The Long Lead-Time Problem

Ship construction typically takes:
- **Handysize bulkers:** 12–18 months
- **Panamax / MR tankers:** 14–22 months
- **Capesize / VLCC:** 18–30 months
- **LNG carriers:** 30–40 months
- **Container megaships (24,000+ TEU):** 24–36 months

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume shipping supply can adjust instantly to demand changes. It **cannot** because of the **shipbuilding lag**. This is why the market exhibits violent cycles — overshooting is built into the system.

### 2.2.3 Scrapping Function (Hodrick's Model)

The decision to scrap a vessel is driven by the comparison between the **scrap price** ($P_s$) and the **continuation value** of operating the ship:

$$\text{Scrapping Occurs If:} \quad P_s > \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\text{TC}_t - \text{OPEX}_t}{(1+r)^t} + \text{SV}_T$$

Where:
- $\text{TC}_t$ = Time-charter earnings in year $t$
- $\text{OPEX}_t$ = Operating expenditure in year $t$
- $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital)
- $\text{SV}_T$ = Residual scrap value at the end of the vessel's economic life
- $T$ = Remaining economic life

### 2.2.4 Short-Run vs. Long-Run Supply Curves

| Feature | Short-Run Supply (SR) | Long-Run Supply (LR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time horizon** | 0–2 years | 2–30 years |
| **Capacity fixed?** | Yes — existing ships | No — new ships can be ordered |
| **Elasticity** | Highly inelastic (almost vertical) | More elastic (upward-sloping) |
| **Adjustable variables** | Speed, port rotation, ballast legs, waiting | New orders, scrapping, redesign |
| **Graph shape** | Steep, near-vertical | Upward-sloping but flatter |

### 2.2.5 Speed Adjustment — The "Slow Steaming" Margin

A crucial short-run supply adjustment: when freight rates fall, ships **reduce cruising speed** to lower bunker consumption, thereby effectively **reducing supply** in ton-miles without removing any vessel from the fleet.

The relationship between speed and bunker consumption is approximately **cubic**:

$$\text{Bunker per day} \propto V^3$$

This means reducing speed by 10% (e.g., from 24 to 21.6 knots) cuts fuel consumption by ~27% — a major lever during downturns.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the only quantitative sheet you need for exam problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Economic Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ton-Mile Demand Identity | $D = W \times \bar{d}$ | Demand = Trade × Distance | Decompose demand shifts |
| 2 | Net Fleet Supply Identity | $S_{t+1} = S_t + N_t - X_t - L_t + \Delta\eta_t$ | New fleet = Old + New − Scrapped − Lost + Δ Efficiency | Trace supply evolution |
| 3 | Speed–Bunker Cubic Law | $B \propto V^3$ | Halving speed uses 1/8 fuel | Short-run supply adjustment |
| 4 | Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_p = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ | Sensitivity of quantity to price | Predict rate sensitivity |
| 5 | Scrapper's Decision Rule | $\text{Scrap if } P_s > \text{PV}(\text{TC} - \text{OPEX})$ | Compare scrap price vs. operating | Determine exit |
| 6 | Time Charter Equivalent | $\text{TCE} = \frac{\text{Gross Revenue} - \text{Voyage Costs}}{\text{Voyage Days}}$ | Net daily earnings | Benchmark freight |
| 7 | Breakeven Freight Rate | $\text{BEFR} = \frac{\text{OPEX} + \text{FINEX} + \text{DEPEX}}{\text{Ton-miles}}$ | Minimum rate to cover cost | Floor for market |
| 8 | Market Equilibrium | $S(P^*) = D(P^*)$ | Set price where supply meets demand | Determine clearing rate |
| 9 | Newbuilding Orderbook Ratio | $\text{Orderbook}\% = \frac{\text{Ordered DWT}}{\text{Existing Fleet DWT}} \times 100$ | Forward supply pressure | Cycle indicator |
| 10 | Port Congestion Adjustment | $A_{eff} = A_{fleet} \times (1 - C_{port})$ | Effective capacity drops with congestion | Supply shock modeling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** Freight rates in USD/MT for voyage charters, USD/day for time charters, USD/TEU for containers, and USD/million for newbuilding prices.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Management

- **Shipowners** use this model to time newbuilding orders and disposals.
- **Shipyards** use demand forecasts to plan capacity and pricing.
- **Charterers** (cargo interests) use the model to hedge freight exposure.
- **Banks and P&I Clubs** use scrapping decisions to value collateral and assess risk.
- **Policy makers** (IMO, EU, US FMC) use supply–demand modeling to assess the impact of regulations (e.g., EEXI, CII) on tonnage availability.
- **Naval architects** use the speed–bunker relationship to design efficient hull forms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Equilibrium Freight Rate

We start from a generic linear demand and supply specification, which is the canonical form used in KTU numerical problems.

### Step 1: Specify the linear demand function

We assume tonnage demand is linear in freight rate:

$$Q^d = a - bP$$

Where:
- $Q^d$ = Quantity of shipping services demanded (in billion ton-miles)
- $P$ = Freight rate (in USD per ton)
- $a$ = Intercept (demand when price is zero) — a positive constant
- $b$ = Slope coefficient (positive constant measuring responsiveness)

**Reasoning:** A higher freight rate makes shipping more expensive relative to other transport modes (pipeline, rail, road), so the quantity demanded falls linearly with $P$.

### Step 2: Specify the linear short-run supply function

In the short run, the world fleet is fixed. However, owners can adjust effective supply by slow steaming or speeding up:

$$Q^s = c + dP$$

Where:
- $Q^s$ = Effective tonnage supplied (adjusted for speed changes)
- $c$ = Intercept (minimum supply even at zero price — vessels must move to next cargo)
- $d$ = Slope coefficient (positive)

**Reasoning:** A higher freight rate encourages owners to steam faster, redeploy laid-up tonnage, and reduce ballast legs, increasing effective supply.

### Step 3: Set the market-clearing condition

In equilibrium, the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied, and we obtain the **equilibrium price** $P^*$:

$$Q^d = Q^s$$

$$a - bP^* = c + dP^*$$

### Step 4: Solve algebraically for the equilibrium price

Move all $P$ terms to one side and constants to the other:

$$a - c = bP^* + dP^*$$

$$a - c = (b + d) P^*$$

$$P^* = \frac{a - c}{b + d}$$

### Step 5: Solve for the equilibrium quantity

Substitute $P^*$ back into either equation:

$$Q^* = a - b \left( \frac{a - c}{b + d} \right)$$

$$Q^* = \frac{a(b+d) - b(a-c)}{b+d}$$

$$Q^* = \frac{ab + ad - ab + bc}{b+d}$$

$$Q^* = \frac{ad + bc}{b + d}$$

### Step 6: Interpret the result

The equilibrium freight rate $P^*$ is **higher** when:
- $a$ is large (high autonomous demand — booming world trade)
- $c$ is small (low autonomous supply — many laid-up ships)

The equilibrium freight rate $P^*$ is **lower** when:
- $b$ is large (demand is very price-sensitive)
- $d$ is large (supply is very price-sensitive — fast steaming, rapid redeployment)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** When asked "What happens to the equilibrium freight rate if world GDP rises?", you must identify the demand-side shift first. An increase in GDP raises $a$, which raises $P^*$. You should state the direction and magnitude qualitatively, e.g., "Since $a$ increases by $\Delta a$, the new equilibrium rate becomes $P^{*'} = \frac{(a + \Delta a) - c}{b + d}$, which is unambiguously higher."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparative Statics: Effect of a Surge in Newbuilding Deliveries

Suppose the world fleet grows by $\Delta N$ DWT in period $t+1$ because many ships ordered during the previous boom are finally delivered.

### Step 1: Translate the delivery surge into a supply shift

The new supply function becomes:

$$Q^{s'}_{t+1} = (c + \Delta N) + dP$$

The supply curve shifts **rightward** by $\Delta N$.

### Step 2: Compute the new equilibrium

$$P^{*'}_{t+1} = \frac{a - (c + \Delta N)}{b + d} = P^*_t - \frac{\Delta N}{b + d}$$

The freight rate falls by exactly $\frac{\Delta N}{b + d}$ for every unit of new tonnage delivered.

### Step 3: Compute the new equilibrium quantity

$$Q^{*'}_{t+1} = Q^*_t + \frac{d \cdot \Delta N}{b + d}$$

The equilibrium quantity rises because the lower price stimulates demand, but the *increase in quantity is smaller* than the supply shift — the demand curve "absorbs" part of the new tonnage by lowering price.

### Step 4: Welfare analysis — Loss of Producer Surplus

The fall in $P$ from $P^*$ to $P^{*'}$ reduces producer surplus (the area between the price line and the supply curve) for shipowners. The rectangular area of revenue loss is approximately:

$$\Delta \text{PS} \approx \left( P^* - P^{*'} \right) \times \frac{Q^* + Q^{*'}}{2}$$

$$\Delta \text{PS} \approx \frac{\Delta N}{b + d} \times \frac{2Q^* + \frac{d \cdot \Delta N}{b + d}}{2}$$

This quantifies how badly a glut hurts owners.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Time-Charter Equivalent (TCE) from Voyage Data

A KTU-favorite numerical problem: given voyage revenues and costs, calculate the TCE rate.

### Step 1: State the inputs

- **Gross Freight Revenue (GFR):** $R$ USD (freight paid by charterer)
- **Voyage Costs (VC):** bunker, port charges, canal dues = $K$ USD
- **Total Voyage Days (Load + Sea + Discharge):** $D$ days
- **Ballast Days (steaming empty to load port):** $B$ days

### Step 2: Compute net revenue

$$\text{Net Revenue} = R - K$$

### Step 3: Compute total days

$$\text{Total Days} = D + B$$

### Step 4: Compute TCE

$$\text{TCE} = \frac{R - K}{D + B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit check:** TCE is always quoted in **USD per day**. If bunker was in USD/MT and consumption is in MT/day, multiply to get USD/day before applying the formula.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Supply and Demand Shocks

| Shock | Direction of $D$ | Direction of $S$ | $P^*$ Effect | $Q^*$ Effect | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **China steel output boom** | Right | None | ↑ | ↑ | Post-2008 stimulus; 2020–2021 dry bulk rally |
| **OPEC cuts oil output** | Left | None | ↓ | ↓ | 2020 Saudi–Russia price war aftermath |
| **Mass newbuilding deliveries** | None | Right | ↓ | ↑ | 2015–2016 Capesize glut |
| **Tight environmental rules (EEXI)** | None | Left (slow steaming forced) | ↑ | ↓ | 2023 IMO regulations |
| **Port congestion** | None | Left (effective supply falls) | ↑ | ↓ | 2021 LA/Long Beach container crisis |
| **Recession (2008 GFC)** | Left | None initially, then Right (delayed) | ↓ then ↓ | ↓ | Prolonged dry bulk slump |
| **Pandemic consumer surge** | Right | None (capacity absorbed) | ↑ | ↑ | 2021–2022 container boom |
| **Bunker price spike** | None | Left (slow steaming) | ↑ | ↓ | 2022 Russia–Ukraine oil shock |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** The demand for capesize tonnage in a region is $Q^d = 500 - 2P$ (million ton-miles), and short-run supply is $Q^s = 100 + 1.5P$. Find the equilibrium freight rate, quantity, consumer surplus, producer surplus, and the new equilibrium if demand rises by 60 million ton-miles.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Set $Q^d = Q^s$:

$$500 - 2P = 100 + 1.5P$$

$$400 = 3.5P$$

$$P^* = \frac{400}{3.5} = 114.29 \text{ USD/ton}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back to find $Q^*$:

$$Q^* = 500 - 2(114.29) = 500 - 228.57 = 271.43 \text{ million ton-miles}$$

**Step 3:** Consumer Surplus (area of triangle above price, below demand intercept):

The demand intercept (where $Q=0$) is $P_{max} = 250$ (set $0 = 500 - 2P \Rightarrow P = 250$).

$$\text{CS} = \frac{1}{2} \times Q^* \times (P_{max} - P^*)$$

$$\text{CS} = \frac{1}{2} \times 271.43 \times (250 - 114.29)$$

$$\text{CS} = \frac{1}{2} \times 271.43 \times 135.71$$

$$\text{CS} \approx 18,420 \text{ USD·thousand ton-miles}$$

**Step 4:** Producer Surplus (area above supply, below price):

The supply intercept is $P_{min} = -66.67$, but economically meaningful supply starts at $P = 0$, which gives $Q^s = 100$. Use $P = 0$ as base.

$$\text{PS} = \frac{1}{2} \times (Q^* - 100) \times (P^* - 0)$$

$$\text{PS} = \frac{1}{2} \times (271.43 - 100) \times 114.29$$

$$\text{PS} = \frac{1}{2} \times 171.43 \times 114.29$$

$$\text{PS} \approx 9,795 \text{ USD·thousand ton-miles}$$

**Step 5:** New equilibrium after demand increase of 60:

New demand: $Q^{d'} = 560 - 2P$

$$560 - 2P = 100 + 1.5P$$

$$460 = 3.5P$$

$$P^{*'} = 131.43 \text{ USD/ton}$$

$$Q^{*'} = 560 - 2(131.43) = 297.14 \text{ million ton-miles}$$

**Conclusion:** Demand rise raises freight from **114.29 → 131.43** (+15%) and quantity from **271.43 → 297.14** (+9.5%). The supply curve "soaks up" part of the demand increase through faster steaming.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Shipping Market Supply–Demand Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[World Trade and Economic Activity] --> B[Shipping Demand D]
    C[World Shipyard Capacity] --> D[Shipping Supply S]
    E[World Fleet Stock] --> D
    F[Newbuilding Orders NB] --> D
    G[Demolition Scrapping SC] --> D
    H[Bunker Fuel Costs] --> D
    I[Port Congestion] --> D
    B --> J[Equilibrium Freight Rate P star]
    D --> J
    J --> K[Charter Parties and Contracts]
    J --> L[Newbuilding Prices]
    J --> M[Secondhand Prices]
    J --> N[Scrap Prices]
    K --> O[Shipowner Revenue]
    O --> P[Reinvestment Decision]
    P --> F
    P --> G
    L --> Q[Shipyard Orderbook]
    Q --> F
    M --> R[Asset Valuation by Banks]
    R --> S[Financing Decisions]
    S --> F
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture:**

- The left side captures **demand generators** (world trade, GDP, commodity cycles).
- The center captures the **market mechanism** (intersection of S and D produces the equilibrium freight rate).
- The right side captures the **feedback loops** — the equilibrium freight rate propagates to all four sub-markets (charter, newbuilding, secondhand, scrap) and ultimately loops back into supply decisions.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Four Interlinked Shipping Sub-Markets

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SubMarket1[NEWBUILDING MARKET]
        NB1[Shipyards] --> NBP[Newbuilding Price]
        NBP --> NB2[Shipowners place orders]
    end
    subgraph SubMarket2[SECONDHAND MARKET]
        SHP1[Sellers withdraw vessels] --> SHP2[Secondhand Price]
        SHP2 --> SHP3[Buyers acquire existing tonnage]
    end
    subgraph SubMarket3[FREIGHT MARKET]
        FR1[Shipowners offer capacity] --> FR2[Freight Rate]
        FR2 --> FR3[Charterers book cargo]
    end
    subgraph SubMarket4[DEMOLITION MARKET]
        DM1[Owners decide to scrap] --> DM2[Scrap Price per LDT]
        DM2 --> DM3[Breakers in South Asia]
    end
    FR3 --> NB2
    FR3 --> SHP1
    FR3 --> DM1
    FR2 --> NBP
    FR2 --> SHP2
    FR2 --> DM1
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **Freight Market** is the central pivot.
- High freight rates → owners invest → order new ships, buy secondhand, defer scrapping.
- Low freight rates → owners withdraw → cancel orders, sell ships, send to scrapyards.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Shipping Cycle (Boom-Bust Dynamics)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Upcycle Trigger<br/>Demand rises, fleet tight] --> P2[Phase 2: Freight Rates Soar<br/>Profits spike]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: New Orders Flood Yards<br/>Orderbook balloons]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Slippages and Delays<br/>Short-term squeeze continues]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Mass Deliveries Arrive<br/>2-3 years after peak]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Tonnage Glut<br/>Freight rates crash]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Scrapping and Cancellations<br/>Supply withdrawn]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 8: Market Bottom<br/>Cycle restarts]
    P8 --> P1
```

**Interpretation:**

- The cycle typically runs **7–10 years** end-to-end.
- The lag between Phases 3 and 5 (orders to deliveries) is the **structural source of cyclicality** in shipping.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — The Speed–Bunker Adjustment Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Falling Freight Rate] --> B[Owner Reduces Service Speed]
    B --> C[Bunker Consumption Falls Cubically V cubed]
    C --> D[Daily OPEX Falls]
    D --> E[Net TCE Improves at Lower Speed]
    E --> F[Effective Supply Contracts in Ton-Miles]
    F --> G[Market Re-equilibrates at Lower Q and Higher P]
    G --> H[Owner Restores Speed if Rates Recover]
```

**Interpretation:**

- Slow steaming is the **most important short-run supply adjustment** in shipping.
- A 1-knot speed reduction can save 5–8% of fuel on a typical tanker.
