# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"Define the shipping market supply–demand model and explain why shipping is regarded as a derived demand market."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The shipping market supply–demand model is an economic framework that analyzes the **interaction between tonnage supply** (the available carrying capacity of the world fleet measured in DWT or TEU) and **tonnage demand** (the requirement for sea transport services arising from world trade in commodities), with the equilibrium condition determining the prevailing **freight rate** and the prices in the related newbuilding, secondhand, and demolition markets.

Shipping is a **derived demand market** because shipowners do not sell transport as an end product; they sell the **movement of goods** that are themselves demanded by final consumers. The demand for tanker tonnage, for instance, is not driven by a wish to transport oil per se, but by the downstream demand for petroleum products (gasoline, diesel, aviation fuel) by households and industries. Hence, fluctuations in world GDP, industrial production, and consumer spending transmit through commodity demand to shipping demand, making the freight market a **barometer of world trade** rather than an independent consumption market. (3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"List any four determinants of tonnage supply in the world shipping market and explain how the long shipbuilding lead time affects market stability."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

Four principal determinants of tonnage supply are:

1. **Newbuilding deliveries** — DWT added by shipyards (depends on orderbook, yard capacity, and slippage rates).
2. **Scrapping and demolition** — DWT withdrawn to breakers' yards (depends on scrap steel price, vessel age, and operating economics).
3. **Casualties and losses** — DWT removed by sinkings, total losses, or prolonged off-hire.
4. **Productivity adjustments** — speed changes, port congestion, and operational efficiency (e.g., slow steaming reduces effective supply in ton-miles).

**Effect of long shipbuilding lead time (2–3 years for most vessel types) on market stability:**

Because ships ordered during a boom take years to deliver, the supply response is **inherently lagged**. This produces the following instability: when freight rates rise, owners flood yards with orders, but these ships arrive only **after** the demand peak has often passed, creating a **tonnage glut** and rate collapse. Conversely, during a slump, owners cancel orders, but the existing fleet cannot be instantly reduced. The result is a self-reinforcing **boom-bust cycle** in which the market persistently **overshoots equilibrium** in both directions. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Adapted)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **"With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the short-run and long-run supply curves in the shipping market. Why is the short-run supply curve steeper than the long-run supply curve?"**

#### Model Answer (7 marks):

**Short-Run Supply (SR) Curve:**
- The short run in shipping is typically defined as a horizon of **0–2 years**, during which the **physical fleet size is fixed** (vessels already in the water cannot be quickly added or removed).
- However, owners can adjust **effective supply** by varying service speed, ballast routing, port calls, and waiting time. As freight rates rise, owners steam faster and redeploy laid-up vessels, increasing ton-miles supplied.
- The SR supply curve is therefore **upward-sloping but very steep** — almost vertical — because the only adjustment margin is operational, not capacity.

**Long-Run Supply (LR) Curve:**
- The long run (2+ years) allows the **fleet size itself to change** through newbuilding orders, scrapping, and sale-purchase transactions.
- The LR supply curve is **flatter (more elastic)** because a higher freight rate triggers substantial new orders, and a lower rate triggers scrapping and lay-up.
- The LR curve is anchored by the **cost of newbuilding** and the **scrap alternative value** — the long-run price band within which owners can profitably supply capacity.

**Why SR is steeper than LR:**
- In the SR, capital stock is fixed by history; only operational levers exist. In the LR, capital stock itself becomes variable, and the supply elasticity is enhanced by the **time to build** and **time to scrap** channels.
- Mathematically, if we approximate the SR supply elasticity as $\varepsilon_{SR} \approx 0.1$ and the LR elasticity as $\varepsilon_{LR} \approx 0.6$, the ratio confirms that LR supply is roughly **6 times more elastic** than SR supply.

(Diagrams should show a steep near-vertical SR curve and a flatter upward-sloping LR curve, with the LR intersecting the demand curve at the long-run equilibrium rate and the SR intersecting at the short-run rate, with a shaded area showing the "wedge" caused by supply rigidity.) (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> **"Discuss the role of the four sub-markets (newbuilding, secondhand, freight, and demolition) in the shipping supply–demand model. Use an example of a freight rate upswing to illustrate your answer."**

#### Model Answer (7 marks):

The shipping market is best understood as **four interlocking sub-markets**, each transmitting price signals and reallocating tonnage:

1. **Freight Market:** This is the **primary market** where the supply–demand model is directly applied. It sets the **spot freight rate** (e.g., the Baltic Capesize Index or the VLCC TD3 route) and the **time-charter rate** for various durations. Demand shocks (e.g., a Chinese steel boom) immediately raise freight rates.

2. **Newbuilding Market:** This is the **forward supply channel**. A sustained freight rate upswing raises expected future returns, prompting owners to place new orders at shipyards. The newbuilding price (typically quoted in USD million) rises, and the **orderbook** (in DWT) expands. The newbuilding market is therefore a **leading indicator** of future supply.

3. **Secondhand Market:** This is the **immediate supply channel**. When freight rates rise, owners prefer to buy existing ships to deploy them quickly (delivery from a yard takes 2+ years, but a secondhand vessel can be delivered in 30–60 days). Secondhand prices therefore rise **faster and more sharply** than newbuilding prices during a boom.

4. **Demolition (Scrapping) Market:** This is the **supply-withdrawal channel**. During upswing, owners defer scrapping because operating earnings exceed scrap value, and old ships stay in service. Scrap prices may also rise in absolute terms (due to steel demand) but the **flow** of tonnage to breakers falls.

**Illustrative example — 2020–2021 Capesize Upswing:**
- **Trigger:** Chinese post-pandemic stimulus drove iron ore imports to record levels.
- **Freight:** The Baltic Capesize 5TC (5-time-charter average) rose from **below USD 5,000/day** in mid-2020 to a peak of **over USD 80,000/day** in October 2021.
- **Secondhand:** Capesize resale prices roughly **doubled** in 12 months.
- **Newbuilding:** Capesize newbuilding contract prices rose from ~USD 50 million to over USD 65 million, and the orderbook expanded.
- **Demolition:** Capesize scrapping fell to near-zero as owners kept every available vessel in service.

This example demonstrates how a single demand shock propagates through all four sub-markets, each playing a distinct role in re-equilibrating supply. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **"Using the demand function $Q^d = 600 - 3P$ and the supply function $Q^s = 200 + 2P$ for a regional shipping market, calculate the equilibrium freight rate and quantity. Also compute the consumer surplus and producer surplus at equilibrium."**

#### Model Answer (7 marks):

**Step 1: Set up the equilibrium condition**

$$Q^d = Q^s$$

$$600 - 3P = 200 + 2P$$

**Step 2: Solve for equilibrium price $P^*$**

$$600 - 200 = 3P + 2P$$

$$400 = 5P$$

$$P^* = \frac{400}{5} = 80 \text{ USD per ton}$$

**Step 3: Solve for equilibrium quantity $Q^*$**

Substitute $P^* = 80$ into the demand function:

$$Q^* = 600 - 3(80) = 600 - 240 = 360 \text{ million ton-miles}$$

Verify with supply:

$$Q^s = 200 + 2(80) = 200 + 160 = 360 \text{ million ton-miles} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute Consumer Surplus (CS)**

The demand curve intercept (where $Q = 0$) is found by setting $600 - 3P = 0 \Rightarrow P = 200$.

$$\text{CS} = \frac{1}{2} \times Q^* \times (P_{max} - P^*)$$

$$\text{CS} = \frac{1}{2} \times 360 \times (200 - 80) = \frac{1}{2} \times 360 \times 120 = 21,600 \text{ USD·thousand ton-miles}$$

**Step 5: Compute Producer Surplus (PS)**

The supply curve intercept (where $Q = 0$) is $P = -100$, which is not economically meaningful. The minimum economically viable price is $P = 0$, at which $Q^s = 200$.

$$\text{PS} = \frac{1}{2} \times (Q^* - 200) \times (P^* - 0)$$

$$\text{PS} = \frac{1}{2} \times (360 - 200) \times 80 = \frac{1}{2} \times 160 \times 80 = 6,400 \text{ USD·thousand ton-miles}$$

**Summary:**

| Variable | Value |
|---|---|
| Equilibrium freight rate $P^*$ | **USD 80/ton** |
| Equilibrium quantity $Q^*$ | **360 million ton-miles** |
| Consumer Surplus | **21,600** units |
| Producer Surplus | **6,400** units |
| Total Welfare | **28,000** units |

(7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> **"Suppose world trade in the above region grows by 20% and the average haul distance increases by 10%. Explain qualitatively how this affects the equilibrium freight rate and quantity. How would your answer change if a major new shipyard delivered 50 million ton-miles of new tonnage in the same period?"**

#### Model Answer (7 marks):

**Effect of 20% Trade Growth + 10% Distance Increase on Demand:**

Recall the ton-mile identity: $D = W \times \bar{d}$.

A 20% increase in trade volume and a 10% increase in average distance would combine multiplicatively:

$$D' = 1.20W \times 1.10\bar{d} = 1.32 \, D$$

Demand would rise by **32%**, shifting the demand curve rightward. The new demand function becomes:

$$Q^{d'} = 1.32 \times (600 - 3P) = 792 - 3.96P$$

**New equilibrium (without new tonnage):**

$$792 - 3.96P = 200 + 2P$$

$$592 = 5.96P$$

$$P^{*'} = 99.33 \text{ USD/ton}$$

$$Q^{*'} = 792 - 3.96(99.33) = 792 - 393.3 = 398.7 \text{ million ton-miles}$$

Both freight rate and quantity rise (rate from 80 → 99.33, a +24% increase; quantity from 360 → 398.7, a +10.7% increase).

**Effect of 50 Million Ton-Miles of New Tonnage Delivery:**

This shifts the supply curve rightward by 50 units:

$$Q^{s'} = (200 + 50) + 2P = 250 + 2P$$

**Combined new equilibrium:**

$$792 - 3.96P = 250 + 2P$$

$$542 = 5.96P$$

$$P^{*''} = 90.94 \text{ USD/ton}$$

$$Q^{*''} = 792 - 3.96(90.94) = 792 - 360.1 = 431.9 \text{ million ton-miles}$$

**Comparison of Three Scenarios:**

| Scenario | $P^*$ (USD/ton) | $Q^*$ (MTM) |
|---|---|---|
| Original equilibrium | 80.00 | 360.0 |
| After demand surge only | 99.33 | 398.7 |
| After demand surge + new tonnage | **90.94** | **431.9** |

**Interpretation:** The new tonnage partially **offsets** the freight rate rise caused by demand growth. The 32% demand shock would have raised rates by ~24%, but the simultaneous 25% supply expansion caps the rate increase to ~14% while boosting quantity to a record high. This illustrates the central insight of the shipping market model: **freight rates are determined by the *net* effect of demand and supply shifts, not by either alone.** (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "demand for shipping" with "demand for ships"** — they are different. Shipping demand is for transport services (freight market); demand for ships is in the newbuilding/secondhand markets.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** in numerical answers — always write USD/ton, USD/day, or million ton-miles explicitly.
> 3. **Drawing the demand curve upward-sloping** — this is the #1 blunder. Demand slopes **downward**; supply slopes **upward** (in the standard model).
> 4. **Ignoring the lag** — saying "supply will rise to meet demand" without acknowledging the 2–3 year shipbuilding lag loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Miscalculating PS** by using the wrong intercept — never use a negative intercept; use $P = 0$ as the baseline for producer surplus in this context.
> 6. **Failing to comment on real-world events** in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners reward application to current market conditions (e.g., Red Sea crisis 2024, IMO 2030, BDI movements).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Shipping Market Supply and Demand Model**

### Core Conceptual Points

- The shipping market is a **derived demand market** driven by world trade in commodities.
- It consists of **four interlocking sub-markets**: newbuilding, secondhand, freight, and demolition.
- **Demand** is downward-sloping; **supply** is upward-sloping.
- **Short-run supply is inelastic** (steep); **long-run supply is more elastic** (flatter).
- The **shipbuilding lag of 2–3 years** is the structural source of boom-bust cycles.

### Key Identities to Memorize

- **Ton-Mile Demand:** $D = W \times \bar{d}$
- **Net Fleet Supply:** $S_{t+1} = S_t + N_t - X_t - L_t + \Delta\eta_t$
- **Speed–Bunker Cubic Law:** $B \propto V^3$
- **Equilibrium Condition:** $Q^d(P^*) = Q^s(P^*)$
- **TCE Formula:** $\text{TCE} = (R - K) / (D + B)$

### High-Yield Definitions

- **Freight Rate** — price per unit of transport service.
- **Time Charter Equivalent (TCE)** — net daily earnings after voyage costs.
- **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** — total carrying capacity including cargo, fuel, stores, water.
- **Orderbook** — tonnage on order at shipyards, expressed as % of fleet.
- **Slow Steaming** — speed reduction to lower fuel costs and effectively contract supply.

### Exam Strategy

- For 3-mark questions: define + give one real-world example.
- For 7-mark sub-parts: formula + diagram + 2–3 lines of interpretation.
- For full 14-mark answers: structure as **definition → diagram → derivation → application → conclusion**.
- Always cite **current events** (Red Sea crisis, IMO 2030, container rates) to demonstrate applied knowledge.

### Common Numerical Setup

- Linear demand: $Q^d = a - bP$
- Linear supply: $Q^s = c + dP$
- Equilibrium: $P^* = (a - c)/(b + d)$, $Q^* = (ad + bc)/(b + d)$
- Welfare: $CS = \frac{1}{2}Q^*(P_{max} - P^*)$, $PS = \frac{1}{2}(Q^* - c')(P^* - P_{min})$
- Shift analysis: change in $a$ → demand shift; change in $c$ → supply shift.

### One-Sentence Summary

> *The shipping market clears where tonnage supply — governed by a slowly adjusting world fleet — meets tonnage demand, a derived demand driven by world trade; the resulting freight rate propagates through four interlocking sub-markets, producing the boom-bust cycle that defines maritime economics.*

---

topics.title: The Four Shipping Markets - Freight Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Four Shipping Markets - Freight Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Four Shipping Markets — Context Setting

The global shipping industry is broadly structured around **four inter-linked markets** that govern the lifecycle, movement, and economic value of a vessel:

1. **The Freight Market** (the market for shipping services — the focus of this module)
2. **The Sale and Purchase (S&P) Market** (the second-hand ship market)
3. **The Shipbuilding Market** (the market for newbuild orders)
4. **The Demolition / Scrap Market** (the recycling market for end-of-life tonnage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Module 4, students must be able to *differentiate the four shipping markets* and *analyse the operational mechanics of the freight market* in relation to charter parties, freight indices, and voyage economics.

### 1.2 The Freight Market — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):** The **Freight Market** is the segment of the maritime commercial environment in which the *transportation of cargoes by sea* is traded between two principal counter-parties — the **Shipowner (Supplier of tonnage)** and the **Charterer (Buyer of tonnage)** — at a negotiated **Freight Rate**, using standardized contracts called **Charter Parties**.

In simple terms, the freight market is the **"stock exchange of sea transport."** It is *not* a physical location, but a global, decentralized, digital marketplace where the commodity being traded is **"tonnage-space"** (the capacity to move cargo).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Shipping Uber"

Imagine you own a fleet of 100 trucks (shipowner), and a large manufacturer needs 5,000 tons of wheat moved from Ukraine to India every month (charterer). You do not sell the truck — you **sell the service of moving the goods**. The price you agree upon is the **freight**, the rate is the **freight rate**, and the legal contract you sign is the **Charter Party**. If 500 trucks show up in the market next month (more supply), your freight rate falls. If a war breaks out and everyone needs trucks urgently (more demand), your freight rate spikes. This dynamic, in real-time across 60,000+ merchant vessels, is the **freight market**.

### 1.4 Core Participants in the Freight Market

| Counter-party | Role | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Shipowner / Disponent Owner** | Supplier of vessel | Maximize TCE earnings, minimize ballast days |
| **Charterer** | Buyer of tonnage | Secure reliable capacity at lowest freight |
| **Shipbroker** | Intermediary | Match principals, advise on market levels |
| **P&I Club** | Liability insurer | Cover 3rd party liabilities |
| **H&M Underwriter** | Hull & Machinery insurer | Cover vessel loss/damage |
| **The Baltic Exchange** | Information provider | Publish benchmark indices (BDI, BCI, etc.) |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in the Freight Market (KTU Highlight)

- **TCE** — *Time Charter Equivalent* (USD/day): the **single most important KPI** in shipping.
- **WS** — *Worldscale*: a percentage-based freight rate index used in the tanker and dry-bulk voyage charter trades.
- **BDI** — *Baltic Dry Index*: a composite benchmark of dry-bulk freight rates.
- **Voyage P&L** — Profit and Loss statement of a single voyage.
- **Demurrage / Despatch** — Penalties/rewards for loading/discharging time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of the Freight Market

The freight market operates on the **law of supply and demand** but with one critical maritime distortion: **tonnage is a near-indestructible, long-life asset** (a Capesize bulker has a 25-year life). Supply cannot react quickly to demand shocks, which causes extreme **volatility** — the cardinal feature of freight rates.

**The Demand Side (Cargo Flows) is driven by:**
- Global GDP growth (1% GDP growth ≈ 1.4% seaborne trade growth)
- Commodity cycles (iron ore, coal, grain, crude oil, LNG)
- Seasonal effects (grain harvests, winter oil demand)
- Geopolitical disruptions (Suez Canal closures, sanctions)
- Inventory levels at major economies

**The Supply Side (Tonnage) is driven by:**
- Orderbook at shipyards (future supply)
- Deliveries from shipyards
- Scrapping / demolition
- Slow steaming (reduces effective supply)
- Port congestion (absorbs supply)

### 2.2 Market Structure — Spot vs. Period

The freight market is **bifurcated** into two segments:

1. **Spot Market** — Single-voyage charters, priced in USD per metric ton or via Worldscale (WS). Highly volatile.
2. **Period Market** — Time charters (TC) and bareboat charters (BBC) for months/years. Less volatile, forms the "base load" of revenue.

### 2.3 The Baltic Exchange & Benchmark Indices

The **Baltic Exchange** (London, founded 1744) is the global centre for maritime freight information. It publishes daily indices that are treated as **the LIBOR/SOFR of shipping**:

- **BDI** = Baltic Dry Index (composite of Capesize, Panamax, Supramax, Handysize)
- **BCI** = Baltic Capesize Index
- **BPI** = Baltic Panamax Index
- **BSI** = Baltic Supramax Index
- **BHSI** = Baltic Handysize Index
- **BDTI** = Baltic Dirty Tanker Index
- **BCTI** = Baltic Clean Tanker Index

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **exam-essential formulas** the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to reproduce from memory. Any numerical problem in the ESE will be built around one or more of these.

$$
\text{Worldscale Freight} = \text{WS Points} \times \frac{\text{Standard Freight (Base Year)}}{100}
$$

$$
\text{Gross Freight Revenue} = \text{Cargo Tonnage} \times \text{Freight Rate per Ton}
$$

$$
\text{Net Voyage Revenue} = \text{Gross Freight} - \text{Voyage Costs (Bunker + Port + Canal)}
$$

$$
\text{TCE (USD per day)} = \frac{\text{Net Voyage Revenue}}{\text{Total Voyage Days (incl. ballast)}}
$$

$$
\text{Bunker Cost} = \text{IFO Consumption (MT/day)} \times \text{Bunker Price (USD/MT)} \times \text{Voyage Days}
$$

$$
\text{Demurrage} = \text{Laytime Exceeded (days)} \times \text{Demurrage Rate (USD/day)}
$$

$$
\text{Despatch} = \text{Laytime Saved (days)} \times \frac{\text{Demurrage Rate}}{2}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{g}$ | Gross Freight | USD |
| $F_{n}$ | Net Freight (after voyage costs) | USD |
| $W$ | Worldscale points | dimensionless |
| $T_{v}$ | Total voyage duration | days |
| $C_{b}$ | Bunker cost | USD |
| $R_{d}$ | Demurrage rate | USD/day |
| $\text{BDI}$ | Baltic Dry Index | index points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Markdown Tables:** Mathematical "absolute-value" or "given" bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking table syntax. KTU scripts often lose marks when students write the formula in pure prose.

### 2.5 Why the Freight Market Matters (Real-World Utility)

- **For Shipowners:** Determines the entire revenue base. A 1,000 USD/day swing in TCE on a Panamax = ~365,000 USD/year per vessel.
- **For Charterers:** Determines landed cost of goods. A grain trader in Geneva sets his CIF price based on the *current WS 100* rate from the US Gulf.
- **For Banks (KFW, DNB):** Freight rates determine whether a 75 million USD loan can be serviced.
- **For NBF (Naval Architecture) & Marine Engineers:** Higher freight → higher asset values → more newbuild orders → more design & construction work.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The TCE (Time Charter Equivalent) Formula

**Why this matters:** TCE is the only metric that allows a shipowner to compare a *one-voyage spot charter* with a *multi-month time charter* on an apples-to-apples daily basis. The KTU examiner frequently sets a 7-mark numerical on TCE.

**Given:**
- Voyage: Rotterdam → Singapore → Rotterdam (typical VLCC ballast/load/ballast triangle)
- Cargo: 280,000 MT crude
- Freight rate: WS 60 (Worldscale 60 points)
- Standard freight (base year) for this route: USD 12.50/MT
- Bunker consumption laden: 90 MT/day; ballast: 75 MT/day
- Bunker price: USD 620/MT
- Port costs laden: USD 250,000; ballast: USD 180,000
- Canal dues: USD 0
- Total laden days: 22; total ballast days: 18

**Step 1 — Calculate Gross Freight Revenue**

$$
F_{g} = 280{,}000 \text{ MT} \times \left(\frac{60}{100} \times 12.50\right) \text{ USD/MT}
$$

$$
F_{g} = 280{,}000 \times 7.50 = 2{,}100{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Bunker Cost Laden**

$$
C_{b,\text{laden}} = 90 \text{ MT/day} \times 620 \text{ USD/MT} \times 22 \text{ days} = 1{,}227{,}600 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Bunker Cost Ballast**

$$
C_{b,\text{ballast}} = 75 \text{ MT/day} \times 620 \text{ USD/MT} \times 18 \text{ days} = 837{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Bunker Cost**

$$
C_{b} = 1{,}227{,}600 + 837{,}000 = 2{,}064{,}600 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Port Costs**

$$
C_{p} = 250{,}000 + 180{,}000 = 430{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Voyage Costs**

$$
C_{v} = C_{b} + C_{p} = 2{,}064{,}600 + 430{,}000 = 2{,}494{,}600 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 7 — Net Voyage Revenue**

$$
F_{n} = F_{g} - C_{v} = 2{,}100{,}000 - 2{,}494{,}600 = -394{,}600 \text{ USD}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The voyage produces a **loss of USD 394,600**. This is the real-world "blood bath" of shipping: a freight rate of WS 60 on a tanker is *below* operating cost.

**Step 8 — Total Voyage Days**

$$
T_{v} = 22 + 18 = 40 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 9 — Calculate TCE**

$$
\text{TCE} = \frac{F_{n}}{T_{v}} = \frac{-394{,}600}{40} = -9{,}865 \text{ USD/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **negative TCE** indicates the spot voyage is uneconomic. The KTU examiner will award full marks as long as you show the sign convention and clearly state *"loss-making voyage."*

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Demurrage / Despatch Calculation

**Given:** Allowed laytime = 72 hours; Demurrage rate = USD 25,000/day; Despatch = ½ demurrage.

**Case A — Charterer used 80 hours (8 hours over):**

$$
\text{Demurrage} = \frac{8}{24} \times 25{,}000 = 8{,}333.33 \text{ USD}
$$

**Case B — Charterer used 60 hours (12 hours saved):**

$$
\text{Despatch} = \frac{12}{24} \times \frac{25{,}000}{2} = 6{,}250 \text{ USD}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Freight Market Participants vs. Risk Exposure

| Risk Category | Shipowner (Voyage) | Shipowner (Time Charter) | Charterer (Voyage) | Charterer (Time Charter) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bunker price risk** | Carrier | Charterer | Carrier | Charterer |
| **Port congestion risk** | Carrier | Charterer | Carrier | Charterer |
| **Freight rate risk** | Carrier | Charterer | Carrier | Charterer |
| **Hull damage risk** | Owner | Owner | n/a | n/a |
| **Cargo damage risk** | Carrier | Charterer | n/a | Charterer |
| **Off-hire risk** | Owner (loss) | Owner (loss) | n/a | Charterer (loss of ship) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip (KTU):** When asked *"Who bears the bunker price risk under a Time Charter?"* — the answer is **the Charterer**, because under a TC the owner hands over commercial operation to the charterer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Architecture of the Freight Market

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Global Seaborne Trade Demand] --> B1[Charterer Cargo Requirement]
    A2[World Tonnage Supply] --> B2[Shipowner Disponent Capacity]
    B1 --> C1{Baltic Exchange Information Hub}
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Spot Market - Voyage Charter]
    C1 --> D2[Period Market - Time Charter]
    C1 --> D3[Bareboat Charter]
    C1 --> D4[Contract of Affreightment]
    D1 --> E1[Charter Party GENCON]
    D2 --> E2[Charter Party NYPE 2015]
    D3 --> E3[Charter Party SALEFORM]
    D4 --> E4[Voyage COA]
    E1 --> F1[Freight Rate in USD per MT or WS]
    E2 --> F2[Hire Rate in USD per Day]
    E3 --> F3[Hire Rate in USD per Day]
    E4 --> F4[Ton Mile Rate]
    F1 --> G1[Voyage P and L]
    F2 --> G2[TC Income Statement]
    F3 --> G3[Owner Cash Flow]
    F4 --> G4[Charterer Cost Calculation]
    G1 --> H1[TCE Time Charter Equivalent]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1((Market Equilibrium))
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Determinants of Freight Rate Movement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[GDP Growth] --> B1[Seaborne Demand Up]
    A2[Commodity Prices] --> B1
    A3[War Sanctions] --> B1
    A4[Seasonal Harvests] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Demand Pressure]
    C1 --> D1[Freight Rate UP]
    A5[Orderbook High] --> B2[Tonnage Supply Up]
    A6[Slow Steaming] --> B3[Tonnage Supply Down]
    A7[Scrapping High] --> B3
    A8[Port Congestion] --> B3
    B2 --> C2[Supply Pressure]
    B3 --> C4[Supply Squeeze]
    C2 --> D2[Freight Rate DOWN]
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[BDI Index]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Charter Party Negotiation]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Voyage Charter vs. Time Charter Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1{What is the charterers primary need?} --> B1[One specific cargo moved one time]
    A1 --> B2[Vessel needed for several months]
    A1 --> B3[Vessel needed for one or more years with full control]
    B1 --> C1[Voyage Charter - GENCON 1994]
    B2 --> C2[Time Charter - NYPE 2015 or BIMCO STB 2001]
    B3 --> C3[Bareboat Charter - SALEFORM 2012]
    C1 --> D1[Freight paid per MT or WS]
    C2 --> D2[Hire paid per day - owner pays crew and stores]
    C3 --> D3[Hire paid per day - charterer pays everything except bare hull]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**

**Q1. Define the term "Freight Market" and list its two principal participants.**
*Model Answer:*
The **Freight Market** is the segment of the maritime commercial environment where the *transportation service of cargo by sea* is traded between the **Shipowner** (supplier of vessel tonnage) and the **Charterer** (buyer of that tonnage) at a negotiated **Freight Rate**, formalised in a **Charter Party**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a Voyage Charter and a Time Charter on three points.**
*Model Answer:*

| Parameter | Voyage Charter | Time Charter |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | One single voyage | A fixed period (months/years) |
| **Freight basis** | Per MT or WS | Hire per day |
| **Commercial control** | Owner | Charterer |
| **Bunker cost paid by** | Owner | Charterer |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Full Module Question Paper Style)]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Option 1*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **structure of the freight market**, the role of the **Baltic Exchange**, and discuss any **three indices** published by it. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With the help of a numerical example, derive the **TCE (Time Charter Equivalent)** of a vessel on a voyage charter. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The freight market is the global, decentralised marketplace in which *sea-transport services* are bought and sold. It is conventionally divided into:

1. **The Spot Market** – single-voyage charters, priced per cargo.
2. **The Period Market** – time charters and bareboat charters spanning months/years.

**Role of the Baltic Exchange (founded 1744, London):**

The Baltic Exchange acts as the **central information and benchmark authority** of the freight market. It does not trade ships — it publishes daily, independently-audited freight indices that the global industry treats as the **LIBOR/SOFR equivalent of shipping**. The role includes:

- **Information dissemination** — daily fixtures, indices, and route rates.
- **Standardisation** — common templates for voyage estimation and TCE reporting.
- **Self-regulation** — enforces the *Baltic Exchange Code of Conduct* on its 600+ member companies.

**Three Key Indices:**

1. **BDI (Baltic Dry Index):** A composite benchmark of dry-bulk freight across four vessel sizes (Capesize, Panamax, Supramax, Handysize). It is often treated as a *leading indicator of global economic health*. In 2008, BDI peaked at **11,793**; in 2016, it bottomed at **290** — illustrating extreme volatility.

2. **BCI (Baltic Capesize Index):** Tracks 5 standard Capesize timecharter routes (e.g., Tubarão → Qingdao). Capesize vessels carry 180,000 MT of iron ore/coal and cannot transit the Suez Canal — so BCI is a pure **deep-sea Pacific/Atlantic benchmark**.

3. **BDTI (Baltic Dirty Tanker Index):** A benchmark of spot freight rates on **13 dirty (crude) tanker routes**, each settled as a *Worldscale percentage* of a base route. It is the global trader's primary tool for valuing crude oil at sea.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given (standard KTU 2024 numerical):**
A Panamax bulk carrier of 75,000 MT deadweight is fixed on a voyage charter from Richards Bay (South Africa) to Rotterdam at a freight rate of **USD 22/MT**. Bunker consumption is 38 MT/day at USD 580/MT. Laden voyage duration = 26 days; ballast = 14 days. Port costs laden = USD 120,000; ballast = USD 85,000. Canal dues = nil.

**Step 1 — Gross Freight Revenue**

$$
F_{g} = 75{,}000 \text{ MT} \times 22 \text{ USD/MT} = 1{,}650{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Bunker Cost Laden**

$$
C_{b,\text{laden}} = 38 \times 580 \times 26 = 572{,}240 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Bunker Cost Ballast**

$$
C_{b,\text{ballast}} = 38 \times 580 \times 14 = 308{,}080 \text{ USD}
$$

> Note: Ballast bunker consumption may differ; we use the same rate per problem simplification.

**Step 4 — Total Bunker**

$$
C_{b} = 572{,}240 + 308{,}080 = 880{,}320 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Port Costs**

$$
C_{p} = 120{,}000 + 85{,}000 = 205{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Voyage Cost**

$$
C_{v} = 880{,}320 + 205{,}000 = 1{,}085{,}320 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 7 — Net Voyage Revenue**

$$
F_{n} = 1{,}650{,}000 - 1{,}085{,}320 = 564{,}680 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 8 — Total Voyage Days**

$$
T_{v} = 26 + 14 = 40 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 9 — Time Charter Equivalent**

$$
\text{TCE} = \frac{564{,}680}{40} = 14{,}117 \text{ USD/day}
$$

**Conclusion:** The voyage produces a TCE of USD 14,117/day. A KTU examiner will accept this as a *competitive result* and award full marks for the labelled step-wise derivation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Option 2*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **four major types of charter parties** used in the freight market, citing the standard BIMCO form for each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A Capesize vessel of 180,000 DWT is fixed on a time charter at **USD 22,000/day** for 90 days. The owner estimates the vessel will consume 65 MT/day of IFO at USD 580/MT plus 4 MT/day of LSMGO at USD 720/MT. Port costs of USD 250,000 are to be borne by the charterer. Calculate the **owner's net daily income** and the **charterer's all-in daily cost**. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Charter Type | Standard BIMCO Form | Freight Basis | Operational Control | Bunker Paid By |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voyage Charter** | GENCON 1994 | Per MT or WS | Owner | Owner |
| **Time Charter** | NYPE 2015 | Hire per day | Charterer | Charterer |
| **Bareboat Charter** | SALEFORM 2012 (BB One) | Hire per day | Charterer (full) | Charterer (full) |
| **Contract of Affreightment** | COACHARTER or INTERCOA 2016 | Ton-mile rate | Owner | Owner |

**Key Distinctions:**

- A **Voyage Charter** is *"hire of cargo space"* — the owner remains fully in command.
- A **Time Charter** is *"hire of a ship with crew"* — charterer directs commercial voyages, but owner keeps technical management.
- A **Bareboat Charter** is *"hire of a bare hull"* — charterer takes over *both* commercial and technical management. It is a step short of ownership.
- A **Contract of Affreightment (COA)** is *not a charter of a specific ship* but a commitment to carry a *quantity of cargo* over a series of voyages — the owner chooses which ship to nominate.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Owner's Gross Income (90 days)**

$$
I_{\text{gross}} = 22{,}000 \times 90 = 1{,}980{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Owner's Bunker Cost**

IFO per day: 65 × 580 = 37,700 USD
LSMGO per day: 4 × 720 = 2,880 USD
Total per day: 40,580 USD

$$
C_{b,\text{total}} = 40{,}580 \times 90 = 3{,}652{,}200 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Owner's Net Income (90 days)**

$$
I_{\text{net}} = 1{,}980{,}000 - 3{,}652{,}200 = -1{,}672{,}200 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Owner's Net Daily Income**

$$
I_{\text{net,day}} = \frac{-1{,}672{,}200}{90} = -18{,}580 \text{ USD/day}
$$

> The owner makes a **loss of USD 18,580/day**. This rate is far below operating cost — typical KTU examiner will award 7 marks with a *commercial commentary* on why such a TC is uneconomic.

**Step 5 — Charterer's All-in Daily Cost**

The charterer pays hire + own port costs:

$$
C_{\text{charterer,day}} = 22{,}000 + \frac{250{,}000}{90} = 22{,}000 + 2{,}778 = 24{,}778 \text{ USD/day}
$$

> The charterer's *all-in cost* is **USD 24,778/day**, although the owner is actually *subsidising* the charterer (paying more in bunker than receiving in hire). This is the classic *"negative-TCE trap"* that KTU examiners love to test.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract ballast days** from net revenue → causes wrong TCE. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up WS points with absolute freight** → WS 100 is *not* USD 100/ton; it is *the standard base-year rate*. Always convert.
> 3. **Treating a COA as a charter of a ship** → a COA is a *contract of carriage*, not a charter party. Examiner will mark this as a major conceptual error.
> 4. **Ignoring sign convention** in TCE → a negative TCE is still a *valid* numerical answer; you must explicitly state "loss-making voyage."
> 5. **Confusing BDI with BCI** → a common error. BDI is the *composite* index; BCI is *only* Capesize.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **Freight Market** is the marketplace for *sea-transport services*, not for ships themselves.
- Two principal counter-parties: **Shipowner** (supply) and **Charterer** (demand).
- The market is **bifurcated** into **Spot (Voyage)** and **Period (Time/Bareboat)** segments.
- The **Baltic Exchange** (London, 1744) is the global benchmark publisher — its indices (BDI, BCI, BPI, BSI, BDTI, BCTI) are the *LIBOR of shipping*.
- **Worldscale (WS)** is a percentage of a base-year standard freight rate; **WS 100 = standard**, **WS 50 = half standard**, etc.
- **TCE = Net Voyage Revenue ÷ Total Voyage Days** — the *single most important* daily KPI in shipping finance.
- **Demurrage** = penalty for exceeding laytime; **Despatch** = reward for saving laytime, typically **½ demurrage rate**.
- Under a **Voyage Charter**, the owner bears *all* voyage costs; under a **Time Charter**, the charterer bears bunker and port costs.
- The freight market is highly **volatile** because tonnage supply is inelastic in the short run — a 25-year asset cannot be replaced in 6 months.
- **KPI hierarchy to remember:** BDI (macro) → Vessel-class index (e.g., BCI) → TCE (company level) → Voyage P&L (single voyage).
- **Exam hot-button:** Know the standard BIMCO forms — **GENCON 1994** (voyage), **NYPE 2015** (time charter), **SALEFORM 2012** (bareboat), **INTERCOA 2016** (COA).
- Always show **sign convention** in TCE derivations — the KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark for a missing "loss-making" or "profitable" declaration.
- Freight rates are **denominated in USD** universally — the *Petrodollar* logic of shipping finance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sale and Purchase Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sale and Purchase Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Sale and Purchase (S&P) Market** in maritime commerce refers to the secondary market mechanism through which ownership of ocean-going vessels is transferred between parties — typically shipowners, investors, financial institutions, and end-users (charterers, operators, or demolition yards). It is governed principally by the **law of contract (sale of goods)**, **admiralty jurisdiction**, and standard industry forms — most notably the **Norwegian Sale Form (NSF)** and the **BIMCO Standard Shipbuilding and Sale Forms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 of *Maritime Law and Shipping Management* identifies the S&P market as a **distinct commercial ecosystem**, distinct from chartering and shipbuilding, although overlapping in legal instruments. Students must understand that the S&P transaction is a **bifurcated process**: (i) commercial negotiation and (ii) legal/title transfer.

**Key Statutory Anchors:**
- **Sale of Goods Act, 1930 (India)** — implied conditions as to title, description, and fitness.
- **Indian Contract Act, 1872** — consensus ad idem and free consent.
- **UN Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG), 1980** — applicable where parties' jurisdictions are signatory states.
- **UNIDROIT Conventions on International Interests in Mobile Equipment (Cape Town Convention, 2001)** — for ship financing and security interests.

> [!NOTE]
> The S&P market is **price-driven** and **asset-class-driven**. Unlike the chartering market (where ships are hired), the S&P market deals with the **permanent transfer of ownership** of a tangible maritime asset. Total global S&P transactions in a typical active year exceed **USD 60–80 billion** in gross asset value.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

Imagine the **used-car market**, but scaled up to **100,000 DWT tankers worth USD 50 million each**, governed by a 30-page contract, surveyed by independent engineers, and routed through escrow accounts in London or New York.

| Used-Car Market | Ship S&P Market |
|---|---|
| Seller advertises on a portal | **Sale circular / broker circulates SnP report** |
| Buyer physically inspects | **Independent surveyor's inspection (condition + valuation)** |
| Negotiation on price | **MOA negotiation with addenda** |
| Title transfer at RTO | **Delivery at a named port; flag/registry transfer** |
| Loan on hypothecation | **Maritime mortgage; deletion certificate** |

**Plain English Summary:** A ship S&P transaction is simply a very large, highly regulated, internationally syndicated version of buying a second-hand house. The "house" floats, costs more, and the paperwork can fill a filing cabinet.

> [!TIP]
> **Three Things Every KTU Student MUST Remember about the S&P Market:**
> 1. It is **negotiated bilaterally** — usually via a **shipbroker** as intermediary.
> 2. The contract is the **Memorandum of Agreement (MoA)** — almost always on a standard form.
> 3. **Title does not pass at signing** — it passes at **physical delivery** of the vessel against a **Bill of Sale** and **Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance**.

---

## 1.3 Market Participants — Who Plays the Game?

- **Sellers:** Outgoing owners, financiers (post-repossession), demolition contractors (re-selling for scrap).
- **Buyers:** Second-hand tonnage buyers, liner companies, tramp operators, financial investors, state-owned shipping corporations.
- **Intermediaries:** Independent **shipbrokers** (e.g., Clarksons, Braemar, Fearnleys, Platou).
- **Surveyors:** Independent classification / valuation surveyors (e.g., Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS, DNV).
- **Financiers:** Banks, export credit agencies, leasing companies holding maritime mortgages.
- **Legal Counsel:** Maritime law firms — typically London, New York, Singapore, Piraeus, or Oslo-based.
- **Flag Administration:** For deletion/registration formalities.

---

## 1.4 Classification of the S&P Market

The market is conventionally segmented into:

1. **Second-Hand Market** — Pre-owned operational tonnage (most active segment).
2. **New-Building Resale Market** — Transfer of a shipbuilding contract prior to delivery.
3. **Demolition / Scrap Market** — Sale for breaking (typically to South Asian yards — Alang, Chittagong, Gadani).
4. **Investment / Distress Sale Market** — Sales arising from default, foreclosure, or bankruptcy proceedings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 examiner** expects students to be able to **distinguish between a 'sale' and a 'charter'**, a 'sale' and a 'lease', and a 'sale' and a 'mortgage' — these distinctions appear regularly as 3-mark definitional questions.

---

## 1.5 The Standard Sale Forms — A Quick Survey

| Form | Year | Key Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Norwegian Sale Form (NSF)** | 1957 (rev. 2011) | Second-hand vessels (most popular globally) |
| **BIMCO Saleform 2012** | 2012 | General second-hand sale — modernized |
| **Singapore Sale Form (SSF)** | 2007 | South-East Asian transactions |
| **Japanese Sale Form** | — | Vessels out of Japan |
| **SAJ (Saleform for Japanese flag ships)** | 2013 | Japan-flag specific |
| **Demolition Form (BIMCO DEMOLISHCON 2017)** | 2017 | Sale for scrap |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do **not** confuse the **Norwegian Sale Form** with **Norwegian origin** of the ship. The form is used for **vessels of any flag**, anywhere in the world. Its name derives from its drafting lineage (Norwegian Maritime Law tradition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Ship S&P Transaction

A complete S&P transaction unfolds in **five legally distinct stages**. The KTU examiner often tests the **chronological sequence** — students frequently jumble the order of events.

### Stage 1 — **Pre-Negotiation Phase (Indications of Interest)**
- The seller (or its broker) issues a **sale circular** describing the vessel, its dimensions, year of build, flag, DWT, TEU, and asking price.
- Confidential negotiations begin; **Letters of Intent (LOI)** or **Heads of Agreement** may be exchanged but are typically **non-binding**.
- A **NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement)** is signed before sensitive operational or financial data is disclosed.

### Stage 2 — **Memorandum of Agreement (MoA) / Recap Phase**
- The terms are summarized in a **Recap (telex recap)**.
- The full **MoA** is drawn up — usually on the **Norwegian Sale Form 2011** or **BIMCO Saleform 2012**.
- **Earnest money / deposit (typically 10% of the purchase price)** is paid into an **escrow account** held by a stakeholder (often the seller's broker or a P&I club-designated stakeholder).
- A **Memorandum of Agreement becomes binding upon signature by both parties** — even before the detailed conditions are fulfilled (this is a unique maritime feature).

### Stage 3 — **Conditions Precedent (CPs) Phase**
Typical CPs include:
- Buyer's satisfactory **inspection / survey** of the vessel.
- Confirmation of **class status** (vessel maintains class without conditions/recommendations).
- **Mortgage consent** — seller's mortgagee must agree to deletion of the vessel from the registry.
- **Charter-free delivery** (or acceptance of existing charter parties).
- **Financing approval** for the buyer.
- **Export/import licences** where required.

### Stage 4 — **Inspection, Documentation & Closing Phase**
- Physical inspection by the buyer's appointed surveyor.
- Review of all **class records, trading certificates, ownership chain, mortgages**.
- **Closing meeting** — at which all documents are exchanged.

### Stage 5 — **Delivery Phase**
- **Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance (PDA)** signed at the agreed port.
- **Bill of Sale** executed and notarized/apostilled.
- **Deletion certificate** from the outgoing flag state.
- **Mortgage deletion letter** from the seller's bank.
- **Payment of the balance 90%** via telegraphic transfer.
- **New flag registration / provisional registration** for the buyer.

---

## 2.2 The Norwegian Sale Form (NSF) — Structural Breakdown

The **NSF 2011** is the **workhorse contract** of the global S&P market. The KTU examiner expects familiarity with its **Clauses 1 through 23**.

| Clause | Subject Matter |
|---|---|
| 1 | Definitions |
| 2 | **Sale and Purchase** — the operative binding clause |
| 3 | **Inspection** — buyer's right, time, place, dry-docking |
| 4 | **Earnings, Expenses, and Running Costs** — apportionment at delivery |
| 5 | **Time and Place of Delivery** |
| 6 | **Deposit** — usually 10% |
| 7 | **Purchase Price & Payment** — balance 90% on delivery |
| 8 | **Encumbrances & Mortgages** — seller's warranty of clear title |
| 9 | **Notices, etc.** — buyers' notice to seller's mortgagee |
| 10 | **Buyers' Default** — forfeiture of deposit |
| 11 | **Sellers' Default** — refund + interest OR specific performance |
| 12 | **Condition** — vessel maintained class, free of recommendations |
| 13 | **Class records** — seller's obligation to provide |
| 14 | **Trading certificates & inspections** |
| 15 | **Encumbrances, etc.** — repeated warranty |
| 16 | **Taxes** — buyer bears post-delivery taxes |
| 17 | **Force Majeure / Requisition** |
| 18 | **Taxes on Purchase Price** |
| 19 | **Arbitration** — usually London, NY, or Oslo |
| 20 | **Governing Law** — typically English Maritime Law |
| 21 | **Notices** |
| 22 | **Entire Agreement** |
| 23 | **Brokerage** — usually shared 50:50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Point:** The **Deposit Clause (Cl. 6)** in the NSF is **non-refundable** if the buyer defaults — this is **English Common Law principle** applied universally. However, in the BIMCO Saleform 2012, deposit handling is more nuanced, allowing stakeholder involvement and return in defined circumstances.

---

## 2.3 The BIMCO Saleform 2012 — Modern Alternative

The **BIMCO Saleform 2012** was introduced to **update** the aging NSF for contemporary shipping realities. Key innovations:

- **Sub-Clause 2(a)** — express binding effect upon signature.
- **Sub-Clause 7** — stakeholder-based deposit handling (safer than the NSF system).
- **Sub-Clause 10** — caps the buyer's exposure to **50% of the deposit** in case of default.
- **Sub-Clause 13** — explicit sanctions / anti-bribery compliance.
- **Sub-Clause 15** — comprehensive **Encumbrance / Title** warranties.
- **Sub-Clause 16** — **EMC (European Monetary Claims) compliance** in 2016 addenda.
- **Sanctions clause** (added 2018) — addressing OFAC, EU, and UN sanctions regimes.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the consolidated **Reference Framework** for S&P Market calculations and structural milestones. Memorize the **bolded boundary conditions** before any numerical problem.

| Element | Standard Reference | Boundary Conditions & Defaults |
|---|---|---|
| **Deposit** | **10%** of Purchase Price | Range: 5%–20% (negotiated) |
| **Balance Payment** | **90%** | Payable in USD/EUR/GBP on delivery |
| **Apportionment of Hire** | Time of delivery (Noon) | Voyage charter freight: **earned for cargo on board** |
| **Lubricating Oil** | **Buyer pays** at port price | Inventory priced separately |
| **Bunkers** | **Buyer pays** at port price | As per BDN (Bunker Delivery Note) |
| **Apportionment Date/Time** | **Noon of delivery day** | Sums apportioned pro-rata |
| **Inspection Period** | **7–14 days** post-LOI | NSF default: "immediately" |
| **Dry-Docking for Inspection** | **Buyer's cost & time** | Unless material defect found |
| **Mortgagee's Fee** | **Buyer pays** | Default: ~1% of mortgage amount |
| **Brokerage** | **Shared 1.25% + VAT each side** | 2.5% total (negotiable) |
| **Arbitration Seats** | **London / NY / Oslo / Singapore** | LMAA Rules (London) |
| **Governing Law** | **English Law (default)** | Norwegian, NY, Singapore also common |

---

## 2.5 Calculation of Apportioned Items (The Numerical Backbone)

At delivery, certain **revenue streams and costs must be apportioned** between seller and buyer. The fundamental formula:

$$
\text{Apportioned Item} = \text{Daily Rate} \times \text{Pro-Rata Fraction of Day}
$$

The **pro-rata fraction of day** is calculated as:

$$
\text{Pro-Rata Fraction} = \frac{\text{Time elapsed from previous noon to delivery moment (in hours)}}{24}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical MoA, **earnings, port charges, crew wages, and routine operating costs** are split based on a **running-on-the-clock** principle. Bunkers and lubricating oil are **physical inventories** that transfer at the **prevailing port price** — not book value.

---

## 2.6 Market Dynamics — Why Prices Move

The S&P market is a **barometer of shipping industry health**. Key drivers:

1. **Bunker prices** — affect secondhand vessel operating economics.
2. **TCE (Time Charter Equivalent) earnings** — proxy for asset profitability.
3. **Demolition steel prices** — set the **floor price** of the asset.
4. **New-building orderbook** — affects second-hand values via substitution.
5. **Financing cost / LIBOR–SOFR spreads** — drives leveraged purchases.
6. **Geopolitical events** — sanctions, war risk, port congestion.
7. **Environmental regulation** — IMO 2020 sulphur cap, EEXI, CII ratings.

> [!TIP]
> **The Price-Setting Equation (Simplified):**
> $$\boxed{P_{\text{second-hand}} \approx \frac{\text{Annualised TCE} \times \text{Years Remaining Life}}{\text{Required IRR}} + \text{Scrap Value (PV)}}$$
> This is the **discounted cash flow (DCF)**-based valuation used by every major shipowner — and is the **same logic** your examiner will expect in a Part B question.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of the S&P Framework

- **Maritime Finance:** Banks require **clean title chain** before disbursing loan against a vessel.
- **Flag State Administration:** A vessel can only be **simultaneously registered in one flag state** — hence **deletion certificate** is critical.
- **P&I Clubs:** Insurance transfers require notification and **no-claim certificates**.
- **Competition Law:** Cross-border S&P transactions may attract **merger control** scrutiny in EU, US, and increasingly in India (CCI).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Apportionment of Hire at Delivery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic 7-mark KTU Part B question type.** Practice it thoroughly.

### Problem Statement

The **M/V SEA LARK**, a Panamax bulker, is sold under a Norwegian Sale Form 2011. The **MoA provides that hire, port charges, and crew wages shall be apportioned at noon of the delivery day**. The vessel is delivered at **10:30 hrs (local)** on the agreed date. The **daily running cost** (crew wages + port charges + routine maintenance) is **USD 8,500/day**, and the vessel was on a **time charter earning USD 22,000/day**.

**Compute:**
- (a) Seller's share of daily running cost.
- (b) Seller's share of daily earnings.
- (c) Net cash transfer from buyer to seller (or vice-versa) at delivery.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the apportionment anchor point.**
The MoA specifies **"noon of the delivery day"** as the reference point. The vessel was delivered at **10:30 hrs** — *before noon*. Therefore, the seller owns the **first 10.5 hours** of the day.

**Step 2 — Convert hours to a decimal fraction of a day.**

$$
\text{Fraction of day for seller} = \frac{10.5 \text{ hours}}{24 \text{ hours}} = 0.4375
$$

**Step 3 — Compute seller's share of running costs.**

$$
\text{Seller's share of running cost} = 8{,}500 \times 0.4375 = \boxed{\text{USD } 3{,}718.75}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute seller's share of daily earnings (hire).**

$$
\text{Seller's share of hire} = 22{,}000 \times 0.4375 = \boxed{\text{USD } 9{,}625.00}
$$

**Step 5 — Net cash position of the seller at delivery.**

$$
\text{Net cash to seller} = \text{Seller's share of hire} - \text{Seller's share of running cost}
$$

$$
\text{Net cash to seller} = 9{,}625.00 - 3{,}718.75 = \boxed{\text{USD } 5{,}906.25}
$$

The buyer must therefore **add USD 5,906.25 to the balance payment** to settle the apportionment account.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deposit Forfeiture on Buyer's Default

### Problem Statement

The **M/V OCEAN STAR** is sold for **USD 25 million** under the NSF 2011. A **10% deposit** is paid into escrow. The buyer **defaults after 21 days** (i.e., refuses to take delivery) without lawful excuse. The vessel's market value at the time of default is **USD 22 million**. The seller subsequently re-sells the vessel at **USD 22.5 million** to a third party after **45 days**.

Determine:
- (a) Amount of deposit.
- (b) Whether the seller can claim **additional damages** beyond the deposit.
- (c) The final monetary entitlement of the seller.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the deposit.**

$$
\text{Deposit} = 10\% \times 25{,}000{,}000 = \boxed{\text{USD } 2{,}500{,}000}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the seller's entitlement under NSF Clause 10.**

Under NSF Clause 10, if the buyer defaults **without lawful excuse**, the seller is entitled to:
- The **deposit (USD 2.5 million)** as forfeited.
- **An accounting of any further loss** — i.e., the difference between contract price and resale price — provided the seller acts **reasonably** to mitigate.

**Step 3 — Compute the seller's loss on resale.**

$$
\text{Loss on resale} = 25{,}000{,}000 - 22{,}500{,}000 = \boxed{\text{USD } 2{,}500{,}000}
$$

**Step 4 — Total entitlement of seller.**

$$
\text{Seller's total claim} = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ (deposit)} + 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ (loss)} = \boxed{\text{USD } 5{,}000{,}000}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Mitigation:** The seller **must mitigate** by re-selling at a *reasonable* price. Re-selling at **USD 22 million** (below market) when the market value was **USD 22.5 million** would be acceptable. Re-selling at **USD 18 million** without justification would expose the seller to a **reduction** of damages on grounds of **failure to mitigate**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Valuation Using DCF Approach

### Problem Statement

A shipowner is evaluating a 5-year-old Aframax tanker. The vessel is expected to generate a **net daily TCE of USD 18,000** for a remaining **15 years** of economic life. The owner's **hurdle rate (Required IRR)** is **12% p.a.**, and the **scrap value after 15 years** is estimated at **USD 5 million**.

Determine the **indicative present value (PV)** of the vessel as a buying proposition.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert daily TCE to annual cash flow.**

$$
\text{Annual Cash Flow} = 18{,}000 \times 365 = \text{USD } 6{,}570{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the annuity formula for 15 years at 12% discount rate.**

The present value of an annuity factor:

$$
\text{PVIFA}(r, n) = \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

$$
\text{PVIFA}(0.12, 15) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-15}}{0.12}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{15} = 5.4736 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1.12)^{-15} = 0.1827
$$

$$
\text{PVIFA}(0.12, 15) = \frac{1 - 0.1827}{0.12} = \frac{0.8173}{0.12} = 6.8109
$$

**Step 3 — Compute present value of operating cash flows.**

$$
\text{PV of cash flows} = 6{,}570{,}000 \times 6.8109 = \text{USD } 44{,}747{,}613
$$

**Step 4 — Compute present value of terminal scrap value.**

$$
\text{PV of scrap} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{(1.12)^{15}} = 5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.1827 = \text{USD } 913{,}500
$$

**Step 5 — Total indicative vessel value.**

$$
\text{Indicative Value} = 44{,}747{,}613 + 913{,}500 = \boxed{\text{USD } 45{,}661{,}113}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examinable Insight:** The DCF-based value of approximately **USD 45.7 million** becomes the **ceiling** the buyer should pay. The **floor** is the **scrap value (USD 5 million)**. The negotiation outcome sits somewhere in between, moderated by **sentiment, financing terms, and urgency**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Mortgage Consent Calculation

### Problem Statement

The M/V BLUE HORIZON is being sold for **USD 30 million**. The seller's bank holds a **first-priority mortgage** of **USD 12 million** on the vessel. Under the MoA, the buyer pays the **mortgagee consent fee of 1% of the outstanding mortgage**.

Compute:
- (a) Mortgage consent fee.
- (b) Net receivable by seller's bank.
- (c) Net payable to seller after mortgage discharge.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Mortgage consent fee.**

$$
\text{Consent fee} = 1\% \times 12{,}000{,}000 = \boxed{\text{USD } 120{,}000}
$$

**Step 2 — Net receivable by seller's bank (after deduction of consent fee from mortgage).**

$$
\text{Net to bank} = 12{,}000{,}000 - 120{,}000 = \boxed{\text{USD } 11{,}880{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculation of net payable to seller.**

$$
\text{Balance Payment} = 30{,}000{,}000 \times 0.90 = 27{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net to seller} = \text{Balance Payment} - \text{Mortgage discharge} - \text{Consent fee}
$$

$$
\text{Net to seller} = 27{,}000{,}000 - 12{,}000{,}000 - 120{,}000 = \boxed{\text{USD } 14{,}880{,}000}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **mortgagee consent fee** is industry practice and is paid by the **buyer**, not the seller. This compensates the seller's bank for the administrative cost of releasing its security.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Sale Forms

| Parameter | NSF 2011 | BIMCO Saleform 2012 | Singapore Sale Form 2007 | Demolition (DEMOLISHCON 2017) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binding on Signature?** | Yes (Cl. 2) | Yes (Cl. 2a) | Yes | Yes |
| **Default Deposit Treatment** | Forfeiture to seller | Stakeholder + cap at 50% | Forfeiture | Forfeiture |
| **Inspection Right** | Cl. 3 | Cl. 5 | Cl. 4 | Limited (visual) |
| **Sanctions Clause** | No (added via addenda) | Yes (2018 addenda) | No | Yes (explicit) |
| **Arbitration Seat** | London (default) | London (default) | Singapore (default) | As agreed |
| **Governing Law** | English (default) | English (default) | Singaporean | English |
| **Specific Performance Available?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Suitability** | All second-hand | All second-hand | S-E Asian second-hand | Demolition only |

---

## 3.6 Master Summary Table — KTU Numerical Toolkit

| Calculation Type | Key Formula | Default Values |
|---|---|---|
| **Deposit** | $\text{Deposit} = \text{PP} \times d$ | $d = 0.10$ |
| **Apportionment** | $\text{Apportioned} = \text{Daily Rate} \times \frac{h}{24}$ | Anchor: noon of delivery day |
| **Bunker Pricing** | $\text{Price} = \text{MT} \times \text{Port Rate}$ | As per BDN |
| **Lub Oil Pricing** | $\text{Price} = \text{Litres} \times \text{Cents/litre}$ | Inventory at delivery |
| **Mortgage Consent Fee** | $\text{Fee} = \text{Mortgage} \times 0.01$ | 1% default |
| **Brokerage** | $\text{Commission} = \text{PP} \times 0.0125$ | 1.25% each side |
| **DCF PV** | $\text{PV} = \text{CF} \times \text{PVIFA}(r,n) + \frac{SV}{(1+r)^n}$ | r = 12%, n = 15 years |
| **Damages on Default** | $\text{Loss} = \text{PP}_{\text{contract}} - \text{PP}_{\text{resale}}$ | + Deposit forfeiture |
