# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> Each answer must be **concise, structured, and citation-aware**.

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]

**Distinguish between the 'Norwegian Sale Form 2011' and the 'BIMCO Saleform 2012' in the context of deposit handling and default consequences.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | NSF 2011 | BIMCO Saleform 2012 |
|---|---|---|
| **Deposit Holder** | Seller's broker (P&I stakeholder) | Independent stakeholder (escrow agent) |
| **Default by Buyer** | Entire deposit forfeited to seller (Cl. 10) | **Cap at 50% of deposit** (Cl. 10) |
| **Default by Seller** | Refund + interest **or** specific performance (Cl. 11) | Refund + interest **or** specific performance |
| **Sanctions Compliance** | Requires addendum | Built-in clause (2018 addenda) |
| **Modernity** | Dated drafting language | Updated for contemporary issues |

The **critical distinction** lies in the **financial exposure of the buyer** upon default: under the NSF 2011, the **entire 10% deposit** is forfeited, while under the BIMCO Saleform 2012, the buyer's exposure is **capped at 50%** of the deposit — a more buyer-friendly provision.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Explain the concept of 'Memorandum of Agreement' in ship sale transactions. Why is it described as 'binding upon signature'?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **Memorandum of Agreement (MoA)** is the **primary contract of sale** in a ship S&P transaction, almost invariably drafted on a standard form — typically the **Norwegian Sale Form 2011** or **BIMCO Saleform 2012**. It contains all material terms: the parties, the vessel's identity, the purchase price, deposit, delivery place and date, conditions precedent, apportionments, governing law, and arbitration.

The MoA is described as **'binding upon signature'** because, by virtue of its **Cl. 2** (NSF 2011) or **Cl. 2(a)** (BIMCO 2012), the parties' signatures alone create a **legally enforceable contract** — even though:
- The physical inspection may not yet have occurred,
- The mortgagee's consent may not yet have been received,
- The financing may not yet have closed.

This is a **distinctive feature of maritime sale contracts**, born out of the practical necessity of immediate commercial certainty in international transactions. The binding nature of the MoA is what makes the **deposit** meaningful and **enforceable** under the law of the agreed seat.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]

#### (a) Examine the legal mechanics of the ship sale and purchase market, highlighting the role of shipbrokers and the standard contractual instruments governing such transactions. (7 marks)

#### (b) A vessel is sold for USD 40 million with a 10% deposit. The buyer defaults without lawful excuse. Compute the seller's entitlement if the vessel is resold for USD 36 million after 60 days. (7 marks)

---

#### (a) Model Answer — Legal Mechanics of S&P Market (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Scope (1 Mark):**
The ship Sale and Purchase (S&P) market is the international secondary market for transfer of ownership of ocean-going vessels, governed by standard form contracts and broker-mediated negotiations.

**2. Role of Shipbrokers (2 Marks):**
- **Commercial Intermediation:** Brokers (e.g., Clarksons, Braemar ACM, Platou) circulate sale circulars, identify counterparties, and negotiate price.
- **Documentation:** Brokers draft the **Recap (telex recap)** — the precursor to the MoA — and assist in MoA preparation.
- **Stakeholder Role:** Under the NSF 2011, the broker typically holds the deposit in escrow (P&I designated stakeholder) until delivery.
- **Commission:** Brokerage is shared 50:50 between seller's and buyer's brokers, usually at **1.25% each side** (2.5% total).

**3. Standard Contractual Instruments (3 Marks):**
- **Norwegian Sale Form (NSF) 2011** — the most widely used S&P contract, comprising 23 clauses.
- **BIMCO Saleform 2012** — a modernized alternative with built-in sanctions, anti-bribery, and stakeholder deposit clauses.
- **Singapore Sale Form (2007)** — used in South-East Asian transactions.
- **SAJ 2013** — for Japanese-flag vessels.
- **DEMOLISHCON 2017** — for sale to scrap yards.

**4. Legal Framework (1 Mark):**
The contract is supplemented by statutory law of the governing jurisdiction (typically **English Maritime Law**), admiralty law, and — where applicable — the **Sale of Goods Act, 1930**, the **Indian Contract Act, 1872**, and the **Cape Town Convention, 2001** for security interests.

---

#### (b) Model Answer — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Purchase Price (PP) = USD 40,000,000
- Deposit = 10% × 40,000,000 = **USD 4,000,000**
- Resale Price = USD 36,000,000
- Buyer's default without lawful excuse.

**Step 1 — Forfeiture of deposit (1 Mark):**
Under **NSF Cl. 10** and general English contract law, the seller is entitled to **forfeit the entire 10% deposit** upon buyer's default without lawful excuse.

$$
\text{Deposit Forfeited} = 4{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Computation of additional loss on resale (2 Marks):**
The seller is also entitled to damages representing the difference between the contract price and the resale price (provided the seller acted reasonably to mitigate).

$$
\text{Loss on Resale} = 40{,}000{,}000 - 36{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of all entitlements (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Total Seller Entitlement} = \text{Deposit Forfeited} + \text{Loss on Resale}
$$

$$
\text{Total Seller Entitlement} = 4{,}000{,}000 + 4{,}000{,}000 = \boxed{\text{USD } 8{,}000{,}000}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion on mitigation and time-value (3 Marks):**
The 60-day delay in resale attracts the issue of **mitigation**. The seller must act as a **prudent owner** to resell at the best available price. If the resale at USD 36M was the prevailing market rate at the time, the damages stand. If the seller delayed unnecessarily, the **buyer could counterclaim** for **excess damages** arising from avoidable depreciation.

The total claim of **USD 8 million** is the **ceiling** of the seller's recovery, subject to:
- **Proof of mitigation efforts**, and
- **Deduction of any savings** (e.g., if the seller avoided 60 days of vessel operating costs of, say, USD 5,000/day, total savings = USD 300,000 — reducing the claim to USD 7.7M).

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]

#### (a) Explain the apportionment of earnings and expenses at the time of delivery of a vessel under the Norwegian Sale Form 2011. Illustrate with a numerical example. (7 marks)

#### (b) Discuss the role of the buyer's pre-delivery inspection in the S&P market. What remedies are available to the buyer if material defects are discovered? (7 marks)

---

#### (a) Model Answer — Apportionment of Earnings and Expenses (7 marks)

**1. Conceptual Basis (2 Marks):**
Under **Cl. 4 of the NSF 2011**, earnings, expenses, and running costs are **apportioned between seller and buyer at the time of delivery**. The apportionment anchor is conventionally **noon of the delivery day** — though the parties may agree otherwise.

The principle is based on the **"running on the clock"** doctrine — i.e., the vessel is treated as continuously earning, and the seller is entitled to revenue (and liable for expenses) for the portion of the day during which the seller remained the registered owner.

**2. Items Subject to Apportionment (2 Marks):**
- **Earnings:** Time charter hire, voyage charter freight for cargo loaded.
- **Expenses:** Crew wages, port charges, routine maintenance, consumables.
- **Special Items:** Bunkers and lubricating oil are **not apportioned** — they are **physically transferred** to the buyer at the **prevailing port price** (per Bunker Delivery Note).

**3. Numerical Illustration (3 Marks):**

**Given:** Vessel delivered at **15:30 hrs** on delivery day. Daily running cost = **USD 9,000**. Daily time charter hire = **USD 25,000**.

$$
\text{Pro-rata fraction of day (seller)} = \frac{15.5}{24} = 0.6458
$$

$$
\text{Seller's share of running cost} = 9{,}000 \times 0.6458 = \text{USD } 5{,}812.50
$$

$$
\text{Seller's share of hire} = 25{,}000 \times 0.6458 = \text{USD } 16{,}145.83
$$

$$
\text{Net cash to seller} = 16{,}145.83 - 5{,}812.50 = \boxed{\text{USD } 10{,}333.33}
$$

The buyer adjusts the **balance payment** upward by this amount to settle the apportionment account at delivery.

---

#### (b) Model Answer — Pre-Delivery Inspection (7 marks)

**1. Right to Inspect (2 Marks):**
**Cl. 3 of the NSF 2011** entitles the buyer (or its appointed surveyor) to inspect the vessel at a **safe and convenient port or anchorage** prior to delivery. The seller must facilitate this inspection — providing access to the engine room, holds, navigation equipment, and class records.

**2. Scope of Inspection (1 Mark):**
- Hull, machinery, and equipment condition.
- Class status — confirmation that the vessel is **"in class"** and **free of conditions/recommendations**.
- Compliance with environmental and safety regulations.
- Visual examination of cargo spaces.

**3. Dry-Docking Right (1 Mark):**
The buyer has the right to require the vessel to be **dry-docked for inspection** — at the buyer's cost and time — unless a **material defect** is found, in which case the seller bears the cost.

**4. Remedies on Discovery of Material Defects (3 Marks):**
- **Renegotiate Price** — buyer may request a price reduction commensurate with the defect.
- **Demand Rectification** — seller may repair the defect before delivery.
- **Rescind the Contract** — if the defect is so material that it constitutes a **breach of condition** (e.g., the vessel is not as described).
- **Claim Damages** — buyer may claim damages post-delivery for latent defects not discoverable by reasonable inspection.
- **Reject Delivery** — in extreme cases, the buyer may refuse to take delivery, claiming fundamental breach.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Time Charter vs. Voyage Charter apportionments.** Voyage freight is treated differently — only freight for cargo **on board at the time of delivery** passes to the buyer, while freight for cargo already **discharged but unpaid** is **seller's**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "noon" anchor.** Students often compute apportionment at "midnight" or at the time of MoA signing — both are **wrong** unless the MoA specifies otherwise.
> 3. **Treating bunkers as an apportioned item.** Bunkers are **NOT** apportioned — they are **transferred physically at port price**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ The S&P market deals with the **transfer of ownership** of ships — distinct from chartering (hire) and shipbuilding (construction).
- ✅ The **Memorandum of Agreement (MoA)** is the **primary contract** — almost always on the **Norwegian Sale Form 2011** or **BIMCO Saleform 2012**.
- ✅ The MoA is **binding upon signature** under Cl. 2 / Cl. 2(a) — even before CPs are satisfied.
- ✅ **Deposit = 10%** of purchase price (negotiable range 5%–20%). **Balance = 90%** on delivery.
- ✅ **Apportionment of earnings and expenses** is anchored at **noon of the delivery day** (default).
- ✅ **Bunkers and lubricating oil** are **physically transferred** at **port price** (BDN basis) — not apportioned.
- ✅ **Voyage charter freight** for cargo **on board** passes to the buyer; for discharged cargo it remains the seller's.
- ✅ **Mortgage consent fee** (typically **1%**) is paid by the **buyer**.
- ✅ **Brokerage** is shared — usually **1.25% each side** (2.5% total).
- ✅ **Buyer's default:** deposit forfeited (NSF) or capped at 50% (BIMCO 2012) + damages.
- ✅ **Seller's default:** refund + interest **or** specific performance.
- ✅ **Title transfers on physical delivery** — supported by **Bill of Sale**, **PDA**, and **Deletion Certificate**.
- ✅ **Vessel can be registered under only one flag** at a time — **deletion** is a prerequisite for **new registration**.
- ✅ **DCF valuation** of a vessel: $P \approx \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{SV}{(1+r)^n}$.
- ✅ **KTU Top 3 Pitfalls:** (i) confusing NSF with BIMCO deposit treatment, (ii) using wrong apportionment anchor time, (iii) treating bunkers as apportioned.
- ✅ **Cross-jurisdictional check:** ensure sanctions compliance (OFAC, EU, UN) and **Cape Town Convention** registration for financed vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategic Advice:** In the **ESE (End Semester Examination)**, Module 4 S&P questions are most often tested via:
> - A **3-mark definition** (MoA, NSF, BIMCO, deposit, apportionment).
> - A **7-mark numerical** on apportionment or DCF valuation.
> - A **7-mark legal analysis** on a specific NSF clause (usually Cl. 10, 11, or 12).
> Practice all three formats — the question bank above is a faithful reproduction of the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newbuilding Market and Demolition Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Newbuilding Market and Demolition Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Newbuilding Market — Formal Definition

The **Newbuilding Market** is the segment of the global maritime industry where newly designed and constructed vessels are traded between shipowners (buyers) and shipyards (sellers/builders). The transaction is formalized through a **Shipbuilding Contract (Newbuilding Contract)**, which is essentially a contract for the construction and sale of a ship "to be built" (i.e., a *specifik* or *specific* contract under the SAJ (Standard Newbuilding Contract) form, or under other standard forms such as the NEWBUILDCON).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The newbuilding market is one of the **four pillars of the shipping market**, alongside the freight (cargo) market, the second-hand (sale & purchase) market, and the demolition (scrapping) market. These four markets are highly interlinked and form the "shipping cycle."

### 1.2 The Demolition Market — Formal Definition

The **Demolition Market** (also called the **Ship-Recycling Market** or **Scrapping Market**) is the segment of the maritime industry in which obsolete, uneconomical, or damaged vessels are sold by shipowners to cash buyers (recycling yards), primarily for the recovery of **steel scrap** and other recyclable materials (machinery, fittings, marine equipment). The vessel is typically stripped down, broken up, and the steel is melted and re-used in re-rolling mills.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Lightweight / Light Displacement Tonnage (LDT):** The price of a ship for demolition is quoted in terms of **United States Dollars (USD) per LDT**. LDT is the weight of the vessel excluding cargo, fuel, water, crew, and consumables — essentially the weight of the empty ship's steel and machinery. This is a **standard KTU exam keyword**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you own a family car. After many years, you have two options when it gets old:

1. **Option A — "Newbuilding" Equivalent:** You sell the old car to a dealer and order a **brand-new car** from a factory (e.g., Toyota, Hyundai). You sign a contract with the factory, pay a deposit, and wait several months for delivery. The factory's workload, the price of steel, and the global demand for cars all influence how expensive and how quickly you get the new car. This is exactly how the **newbuilding market** works — with **shipyards** (Korea's Hyundai Heavy Industries, China's CSSC, Japan's Imabari) acting as the "factories" and **shipowners** acting as the buyers.

2. **Option B — "Demolition" Equivalent:** Instead of buying a new car, you simply **scrap** the old one at a junkyard for its metal value. The junkyard pays you based on the **weight of the steel** in the car, not on the car's original price. The junkyard strips out valuable parts (engine, battery, catalytic converter) and melts the metal. This is exactly how the **demolition market** works — with **recycling yards in Alang (India), Chittagong (Bangladesh), Gadani (Pakistan), and Aliaga (Turkey)** acting as the "junkyards" and **cash buyers** acting as the intermediaries.

### 1.4 Key Terminology in Callout Format

> [!NOTE]
> **Sale & Purchase (S&P) Brokers:** Intermediaries who match buyers and sellers in both newbuilding and demolition markets. Examples: Clarksons, Braemar ACM, Fearnleys, Howe Robinson.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cash Buyer:** A specialist buyer (often a recycling-yard owner or affiliated trader) who purchases a vessel specifically for demolition, paying in cash (or quick bank transfer) and assuming the risk of onward sale to a recycling yard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **METRIC TO REMEMBER:** Demolition prices fluctuate between **USD 200/LDT** (slump) and **USD 700+/LDT** (boom). The **BDI (Baltic Dry Index)**, **Capesize/Tanker earnings**, and **steel-scrap prices in India/Turkey** are the chief drivers.

### 1.5 Why Study These Two Markets Together?

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Module 4 deliberately groups these two markets because they represent the **two extreme ends of a vessel's economic life cycle**:

$$\text{Ship Life Cycle} \;=\; \text{Newbuilding} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Second-hand (S\&P)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Demolition}$$

A newbuilding order today is a future supply unit for the world fleet 2–3 years from now. Conversely, a vessel sold for demolition today removes tonnage from the active fleet. Therefore, both markets act as **supply-side adjustment mechanisms** for the global shipping industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Newbuilding Market — Theoretical Breakdown

The newbuilding market is a **highly cyclical, capital-intensive, and order-driven** market. Unlike the spot freight market (which clears in days), a newbuilding order commits capital for **2 to 4 years** (the construction period). Below is the structured logic of the market.

#### 2.1.1 Parties to a Newbuilding Contract

- **Builder (Shipyard):** The shipbuilder. May be a privately owned yard, a state-owned yard (e.g., China State Shipbuilding Corporation — CSSC), or a multinational consortium.
- **Buyer (Shipowner / Disponent Owner):** The party placing the order. May be a single-ship owner or a major liner (e.g., Maersk, MSC, COSCO).
- **Guarantor / Bank / Mortgagee:** Often a financing bank that issues a **Refund Guarantee** to protect the buyer's pre-delivery instalments.

#### 2.1.2 Phases of a Newbuilding Transaction

1. **Inquiry & Negotiation Phase** — Owner approaches yards; technical specification is drafted.
2. **Letter of Intent (LOI) Signing** — Non-binding agreement; deposit paid (typically **2%–5%** of contract price).
3. **Contract Signing** — Binding contract executed; first instalment paid (typically **20%** on signing).
4. **Construction Phase** — Steel-cutting, keel-laying, launching, fitting-out, sea trials. Second instalment (typically **30%**) on keel-lay; third instalment (typically **30%**) on launch.
5. **Delivery Phase** — Final instalment (typically **20%**) paid against delivery and **Bill of Sale**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Payment Schedule (4-Stage Instalment Plan) — THE most tested fact in KTU exams:**
> Stage 1: 20% on Contract Signing
> Stage 2: 30% on Keel Laying
> Stage 3: 30% on Launching
> Stage 4: 20% on Delivery

#### 2.1.3 Key Drivers of the Newbuilding Market

- **Freight Rates & TCE Earnings:** When TCE (Time Charter Equivalent) earnings are high, owners place orders; when low, they cancel or postpone.
- **Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio:** A high ratio signals oversupply risk.
- **Steel Prices (Plate Steel):** Directly affects yard cost.
- **Yard Capacity & Slot Availability:** Major yards are usually booked years in advance.
- **Bunker Fuel & Environmental Regulations:** Drives demand for eco-ships (LNG-ready, dual-fuel, ammonia-ready).
- **Financing Costs (LIBOR/SOFR, Interest Rates):** Higher rates suppress new orders.
- **Speculative vs. Replacement Orders:** Speculative orders (no charter backing) fuel bubbles.

#### 2.1.4 Newbuilding Price Indices

The most commonly referenced indices are:

- **Clarksons Newbuilding Price Index (CNPI)**
- **Braemar Newbuilding Index**
- **Yangzijiang Newbuilding Index (for Chinese yards)**

> [!NOTE]
> A newbuilding contract is a **Forward Contract** in finance terminology — the price is locked in today for a deliverable asset to be produced in the future. Hence, "newbuilding prices lead freight rates" in cyclical analysis.

#### 2.1.5 Major Shipbuilding Nations (the "Big Three" + a Few)

| Country | Market Share (approx.) | Notable Yards |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **China (PRC)** | ~50% | CSSC, COSCO, New Times Shipbuilding |
| **South Korea** | ~30% | Hyundai HI, Samsung HI, Hanwha Ocean, Daewoo |
| **Japan** | ~10% | Imabari, Tsuneishi, K Line, Oshima |
| **Philippines / Vietnam / India / Türkiye / Europe** | ~10% | Smaller niche/specialised yards |

### 2.2 The Demolition Market — Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Methods of Ship Demolition

1. **Beaching Method** (used in India, Bangladesh, Pakistan) — The vessel is deliberately run aground on a tidal beach during high tide; as the tide recedes, the ship rests on the beach and is cut apart manually. **Cheapest method** but most hazardous.
2. **Slipway / Ramp Method** (used in Türkiye, China) — Vessel is winched up a prepared ramp or slipway, then dismantled. **Safer and more environmentally controlled.**
3. **Alongside / Jetty Method** (China, some European yards) — Vessel is moored at a quay; dismantling occurs at the berth. **Most expensive but safest.**
4. **Dry-Dock Method** (Europe) — Vessel enters a floating or graving dock for dismantling. **Rare due to cost.**

#### 2.2.2 Key Drivers of the Demolition Market

- **Age of the World Fleet** (typical scrapping age: **25–35 years** for tankers and bulkers).
- **Steel Scrap Prices** in importing countries (especially India and Türkiye).
- **Second-hand Sale & Purchase Prices** — If S&P price > demolition price, owners sell for trade; otherwise, for scrap.
- **Environmental Regulations** — IMO's **Hong Kong Convention (2009)**, **EU Ship Recycling Regulation (SRR) 1257/2013**, Basel Ban Amendment.
- **Bunker Regulations & EEXI/CII** — Older, less-efficient vessels become economically obsolete.
- **Currency Exchange Rates** — Strong Indian Rupee (INR) and Pakistani Rupee (PKR) support higher LDT prices.
- **Freight Rate Trough** — When freight rates are low, owners scrap rather than operate at a loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule for an Owner:** Scrap if **(Demolition Price × LDT) + (Remaining S&P value) > (Expected Net Earnings from Operation) − (Operational Costs + Bunker + Port Charges + Special Survey Costs)**. This is the **core economic decision** behind every demolition sale.

#### 2.2.3 Major Ship-Recycling Nations (Top Demolition Markets)

| Country | Method Dominant | Approx. Share |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Bangladesh (Chittagong)** | Beaching | ~50% |
| **India (Alang, Gujarat)** | Beaching | ~25% |
| **Pakistan (Gadani)** | Beaching | ~10% |
| **Türkiye (Aliaga)** | Slipway | ~10% |
| **China (various)** | Alongside / Slipway | ~5% |

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Single Source of Truth)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated, exam-ready formula sheet. All numerical values use the explicit notation `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid table syntax issues. Memorize every cell.

| Concept | Formula / Key Metric | Typical Units | Exam Significance |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Demolition Sale Proceeds** | $\text{Proceeds} \;=\; \text{Demolition Price} \times \text{LDT}$ | USD | Direct revenue to seller |
| **Newbuilding Instalment** | $\text{Instalment}_{i} \;=\; \text{Contract Price} \times \text{Instalment } \%_{i}$ | USD | 20 / 30 / 30 / 20 schedule |
| **TCE Earnings** | $\text{TCE} \;=\; \frac{\text{Gross Revenue} - \text{Voyage Costs}}{\text{Loadable Days}}$ | USD/day | Drives newbuilding orders |
| **Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Orderbook Tonnage}}{\text{Existing Fleet Tonnage}} \times 100$ | % | Barometer of future supply |
| **Scrap Age (Average)** | $\bar{A}_{\text{scrap}} \;\approx\; 28 \;\text{to}\; 32 \;\text{years}$ | years | A bulk tanker / bulker |
| **Reflagging for Scrapping** | $\text{Note: vessel often reflagged to Comoros, St. Kitts, Tuvalu}$ | — | Legal flag-of-convenience step |
| **LDT Conversion (Approx.)** | $\text{LDT} \;\approx\; 0.25 \;\text{to}\; 0.35 \times \text{DWT}$ | Tonnes | Engineering estimate only |
| **Hull Insurance (Demolition)** | Usually sold "as is, where is" with NO insurance; cash buyer bears all risk | — | Sale contract clause |
| **Newbuilding Lead Time** | $\text{Lead Time} \;\approx\; 24 \;\text{to}\; 36 \;\text{months}$ | months | Bulker/Tanker typical |
| **Steel Recycling Yield** | $\text{Yield} \;\approx\; 90\% \;\text{to}\; 95\%$ of LDT as usable scrap | % | Of vessel's LDT |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **For Shipowners:** Knowing the relative attractiveness of selling for trade vs. scrapping helps in **fleet renewal** decisions and timing the S&P cycle.
- **For Shipyards:** Forward orderbook analysis informs **capital expenditure** and **dock-slot allocation**.
- **For Maritime Lawyers:** Drafting of **MOA (Memorandum of Agreement) for demolition sales** must comply with the **Hong Kong Convention** and the **EU SRR**. Improper documentation can lead to criminal liability.
- **For Economists & Analysts:** The **flow of tonnage** between newbuilding and demolition defines the **Net Fleet Growth Rate**, which determines freight rate cycles.

$$\text{Net Fleet Growth (\%)} \;=\; \frac{\text{New Deliveries} - \text{Demolition Sales}}{\text{Existing Fleet}} \times 100$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations and Numerical Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **step-by-step, fully written out**, and free of any code. Each algebraic transition is justified in plain text. The topic is qualitative-economics in nature; hence, the "derivations" are mostly **decision-rule derivations** and **payment-schedule derivations**.

---

#### DERIVATION 1: Demolition Sale Proceeds — Worked Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU style):**
A 25-year-old Aframax tanker of **115,000 DWT** is sold for demolition. Given the **Light Displacement Tonnage (LDT) is 18,500 tonnes** and the prevailing **demolition price is USD 510/LDT**, calculate:
(a) The gross sale proceeds to the shipowner.
(b) The net proceeds if the seller's commission to the cash buyer is **USD 15/LDT**.
(c) The break-even scrap steel price (USD per tonne of re-rollable steel) at the recycling yard, if the yard's total cost of dismantling is **USD 4,500,000** and the non-steel resale (machinery, copper, aluminium) is **USD 1,200,000**.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables explicitly.**

Let:
- $L \;=\; 18{,}500 \;\text{tonnes}$ (LDT)
- $P_{\text{demo}} \;=\; 510 \;\text{USD/LDT}$ (gross demolition price)
- $C_{\text{comm}} \;=\; 15 \;\text{USD/LDT}$ (cash buyer commission)
- $C_{\text{dismantle}} \;=\; 4{,}500{,}000 \;\text{USD}$ (yard dismantling cost)
- $R_{\text{non-steel}} \;=\; 1{,}200{,}000 \;\text{USD}$ (non-steel resale value)

**Step 2 — Compute the gross proceeds to the owner.**

The gross proceeds is simply the LDT multiplied by the gross demolition price:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Proceeds} \;&=\; P_{\text{demo}} \times L \\
\;&=\; 510 \;\frac{\text{USD}}{\text{tonne}} \times 18{,}500 \;\text{tonnes} \\
\;&=\; 9{,}435{,}000 \;\text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the net proceeds after the cash buyer's commission.**

The cash buyer's commission is calculated on the same LDT basis, hence the net price per LDT is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{net}} \;&=\; P_{\text{demo}} - C_{\text{comm}} \\
\;&=\; 510 - 15 \\
\;&=\; 495 \;\text{USD/LDT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by LDT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Proceeds} \;&=\; P_{\text{net}} \times L \\
\;&=\; 495 \times 18{,}500 \\
\;&=\; 9{,}157{,}500 \;\text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the break-even scrap steel price for the recycling yard.**

The recycling yard pays the cash buyer at the gross price, then must recover:
- The cost paid to the cash buyer (already in the gross price equation).
- The dismantling cost $C_{\text{dismantle}}$.
- Minus the revenue from non-steel resale $R_{\text{non-steel}}$.

Let $P_{\text{steel}}$ be the break-even selling price of the recovered steel per tonne. The yard needs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{steel}} \times L \;&\geq\; P_{\text{demo}} \times L \;+\; C_{\text{dismantle}} \;-\; R_{\text{non-steel}} \\
P_{\text{steel}} \;\geq\;& \frac{P_{\text{demo}} \times L \;+\; C_{\text{dismantle}} \;-\; R_{\text{non-steel}}}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{steel}} \;&\geq\; \frac{(510 \times 18{,}500) \;+\; 4{,}500{,}000 \;-\; 1{,}200{,}000}{18{,}500} \\
\;&\geq\; \frac{9{,}435{,}000 \;+\; 4{,}500{,}000 \;-\; 1{,}200{,}000}{18{,}500} \\
\;&\geq\; \frac{12{,}735{,}000}{18{,}500} \\
\;&\geq\; 688.38 \;\text{USD/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

> The yard must sell the recovered re-rollable steel scrap at **at least USD 688.38 per tonne** to break even. If the prevailing market price for ship-breaking steel in India/Türkiye is, say, USD 620/tonne, then this particular vessel would be **unattractive to the recycler**, and the cash buyer would not buy it at USD 510/LDT.

---

#### DERIVATION 2: Newbuilding Instalment Schedule — Worked Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A shipowner signs a newbuilding contract for a **Kamsarmax bulk carrier of 82,000 DWT** at a **contract price of USD 38,000,000** with a major Korean yard, on the **standard 20/30/30/20 instalment schedule**. Calculate:
(a) The amount payable at each stage.
(b) The **Refunded First Instalment (RFI)** if the yard goes bankrupt after signing but before keel-laying. State the typical instrument that protects this amount.

**Step 1 — Restate the schedule.**

| Stage | Trigger | % of Contract |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Contract Signing | 20 |
| 2 | Keel Laying | 30 |
| 3 | Launching | 30 |
| 4 | Delivery | 20 |

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute amount per stage.**

Let $C \;=\; 38{,}000{,}000 \;\text{USD}$ (contract price).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1} \;&=\; 0.20 \times 38{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 7{,}600{,}000 \;\text{USD} \\
\text{Stage 2} \;&=\; 0.30 \times 38{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 11{,}400{,}000 \;\text{USD} \\
\text{Stage 3} \;&=\; 0.30 \times 38{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 11{,}400{,}000 \;\text{USD} \\
\text{Stage 4} \;&=\; 0.20 \times 38{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 7{,}600{,}000 \;\text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum Check:** $7.6 + 11.4 + 11.4 + 7.6 \;=\; 38.0$ million USD. ✓

**Step 3 — Determine the Refunded First Instalment (RFI).**

If the yard becomes bankrupt before keel laying, the owner has paid **Stage 1 (USD 7,600,000)** but received nothing in return. To protect the owner, a **Refund Guarantee (RG)** is issued by a bank, usually the same bank that finances the yard. The RG is typically **in the amount of Stage 1 + Stage 2** to cover the first two instalments.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RG Coverage (typical)} \;&=\; \text{Stage 1} + \text{Stage 2} \\
\;&=\; 7{,}600{,}000 + 11{,}400{,}000 \\
\;&=\; 19{,}000{,}000 \;\text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A *Refund Guarantee* is a **counter-guarantee** issued by the yard's bank to the owner's bank, payable **on demand** if the yard breaches. The owner is fully protected against yard insolvency in the pre-delivery phase.

---

#### DERIVATION 3: Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio — Forecasting Future Supply

**Problem Statement:**
At the start of 2024, the global Capesize bulk carrier fleet stands at **2,000 vessels** with a total DWT of **400 million tonnes**. The current orderbook is **80 vessels** totalling **18 million DWT**, scheduled for delivery over the next 3 years. Annual demolition of Capesize vessels averages **6 million DWT** per year. Calculate:
(a) The current orderbook-to-fleet ratio (in %).
(b) The expected fleet size in DWT after 3 years, ignoring scrapping for simplicity, and comment on the supply outlook.

**Step 1 — Calculate the ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} \;&=\; \frac{\text{Orderbook DWT}}{\text{Existing Fleet DWT}} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; \frac{18{,}000{,}000}{400{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
\;&=\; 4.5 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

A ratio of **4.5%** is **moderate-to-low** (the historical comfort zone is 5%–10%; above 15% is dangerously oversupplied).

**Step 2 — Compute the expected 3-year fleet size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fleet}_{\text{3 years}} \;&=\; 400 \;\text{million} + 18 \;\text{million (deliveries)} - (3 \times 6 \;\text{million}) \\
\;&=\; 400 + 18 - 18 \\
\;&=\; 400 \;\text{million DWT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment.**

Despite 18 million DWT of newbuilding deliveries, the demolition of an equal 18 million DWT over the same 3 years keeps the fleet **flat**. This indicates a **supply-demand equilibrium** scenario, where freight rates are likely to remain stable provided demand is constant.

---

#### DERIVATION 4: Decision Rule — Scrap vs. Continue Trading

**Problem Statement:**
A 28-year-old Panamax bulker of 75,000 DWT has an LDT of **9,200 tonnes**. The owner is offered:
- **Trade Sale (S&P):** USD 6.5 million
- **Demolition Sale:** USD 480/LDT

The vessel's expected annual net operating profit (after operational costs, excluding drydock) is **USD 1.2 million**. The next Special Survey (dry-dock) will cost **USD 2.0 million** and is due in 6 months. Ignoring financing and time-value of money, should the owner sell for trade or for demolition?

**Step 1 — Compute the demolition value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Demolition Value} \;&=\; 480 \;\text{USD/LDT} \times 9{,}200 \;\text{tonnes} \\
\;&=\; 4{,}416{,}000 \;\text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare the two options.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Trade Sale} \;&=\; 6{,}500{,}000 \;\text{USD} \\
\text{Demolition Sale} \;&=\; 4{,}416{,}000 \;\text{USD} \\
\text{Difference} \;&=\; 2{,}084{,}000 \;\text{USD} \;\text{in favour of trade}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check the "continue trading" option.**

If the owner continues to trade for 1 year:
- Earns USD 1,200,000 in net profit
- But must spend USD 2,000,000 on the Special Survey
- Net cash flow for the year = $1{,}200{,}000 - 2{,}000{,}000 = -800{,}000$ USD (a **loss** of USD 800,000)
- Plus the vessel depreciates further and may be worth only ~USD 4 million in 12 months.

**Step 4 — Decision.**

> The owner should **sell for trade (S&P) immediately** at USD 6.5 million, since the demolition value is lower and continuing to trade results in a near-term cash drain plus depreciation.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Newbuilding vs. Demolition

| Parameter | Newbuilding Market | Demolition Market |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Asset Quality** | Brand new, to owner spec | End-of-life, obsolete |
| **Buyer** | Shipowner (tanker, bulker, container operators) | Cash buyer → Recycling yard |
| **Seller** | Shipyard | Shipowner (desperate or strategic) |
| **Pricing Unit** | USD per vessel (lump sum) | USD per LDT |
| **Price Range** | USD 30M – 250M+ per vessel | USD 200 – 700 per LDT |
| **Lead Time** | 2–4 years | 1–3 months delivery to yard |
| **Payment Terms** | 4-stage instalments | Cash, "as is, where is" |
| **Key Driver** | Freight rates, demand outlook | Steel scrap prices, fleet age |
| **Standard Contract** | NEWBUILDCON (SAJ) | MOA "As Is, Where Is" + Recycle Green Protocol |
| **Regulatory Body** | Classification Society, Flag State | IMO Hong Kong Convention; EU SRR |
| **Risk to Buyer** | Yard insolvency → covered by Refund Guarantee | Hazardous materials, oil residues, beach pollution |
| **Risk to Seller** | Late delivery, specification defects | Unpaid demolition (mitigated by cash) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ship Economic Life Cycle — High-Level Flow

This Mermaid diagram shows how a vessel flows from newbuilding through trade to eventual scrapping, with the **two market segments** under study highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    NB2[Shipyard / Builder]

    SP1[Second-Hand S&P Market]
    SP2[Active Trading 20 to 30 yrs]
    DM1[Owner Decision Scrap or Trade]
    DM2[Cash Buyer Purchases]
    DM3[Recycling Yard Alang Chittagong Aliaga]
    DM4[Steel Re-rolled and Resold]

    NB1 --> NB2
    NB2 --> NB3
    NB3 --> NB4
    NB4 --> NB5
    NB5 --> SP1
    SP1 --> SP2
    SP2 --> DM1
    DM1 -->|Scrap Decision| DM2
    DM2 --> DM3
    DM3 --> DM4

    classDef newbuild fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef trade fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef demo fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class NB1,NB2,NB3,NB4,NB5 newbuild
    class SP1,SP2,DM1 trade

```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Newbuilding vs. Demolition Market Drivers

This final Mermaid block diagram shows the **causal drivers** affecting each market, allowing students to visualise how the same global variable (e.g., freight rates) ripples into both markets.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    subgraph DM["DEMOLITION MARKET DRIVERS"]

        DM5[High Special Survey Cost]
    end

    NB1 -->|Encourages Orders| OrderRise[Orderbook Rises]
    OrderRise -->|Future Supply Up| FRSlow[Freight Rates Soften in 2-3 Years]
    FRSlow -->|Owner Pressure| DM1
    DM1 -->|Pushes for Scrapping| DemoRise[Demolition Sales Rise]
    DemoRise -->|Tonnage Withdrawn| FRRecover[Freight Rates Recover]

    NB2 -->|Cheap Finance| OrderRise
    DM2 -->|Higher USD per LDT| DemoRise
    DM3 -->|Candidates Available| DemoRise
    DM4 -->|Older Ships Penalised| DemoRise
    DM5 -->|Owner Decides to Scrap| DemoRise
    NB3 -->|Cheap Yards| OrderRise
    NB4 -->|Demand for Eco Ships| OrderRise
    NB5 -->|Adds to Bubble| OrderRise
```
