<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**"Define the Demolition Market in shipping. What is meant by LDT and why is it used as the pricing unit for demolition sales?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Demolition Market** (or Ship-Recycling Market) is the segment of the maritime industry in which obsolete, uneconomical, or damaged vessels are sold for breaking up, primarily to recover steel scrap and reusable machinery. The dominant markets are India (Alang), Bangladesh (Chittagong), Pakistan (Gadani), and Türkiye (Aliaga).

**LDT** stands for **Light Displacement Tonnage**, defined as the weight of a vessel in tonnes when it is empty — without cargo, fuel, fresh water, ballast, crew, or consumables. It essentially represents the **weight of the steel structure plus machinery**.

LDT is used as the pricing unit because (i) the recycler is paid for the **metal content** of the ship, not its earning capacity; (ii) it provides a **standardised weight measure** comparable across vessels of different sizes and types; and (iii) the demolition price is internationally quoted as **USD per LDT**, which simplifies negotiation.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**"List and briefly explain the four standard instalment stages of a newbuilding contract under the SAJ form."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under the **SAJ (Standard Newbuilding Contract)** form, the contract price is paid in **four instalments**, typically as follows:

1. **First Instalment (20%)** — Payable **on signing of the newbuilding contract**. This is the buyer's commitment deposit. It is protected by a **Refund Guarantee** issued by the yard's bank.

2. **Second Instalment (30%)** — Payable **on keel laying**, i.e., when the first section of the ship's hull is physically placed on the building berth. It signals the start of physical construction.

3. **Third Instalment (30%)** — Payable **on launching**, i.e., when the hull is floated for the first time. At this stage, the buyer can usually inspect the vessel.

4. **Fourth Instalment (20%)** — Payable **on delivery**, after successful sea trials, class approval, and exchange of the **Bill of Sale**. The yard retains this final instalment as its primary profit retention.

This 20/30/30/20 schedule is the **industry standard** and is **extensively tested** in KTU exams.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**"Discuss in detail the structure of the global newbuilding market. Cover the major shipbuilding nations, the standard payment schedule, the key pricing drivers, and the legal instruments that protect the buyer's pre-delivery payments."**

*Sub-parts:*

**(a)** Identify the top three shipbuilding nations and explain how their relative competitiveness has shifted over the last two decades. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the standard 4-stage instalment schedule and describe the role and importance of the **Refund Guarantee** in protecting the buyer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Top three shipbuilding nations — shifting competitiveness (7 marks):**

The global newbuilding market is dominated by three East Asian shipbuilding nations, often called the **"Big Three"**:

1. **China (People's Republic of China)** — Currently the **largest shipbuilder** in the world by gross tonnage, with a market share of approximately **50%**. Major yards include **CSSC (China State Shipbuilding Corporation), COSCO Shipping Heavy Industry, and New Times Shipbuilding**. China's rise was driven by **state subsidies, low labour costs, and aggressive consolidation** in the late 2000s.

2. **South Korea** — The **technology leader**, holding around **30%** market share, with dominance in **high-value-added vessels** such as LNG carriers, VLCCs, ultra-large container ships, and offshore units. Key yards are **Hyundai Heavy Industries, HD Hyundai, Samsung Heavy Industries, and Hanwha Ocean** (formerly Daewoo).

3. **Japan** — Historically the global leader until the 2000s, Japan now holds about **10%** of the market, with strengths in **mid-size bulkers, product tankers, and car carriers**. Prominent yards include **Imabari Shipbuilding, Tsuneishi Shipbuilding, and Oshima Shipbuilding**.

**The shift over two decades:**
- In the **1980s–1990s**, Japan led the world. South Korea overtook Japan around **2003–2004** in terms of orderbook volume. China overtook South Korea around **2010–2012**.
- The shift was driven by **(i) Chinese state subsidies**, **(ii) Korean specialisation in complex vessels**, and **(iii) Japanese yards moving upmarket** into quality, fuel-efficient eco-ships.
- European shipyards (Germany, Italy, Romania) have largely exited commercial newbuilding, focusing on **naval vessels, cruise ships, and specialised tonnage**.

**(b) Standard 4-stage payment schedule and Refund Guarantee (7 marks):**

The **standard instalment schedule** under the SAJ form is:

| Stage | Trigger | % of Contract Price |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Contract Signing | 20% |
| 2 | Keel Laying | 30% |
| 3 | Launching | 30% |
| 4 | Delivery | 20% |

- **Stage 1 (20%)** is paid on contract signing. It serves as a **non-refundable** deposit for the buyer, but it is **refundable by a bank** in case of yard default.
- **Stage 2 (30%)** is paid on keel laying — a key milestone confirming physical construction has begun.
- **Stage 3 (30%)** is paid on launching — when the hull is waterborne.
- **Stage 4 (20%)** is the **delivery payment**, paid only after successful sea trials, classification approval, and exchange of the **Bill of Sale**.

**The Refund Guarantee (RG):**
- A **Refund Guarantee** is a **bank guarantee** issued by the yard's financing bank, payable **on demand** to the buyer (or the buyer's bank) if the yard fails to deliver the vessel.
- It typically covers the **first two instalments (Stage 1 + Stage 2 = 50% of contract price)**, although the exact coverage is contractually negotiated.
- The RG is the **single most important legal instrument** protecting the buyer's pre-delivery investment. Without a strong RG, the buyer's capital is at the mercy of the yard's solvency.
- Leading issuing banks include **K-Sure (Korea Trade Insurance Corporation)**, **KEXIM (Korea Export-Import Bank)**, **JBIC (Japan Bank for International Cooperation)**, and Chinese policy banks.
- The RG must be **"unconditional and irrevocable"** and payable on first written demand, without any protest or court intervention.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (a):** Students often confuse the **order of dominance** between Japan, South Korea, and China. A common error is to say "Japan still leads" or "China leads only in bulkers." Be precise: **China leads in volume, Korea leads in complex/ high-value vessels, Japan is third**. Marks are lost when the temporal shift is not explained.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**"Explain the structure, pricing mechanism, and regulatory environment of the global ship demolition market. Discuss the major recycling countries, the methods of demolition, and the significance of the Hong Kong Convention and the EU Ship Recycling Regulation."**

*Sub-parts:*

**(a)** Describe the **four major ship-recycling countries**, the **methods** used, and the factors that determine where a vessel is sent for demolition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Hong Kong International Convention for the Safe and Environmentally Sound Recycling of Ships (2009)** and the **EU Ship Recycling Regulation (SRR) 1257/2013**. What are the implications for a shipowner selling a vessel for demolition? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four major recycling countries, methods, and location determinants (7 marks):**

The four dominant ship-recycling countries are:

1. **India (Alang, Gujarat)** — The **largest organised recycling cluster** with around 200 recycling plots along a 10-km beach. Uses the **beaching method** predominantly. Capacity: 6–8 million LDT/year. The **Alang-Sosiya Ship Recycling Yard** is regulated by the **Gujarat Maritime Board** and increasingly compliant with the **Hong Kong Convention**.

2. **Bangladesh (Chittagong)** — The **largest by volume** in some years (especially 2020–2023). Capacity: 8–10 million LDT/year. Uses **beaching method** in nearly 100% of cases. Lower labour cost than India but weaker environmental enforcement.

3. **Pakistan (Gadani)** — Historic recycling cluster, capacity ~3–5 million LDT/year. **Beaching method**. Currently the smallest of the South Asian trio.

4. **Türkiye (Aliaga)** — The **only OECD country** in the top tier. Capacity ~2–3 million LDT/year. Uses the **slipway method**, which is significantly **safer and more environmentally controlled** than beaching. Türkiye is the **preferred destination** for EU-flagged vessels and oil-major-controlled tonnage (Shell, BP, TotalEnergies) because it meets **EU SRR standards**.

**Methods of Demolition:**
- **Beaching** (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan) — Cheapest, most hazardous.
- **Slipway / Ramp** (Türkiye, China) — Safer, more controlled.
- **Alongside** (some Chinese yards) — Most expensive, safest.
- **Dry Dock** (rare; Europe) — Most capital-intensive.

**Location determinants** include: (i) **prevailing scrap steel prices** in the importing country; (ii) **currency exchange rates**; (iii) **capacity at the yard**; (iv) **regulatory compliance** (e.g., EU SRR compliance restricts voyages to sub-standard yards); (v) **logistics** (proximity to major shipping lanes); and (vi) **oil-major approval** for tankers.

**(b) Hong Kong Convention and EU SRR — implications for shipowners (7 marks):**

**The Hong Kong Convention (2009):**
- Adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in May 2009; entered into force on **26 June 2025** after meeting the ratification threshold.
- It is the **first legally binding international instrument** specifically addressing ship recycling.
- Applies to ships of **500 GT and above** engaged in international voyages, and to recycling facilities.
- Requires shipowners to:
  - Maintain an **Inventory of Hazardous Materials (IHM)** throughout the vessel's operational life.
  - Obtain a **Ship Recycling Plan (SRP)** from the recycling facility before scrapping.
  - Ensure the recycling facility is **authorized** by its national authority.
- Prohibits or restricts the use of certain hazardous materials (asbestos, PCBs, ozone-depleting substances, tributyl tin, lead, etc.) in new ship construction.

**The EU Ship Recycling Regulation (SRR) 1257/2013:**
- Came into force on **30 December 2013**; in full effect from 31 December 2018.
- Applies to all **EU-flagged vessels** and certain non-EU vessels calling at EU ports.
- Bans the **beaching method** outright.
- Requires EU-flagged vessels to be recycled only in yards **included on the European List** of approved ship-recycling facilities.
- As of 2024, only yards in **Türkiye, the USA, the UK, Norway, and the EU itself** are on the European List.

**Implications for a shipowner selling a vessel for demolition:**

1. The owner must **commission and maintain an IHM**, which adds an annual cost of approximately **USD 5,000–15,000** per vessel.
2. EU-flagged vessels **cannot be sent to Alang, Chittagong, or Gadani** for scrapping (these are beaching yards not on the European List).
3. Non-EU-flagged vessels still often face **oil-major charter restrictions** that require the vessel to be scrapped only in approved yards.
4. The **cost of compliance** is now a recurring expense, raising the **total cost of vessel ownership** and indirectly increasing the **scrap age** decision boundary.
5. Owners may **reflag** to a non-EU flag just before demolition to avoid SRR, but this raises legal and reputational risks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (b):** A common error is to state that the **Hong Kong Convention is already in force**. The convention was adopted in 2009 but only **entered into force on 26 June 2025**, after 15 major ship-recycling states (including India and Bangladesh) ratified it, meeting the 40% combined ship-recycling tonnage threshold. Marks are lost for stating the wrong entry-into-force date. Another common error is to confuse the **Hong Kong Convention** with the **Basel Convention** — the Basel Convention controls **transboundary movement of hazardous waste**, while the Hong Kong Convention is a **ship-specific IMO instrument**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Punchlist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ The **newbuilding market** is dominated by **China, South Korea, and Japan** (the "Big Three").
- ☐ Standard **SAJ instalment schedule is 20 / 30 / 30 / 20** percent (signing, keel laying, launching, delivery). This is the **single most-tested fact**.
- ☐ A **Refund Guarantee (RG)** protects the buyer's pre-delivery instalments; issued by the yard's financing bank, typically covering the **first 50%** (Stages 1 + 2).
- ☐ The **demolition market** is dominated by **Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, and Türkiye**.
- ☐ **LDT (Light Displacement Tonnage)** is the unit of pricing; demolition price quoted in **USD per LDT**.
- ☐ Typical demolition price range: **USD 200 – 700 per LDT**.
- ☐ **Beaching method** (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan) is cheapest but most hazardous; **slipway method** (Türkiye) is safer.
- ☐ **Hong Kong Convention (2009)** entered into force on **26 June 2025**; requires **IHM (Inventory of Hazardous Materials)**.
- ☐ **EU Ship Recycling Regulation 1257/2013** bans **beaching**; restricts EU-flagged vessels to **European List yards**.
- ☐ The **orderbook-to-fleet ratio** is a key indicator of future shipping supply; **>15% is oversupplied**.
- ☐ Average **scrap age** is **28–32 years** for tankers and bulkers.
- ☐ Decision rule: **Scrap when (Demolition Price × LDT) > Expected Net Earnings – Special Survey Cost**.
- ☐ Major demolition price drivers: **Steel scrap prices, freight rates, fleet age, USD/local currency rates, environmental regulations**.
- ☐ Major newbuilding price drivers: **TCE earnings, steel plate prices, yard capacity, financing rates, environmental regulations**.
- ☐ A **cash buyer** is an intermediary who pays the owner in cash and assumes the risk of onward sale to a recycling yard.
- ☐ The **"As Is, Where Is"** clause is standard in demolition MOAs; the buyer bears all risk from the moment of delivery.
- ☐ The **Bill of Sale** is the legal instrument that transfers ownership of the vessel in a demolition sale.
- ☐ The **four market segments** of shipping are: **Freight Market, Newbuilding Market, Second-hand (S&P) Market, Demolition Market** — all four are interlinked.
- ☐ **Net Fleet Growth (%) = (New Deliveries − Demolition Sales) / Existing Fleet × 100**.
- ☐ The typical **newbuilding lead time** is **24–36 months** for tankers and bulkers.
- ☐ A **Letter of Intent (LOI)** precedes the binding newbuilding contract; it is non-binding except for an exclusivity/exit clause.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Maritime Law and Shipping Management**, the **Classification of Costs** refers to the systematic categorization of all expenditures incurred in the ownership, operation, and management of a vessel or shipping enterprise into homogeneous groups based on behavior, traceability, function, and controllability. This classification is essential for **voyage estimation, charter party negotiations, freight rate determination, and financial decision-making** under both time charter and voyage charter frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for PENST525, students must demonstrate the ability to **classify, compute, and apply cost concepts** in shipping scenarios such as estimating voyage costs, evaluating charter party terms, and assessing shipowner's profitability.

## 1.2 Key Cost Categories — The Master Classification

Shipping costs are classified along **multiple dimensions** simultaneously, because a single cost (e.g., bunkers) can belong to several categories depending on the lens applied.

### A. By Behavior (Volume Sensitivity)
- **Fixed Costs** — Independent of voyage activity
- **Variable Costs** — Change directly with output (tonnes/miles)
- **Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs** — Contain both fixed and variable elements

### B. By Traceability
- **Direct Costs** — Traceable to a specific voyage or vessel
- **Indirect Costs** — Allocated across multiple voyages/fleets

### C. By Function in Shipping
- **Capital Costs** (Investment-related)
- **Operating Costs** (Voyage costs + Vessel costs)
- **Periodic Costs** (Maintenance, Insurance)

### D. By Controllability
- **Controllable Costs** — Management can influence (crew wages, route)
- **Uncontrollable Costs** — Outside managerial discretion (port dues, canal tolls)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Family Car"**
>
> Imagine you own a car used as a taxi. **Insurance, road tax, and depreciation** are like **fixed costs** — you pay them whether the car moves or not. **Fuel and toll charges** are **variable costs** — the more you drive, the more you spend. **A monthly service contract** is **semi-variable** — you pay a base fee plus extra for every 1,000 km.
>
> In shipping, the ship is the taxi, the voyage is the journey, and the shipowner is the taxi-owner. Bunker fuel, port charges, and canal dues are the petrol-pump bills; crew wages and insurance are the standing bills; and dry-docking is the major service overhaul.

## 1.4 Standard Shipping Industry Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Voyage Cost | $V_c$ | USD per voyage |
| Daily Vessel Cost | $DC$ | USD per day |
| Bunker Consumption | $b$ | Tonnes/day (TPD) |
| Port Turnaround Time | $T_p$ | Days |
| Voyage Days | $V_d$ | Days |
| Speed | $s$ | Knots |
| Laden Distance | $D_l$ | Nautical Miles (nm) |
| Total Cost per Ton | — | USD/tonne cargo |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The classification of costs is not a theoretical exercise — it is the **foundation of voyage estimation and freight rate calculation**, which is examined almost every KTU cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost Classification by Behavior — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Fixed Costs (FC)

**Definition:** Costs that remain constant in total over a relevant range of activity but vary per unit when output changes.

**Shipping Examples:**
- **Vessel depreciation** (straight-line)
- **Hull & Machinery (H&M) insurance premiums**
- **Protection & Indemnity (P&I) club contributions**
- **Loan repayment / mortgage interest**
- **Permanent crew wages** (under time charter)
- **Flag state registration fees**
- **Classification society annual fees**

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$FC_{total} = \text{Constant} \quad \text{for a given period}$$

$$FC_{per\,unit} = \frac{FC_{total}}{Q}$$

Where $Q$ is the quantity of output (tonnes carried or voyages performed).

### 2.1.2 Variable Costs (VC)

**Definition:** Costs that vary **in total directly and proportionately** with the level of activity, but remain constant per unit.

**Shipping Examples:**
- **Bunker fuel** (FO + DO + LSFO in modern ECA zones)
- **Port charges** (if paid per call)
- **Canal dues** (Suez, Panama, Kiel)
- **Cargo handling / Stevedoring**
- **Lashing materials**
- **Pilotage and tug assistance** (per movement)

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$VC_{total} = v \times Q$$

$$VC_{per\,unit} = v = \frac{VC_{total}}{Q}$$

Where $v$ is the variable cost per unit of output.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs

**Definition:** Costs containing both a fixed component and a variable component.

**General Linear Form:**

$$TC = FC + (v \times Q)$$

**Shipping Examples:**
- **Crew wages** — Base salary (fixed) + overtime/bonuses (variable)
- **Maintenance & repair** — Standing maintenance contract + per-call servicing
- **Lubricating oil consumption** — Standing stock + operational usage
- **Telecommunications** — Monthly subscription + usage charges
- **Management fees** — Fixed retainer + commission on freight earned

### 2.1.4 Step-Fixed Costs (Discretionary Fixed)

A special sub-type in shipping where costs remain fixed within a **step range** and jump to a new level when capacity is exceeded (e.g., hiring an additional officer when fleet size crosses a threshold).

## 2.2 Cost Classification by Function in Shipping

### 2.2.1 Capital Costs (Investment Costs)

These are the **one-time, large-scale costs** incurred to acquire the vessel.

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| Newbuilding Price | Contract price with shipyard |
| Financing Cost | Interest on mortgage / loan |
| Depreciation | Systematic allocation of cost over useful life |

**Straight-Line Depreciation Formula:**

$$D_{annual} = \frac{P - S}{n}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Initial purchase price (USD)
- $S$ = Scrap/Salvage value (USD)
- $n$ = Useful life in years

### 2.2.2 Voyage Costs

Costs that **incur only when the vessel undertakes a voyage**.

| Cost Element | Billed By |
|---|---|
| Bunkers | Bunker suppliers |
| Port charges | Port authority |
| Canal dues | Suez/Panama Canal Authority |
| Pilotage, towage | Port services |
| Stevedoring/loading | Terminal operator |
| Agency fees | Ship's agent |

### 2.2.3 Vessel Costs (Standing/Hull Costs)

Costs incurred **regardless of whether the vessel sails** — they accrue with the calendar.

| Cost Element | Nature |
|---|---|
| Crew wages & benefits | Fixed contractual |
| Manning & recruitment | Annual/periodic |
| Stores & provisions | Periodic replenishment |
| Lubricating oil | Standing + running |
| H&M insurance | Annual premium |
| P&I insurance | Annual call |
| Maintenance & repair | Planned + unplanned |
| Class survey fees | 5-yearly + annual |
| Administration | Office overheads |

## 2.3 Cost Classification by Controllability

| Type | Definition | Shipping Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Controllable** | Management can influence through decisions | Route choice, speed, bunker hedging, crew size |
| **Uncontrollable** | Outside managerial control | Port tariffs, canal tolls, flag state fees, regulatory levies (e.g., EU ETS, CII) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — The Master Cheat Code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Reference Table:** Memorize these for the 14-mark questions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TC = FC + VC$ | Total Cost = Fixed + Variable | Total voyage cost |
| 2 | $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$ | Average Cost = TC ÷ Quantity | Cost per tonne |
| 3 | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | Marginal Cost | Additional unit cost |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{P - S}{n}$ | Annual Depreciation | Capital recovery |
| 5 | $VC_{voyage} = b \times P_b \times V_d$ | Bunker cost | Voyage estimation |
| 6 | $Bunker = b \times T \times P_b$ | Total bunker cost | ECA/NOx zones |
| 7 | $TCE = \dfrac{\text{Net Revenue} - V_c}{V_d}$ | Time Charter Equivalent | Comparing charters |
| 8 | $Profit_{voyage} = F - TC$ | Freight minus total cost | Voyage profitability |
| 9 | $Break\text{-}Even\ Freight = \dfrac{TC}{Cargo\ Tonnage}$ | Minimum freight | Tender bids |
| 10 | $Daily\ Cost = \dfrac{FC_{annual}}{365} + DC_{daily}$ | Daily burn rate | Charter comparisons |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Managerial Utility

- **Charter Party Negotiations:** Owners compute daily vessel cost to set time charter rates; charterers compute voyage cost to bid on voyage charters.
- **Fleet Expansion Decisions:** Capital cost classification aids investment appraisal (NPV, IRR).
- **Route Profitability Analysis:** Variable cost classification isolates the cost of every additional nautical mile.
- **Bunker Hedging Strategy:** Variable cost identification allows financial instruments (FFAs, bunker swaps).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** EU ETS, CII, and EEXI regulations depend on accurate cost classification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Voyage Cost from First Principles

A vessel is fixed on a voyage charter from Port A to Port B. We must compute the **Total Voyage Cost (TVC)**.

### Given:
- Bunker consumption: $b = 80$ TPD
- Voyage duration: $V_d = 25$ days
- Bunker price: $P_b = \text{USD } 600$ per tonne
- Port charges at load port: $P_L = \text{USD } 25{,}000$
- Port charges at discharge port: $P_D = \text{USD } 20{,}000$
- Canal dues: $C = \text{USD } 80{,}000$
- Daily vessel cost (crew, insurance, admin): $DC = \text{USD } 8{,}000$/day
- Cargo quantity: $Q = 30{,}000$ tonnes

### Step 1: Identify the Cost Categories

```
Total Voyage Cost (TVC) = Voyage Costs + Vessel Costs (for voyage period)
```

### Step 2: Compute the Variable Component — Bunker Cost

$$Bunker\ Cost = b \times V_d \times P_b$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Bunker\ Cost &= 80 \times 25 \times 600 \\
&= 80 \times 15{,}000 \\
&= \text{USD } 1{,}200{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The vessel burns 80 tonnes per day, for 25 days, at USD 600 per tonne. Multiply all three to get the total fuel bill for the voyage.

### Step 3: Compute Voyage-Specific Costs

$$\begin{aligned}
Voyage\ Costs\ (V_c) &= Bunker + P_L + P_D + C \\
&= 1{,}200{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 80{,}000 \\
&= \text{USD } 1{,}325{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Bunkers, port charges at both ends, and canal dues are all incurred **only because the vessel is undertaking this voyage** — they are pure variable voyage costs.

### Step 4: Compute Vessel (Standing) Cost Allocated to the Voyage

$$Vessel\ Cost_{voyage} = DC \times V_d$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Vessel\ Cost_{voyage} &= 8{,}000 \times 25 \\
&= \text{USD } 200{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The owner pays crew, insurance, and administration at USD 8,000/day **whether the ship sails or not**, but for this voyage, we allocate 25 days' worth.

### Step 5: Compute Total Voyage Cost

$$TVC = V_c + Vessel\ Cost_{voyage}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
TVC &= 1{,}325{,}000 + 200{,}000 \\
&= \text{USD } 1{,}525{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Compute Cost Per Tonne (Average Cost)

$$AC = \frac{TVC}{Q}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
AC &= \frac{1{,}525{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
&\approx \text{USD } 50.83\ /\text{tonne}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** To know the minimum freight to break even, we divide the total cost by the cargo carried.

### Step 7: Compute Break-Even Freight Rate

$$\text{Break-Even Freight} = \frac{TVC}{Q} = \text{USD } 50.83\ \text{per tonne}$$

If the charterer offers USD 55/tonne, profit per tonne = USD 4.17, total profit = USD 125,100.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cost Behavior Sensitivity Table

| Cost Element | Behavior | Cost per Voyage (USD) | % of TVC | Controllable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bunker Fuel | Variable | 1,200,000 | 78.69% | Yes (speed, route) |
| Port Charges (Load) | Variable | 25,000 | 1.64% | Partially |
| Port Charges (Discharge) | Variable | 20,000 | 1.31% | Partially |
| Canal Dues | Variable | 80,000 | 5.25% | No (route-fixed) |
| Crew & Insurance (allocated) | Fixed | 200,000 | 13.11% | No (contracted) |
| **TOTAL** | — | **1,525,000** | **100%** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight from the Matrix:** In this voyage, **bunker fuel alone accounts for nearly 79% of the total cost**. This is why slow steaming, scrubber installation, and alternative fuels are central to modern shipping economics — and a frequent KTU question theme.

## 3.3 Semi-Variable Cost Decomposition — Worked Example

A vessel's **telecommunications cost** is given as:
- Fixed monthly subscription: USD 500
- Per-MB data charge: USD 0.10
- Estimated monthly usage: 50,000 MB

**Total monthly cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TC &= FC + (v \times Q) \\
&= 500 + (0.10 \times 50{,}000) \\
&= 500 + 5{,}000 \\
&= \text{USD } 5{,}500
\end{aligned}$$

**Variable portion:** USD 5,000 (90.9%) — **Variable**
**Fixed portion:** USD 500 (9.1%) — **Fixed**

**High-Low Method to separate Fixed from Variable:**

If in month 1, $Q_1 = 30{,}000$ MB and $TC_1 = 3{,}500$
If in month 2, $Q_2 = 70{,}000$ MB and $TC_2 = 7{,}500$

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{TC_2 - TC_1}{Q_2 - Q_1} = \frac{7{,}500 - 3{,}500}{70{,}000 - 30{,}000} = \frac{4{,}000}{40{,}000} = \text{USD } 0.10/\text{MB} \\
FC &= TC_1 - (v \times Q_1) = 3{,}500 - (0.10 \times 30{,}000) = 3{,}500 - 3{,}000 = \text{USD } 500
\end{aligned}$$

**Verified:** The fixed component is USD 500 and variable rate is USD 0.10/MB.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Shipping Cost Universe

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipping Costs] --> B[By Behavior]
    A --> C[By Traceability]
    A --> D[By Function]
    A --> E[By Controllability]
    B --> B1[Fixed Costs]
    B --> B2[Variable Costs]
    B --> B3[Semi-Variable]
    B --> B4[Step Fixed]
    C --> C1[Direct]
    C --> C2[Indirect]
    D --> D1[Capital Costs]
    D --> D2[Operating Costs]
    D --> D3[Voyage Costs]
    D --> D4[Vessel Costs]
    E --> E1[Controllable]
    E --> E2[Uncontrollable]
    D1 --> F1[Newbuilding Price]
    D1 --> F2[Depreciation]
    D1 --> F3[Financing Interest]
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> G1[Bunkers]
    D3 --> G2[Port Charges]
    D3 --> G3[Canal Dues]
    D3 --> G4[Pilotage and Towage]
    D3 --> G5[Cargo Handling]
    D4 --> H1[Crew Wages]
    D4 --> H2[Insurance HM and PI]
    D4 --> H3[Stores and Provisions]
    D4 --> H4[Maintenance and Repair]
    D4 --> H5[Admin and Overheads]
```

## 4.2 Cost Behavior Pattern — Visual Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Output Level Q] --> B[Total Cost TC]
    B --> C[Fixed Cost FC Constant]
    B --> D[Variable Cost VC Sloped]
    C --> E[Horizontal Line]
    D --> F[Linear Upward Slope]
    G[Per Unit Cost] --> H[FC per unit Decreases]
    G --> I[VC per unit Constant]
    G --> J[AC U Shaped]
```

## 4.3 Voyage Cost Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Voyage Charter Decision] --> V1[Step 1 Estimate Voyage Days]
    V1 --> V2[Step 2 Compute Bunker Cost]
    V2 --> V3[Step 3 Add Port and Canal Dues]
    V3 --> V4[Step 4 Allocate Vessel Standing Cost]
    V4 --> V5[Step 5 Total Voyage Cost TVC]
    V5 --> V6[Step 6 Divide by Cargo Tonnage]
    V6 --> V7[Step 7 Break Even Freight Rate]
    V7 --> V8[Step 8 Compare with Charter Offer]
    V8 --> V9{Profit greater than 0?}
    V9 --> V10[Accept Voyage]
    V9 --> V11[Reject Voyage]
```

## 4.4 Charter Party Type vs Cost Responsibility Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TC[Time Charter] --> T1[Charterer pays Bunkers]
    TC --> T2[Charterer pays Port Charges]
    TC --> T3[Owner pays Crew and Vessel Costs]
    VC[Voyage Charter] --> V1[Owner pays Bunkers]
    VC --> V2[Owner pays Port Charges]
    VC --> V3[Owner pays Crew and Vessel Costs]
    BC[Bareboat Charter] --> B1[Charterer pays All Operating Costs]
    BC --> B2[Owner receives Bareboat Hire Only]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Fixed Costs and Variable Costs. Give two shipping examples of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Fixed Costs** are costs that remain constant in total over a relevant range of activity, irrespective of the volume of cargo carried or the number of voyages performed. They are **time-based**, not output-based.

*Shipping examples:*
1. Annual Hull and Machinery insurance premium
2. Depreciation of the vessel (straight-line method)
3. Permanent crew wages under a time charter

**Variable Costs** are costs that vary in direct proportion to the level of activity. The more the vessel sails, the more these costs accumulate. Per unit, however, they remain constant.

*Shipping examples:*
1. Bunker fuel consumption (FO + DO)
2. Port charges and canal dues
3. Stevedoring / cargo handling charges

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always cite a **shipping-specific example**. Generic factory-based examples will cost you marks.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Direct Costs and Indirect Costs in shipping operations. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Direct Costs | Indirect Costs |
|---|---|---|
| **Traceability** | Can be directly traced to a specific voyage, vessel, or charter | Cannot be traced to a single voyage — must be allocated |
| **Examples** | Bunkers for voyage X, port charges at Rotterdam, Suez Canal dues for a specific transit | Head office rent, fleet manager salary, corporate legal fees, depreciation |
| **Cost Object** | Single voyage or single vessel | Entire fleet or company |
| **Allocation Method** | Direct assignment | Apportionment by tonnage-miles, days, or revenue |

**Key Insight:** In shipping, voyage costs (bunkers, port dues) are **direct**, while administrative and corporate overheads are **indirect**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

#### (a) Classify shipping costs into Capital Costs, Voyage Costs, and Vessel Costs. For each category, list at least four cost elements and explain how each behaves with respect to a vessel's trading pattern. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Capital Costs (Investment Costs)**
These are non-recurring, one-time expenditures incurred to acquire and finance a vessel.
- **Newbuilding Price** — Lump-sum paid to the shipyard
- **Loan Interest / Mortgage Charges** — Annual interest on borrowed capital
- **Depreciation** — Systematic write-down of the asset value over useful life
- **Initial Classification and Registration Fees** — One-time statutory costs

**Behavior:** Capital costs are **sunk and time-based**; they do not vary with voyage activity. The shipowner recovers them through freight and hire income over the vessel's economic life (typically 20–25 years).

**2. Voyage Costs**
These are costs incurred **only when the vessel sails** on a specific voyage.
- **Bunker fuel** — Main driver; varies with distance, speed, and idle time
- **Port charges** — Both load and discharge ports
- **Canal dues** — Suez, Panama, Kiel
- **Pilotage and towage** — Per movement
- **Stevedoring / cargo handling**
- **Agency fees**

**Behavior:** Voyage costs are **purely variable**; they rise and fall directly with the number of voyages and the trading pattern. A vessel on a ballast voyage still incurs bunkers but no cargo-handling costs.

**3. Vessel Costs (Standing / Hull Costs)**
These are costs that accrue **with the calendar**, regardless of whether the ship is sailing.
- **Crew wages, benefits, and overtime**
- **Manning and crew travel**
- **Stores, provisions, and lubricating oil**
- **Hull and Machinery (H&M) insurance**
- **Protection and Indemnity (P&I) club calls**
- **Maintenance, repair, and dry-docking**
- **Classification society survey fees**
- **Administration and management overheads**

**Behavior:** Vessel costs are predominantly **fixed** in the short term, with some **semi-variable** components (e.g., overtime, repair call-outs).

**Trading Pattern Implication:**
- A vessel on **long-haul, high-utilization trades** spreads fixed vessel costs over more revenue days, improving unit economics.
- A vessel on **short-sea, multi-port trades** incurs high voyage costs and low revenue per call, requiring tighter cost control.

---

#### (b) A bulk carrier consumes 65 tonnes of fuel oil per day at sea and 8 tonnes per day in port. Bunker price is USD 620 per tonne. The voyage comprises: 12 days at sea laden, 3 days at sea in ballast, and 4 days in port. Compute: (i) total bunker cost, (ii) cost per nautical mile if the laden voyage covers 3,600 nm, and (iii) classify this cost behaviorally. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Consumption at sea: $b_s = 65$ TPD
- Consumption in port: $b_p = 8$ TPD
- Bunker price: $P_b = \text{USD } 620$/tonne
- Days laden at sea: 12
- Days ballast at sea: 3
- Days in port: 4
- Laden distance: 3,600 nm

**(i) Total Bunker Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sea days total} &= 12 + 3 = 15\ \text{days} \\
\text{Port days} &= 4\ \text{days} \\
\text{Fuel at sea} &= 65 \times 15 = 975\ \text{tonnes} \\
\text{Fuel in port} &= 8 \times 4 = 32\ \text{tonnes} \\
\text{Total fuel} &= 975 + 32 = 1{,}007\ \text{tonnes} \\
\text{Total Bunker Cost} &= 1{,}007 \times 620 = \text{USD } 624{,}340
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Cost per Nautical Mile**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per nm} &= \frac{\text{Total Bunker Cost}}{\text{Laden Distance}} \\
&= \frac{624{,}340}{3{,}600} \\
&\approx \text{USD } 173.43\ \text{per nm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Note:* The ballast distance is not included in "laden voyage" for this metric, as per standard KTU practice.

**(iii) Behavioral Classification**

Bunker fuel is a **Variable Cost** because:
- It varies **directly** with voyage activity (more days sailing = more fuel)
- It is incurred **only when the vessel operates**
- It can be controlled by **speed optimization, slow steaming, weather routing**, and **bunker hedging**

However, in the **short run**, since bunker price may be fixed by a supply contract, it can behave **semi-variable** until contract renewal. KTU examiners typically classify it as **Variable**.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Explain with suitable examples the difference between Controllable and Uncontrollable costs in shipping management. How does this classification influence managerial decision-making? (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Controllable Costs** are expenses over which the shipowner or fleet manager has direct influence through operational or strategic decisions.

*Examples:*
- Bunker consumption (via slow steaming, route optimization)
- Crew size and overtime
- Procurement strategy (bunker hedging, supplier negotiation)
- Speed of the vessel
- Choice of bunkering port
- Maintenance scheduling (preventive vs breakdown)

**Uncontrollable Costs** are expenses determined by external parties, market forces, or regulations, and the shipowner cannot directly influence them.

*Examples:*
- Port tariffs set by port authorities
- Suez and Panama Canal tolls
- Flag state registration and annual fees
- IMO regulations (e.g., EEXI, CII, EU ETS carbon allowances)
- Insurance market premium rates
- Exchange rate fluctuations on USD-denominated bunker purchases in non-USD jurisdictions
- Mandatory class survey costs

**Managerial Decision-Making Influence:**

1. **Performance Evaluation** — Managers are judged on controllable costs only; uncontrollable costs are excluded from variance analysis.
2. **Responsibility Accounting** — Each manager is assigned cost centers (e.g., fleet manager for vessel costs, chartering manager for voyage costs).
3. **Strategic Decisions** — Controllable cost focus drives long-term contracts, slow steaming policies, and scrubber investments.
4. **Risk Management** — Uncontrollable costs require hedging instruments (FFAs, bunker swaps, freight derivatives).
5. **Regulatory Compliance** — Uncontrollable regulatory costs drive the carbon-intensity index (CII) rating strategy.

**Shipping-Specific Insight:** With the EU ETS extension to shipping (2024) and IMO 2023 strategy, uncontrollable regulatory costs are rising sharply, reshaping the controllable-vs-uncontrollable balance.

---

#### (b) A vessel incurs the following costs for an annual operating period. Classify each cost as Fixed, Variable, or Semi-Variable with clear justification. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

| Cost Item | Annual Amount (USD) | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Annual H&M Insurance | 180,000 | **Fixed** | Premium is contractually fixed for the year; independent of voyages |
| Bunker Fuel | 1,200,000 | **Variable** | Varies directly with sailing days and distance |
| Crew Base Wages | 420,000 | **Fixed** | Contractually fixed monthly salaries |
| Crew Overtime | 95,000 | **Variable** | Increases with port calls, cargo operations, and sea days |
| Port Charges | 350,000 | **Variable** | Incurred per port call; zero when vessel is idle |
| Depreciation | 600,000 | **Fixed** | Straight-line allocation, time-based |
| Telephone Base Subscription | 6,000 | **Fixed** | Flat annual fee |
| Telephone Usage Charges | 14,000 | **Variable** | Increases with communication volume |
| Management Fee (Fixed Component) | 50,000 | **Fixed** | Retainer to managing company |
| Management Fee (Commission on Freight) | 120,000 | **Variable** | 5% of USD 2.4M freight revenue |
| Classification Survey | 25,000 | **Fixed** (Step-Fixed) | Due annually, periodic major survey every 5 years |
| Bunker H&M (Repair and Maintenance) | 280,000 | **Semi-Variable** | Standing maintenance contract + per-call servicing |

**Justification Summary:**

- **Fixed (5 items):** 1,231,000 USD — These are time-driven and unaffected by activity.
- **Variable (5 items):** 1,779,000 USD — These are activity-driven and traceable per voyage.
- **Semi-Variable (1 item):** 280,000 USD — Mixed cost behavior.

**Total Cost:**

$$TC = FC + VC + SVC = 1{,}231{,}000 + 1{,}779{,}000 + 280{,}000 = \text{USD } 3{,}290{,}000$$

**Key Insight:** The variable-to-fixed ratio in this vessel is approximately **1.45:1**, indicating moderate operational gearing — a 10% increase in sailing activity would raise total cost by roughly 5.4%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not classify depreciation as variable** — it is fixed (time-based, not output-based).
> 2. **Do not mix "per call" with "variable" blindly** — port charges are variable only if they scale with voyages; an annual port registration fee would be fixed.
> 3. **Always state the cost driver** in your justification (e.g., "Bunker varies with sea days").
> 4. **Do not confuse semi-variable with step-fixed** — Step-fixed jumps in discrete blocks (e.g., adding a 6th officer when fleet exceeds 5 vessels).
> 5. **Currency consistency:** Always express final answers in USD or EUR as given; do not switch mid-solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Classification of Costs**

### Must-Know Definitions
- Fixed Cost: Constant in total; varies per unit
- Variable Cost: Varies in total; constant per unit
- Semi-Variable: FC + (v × Q) — both elements
- Direct Cost: Traceable to a single voyage/vessel
- Indirect Cost: Allocated via apportionment
- Capital Cost: One-time acquisition (newbuilding, financing)
- Voyage Cost: Incurred only on sailing
- Vessel Cost: Time-based, accrues with calendar
- Controllable: Manager can influence
- Uncontrollable: External determination

### Must-Know Formulas
- $TC = FC + VC$
- $AC = TC / Q$
- $MC = \Delta TC / \Delta Q$
- $D_{annual} = (P - S) / n$
- $Bunker = b \times T \times P_b$
- $TCE = (\text{Net Revenue} - V_c) / V_d$
- $Break\text{-}Even = TC / Q$

### Must-Know Numerical Traps
- Always include **both laden and ballast** fuel for total bunker, but **laden distance only** for "per nautical mile"
- **High-Low method** is the standard KTU tool to split semi-variable cost
- **Voyage cost ≠ Total cost**; voyage cost excludes standing vessel cost unless explicitly included

### Must-Know Industry Updates (2024 KTU Perspective)
- **EU ETS** for shipping — new uncontrollable regulatory cost
- **CII and EEXI** — affect speed and therefore variable costs
- **LNG / methanol / ammonia** fuels — reshape capital cost profile
- **Scrubber investments** — convert variable bunker cost to fixed capital cost

### Frequently Tested Sub-Topics
1. Bunker cost computation (ladden + ballast + port)
2. Voyage estimation under different charter types
3. Cost per tonne / break-even freight
4. Depreciation methods
5. Semi-variable cost separation
6. Controllable vs uncontrollable in the EU ETS era
7. Capital recovery in ship finance

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** In every numerical, **show the cost-category label first** (e.g., "Variable: Bunker = ..."), then the formula, then the arithmetic. This 3-line discipline scores full marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financing Ships and Shipping Companies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST525 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Financing Ships and Shipping Companies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Ship Financing?

**Ship Financing** is the structured provision of capital—through debt, equity, or hybrid instruments—to fund the **acquisition, construction, conversion, or operation** of sea-going vessels and the corporate entities that own them. Because a modern vessel may cost anywhere from **USD 30 million** (small handy-size bulk carrier) to over **USD 200 million** (VLCC, LNG carrier, or cruise ship), no single shipping company can typically fund such capital outlays from internal accruals alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST525 – Module 4):**
> "Ship financing refers to the legal, financial, and commercial mechanisms through which a shipping company raises capital to acquire, build, or operate vessels, while ensuring repayment through the cash flows generated by the ship or the corporate balance sheet, secured by preferred ship mortgages, maritime liens, and contractual covenants."

---

## 1.2 The "House Mortgage vs. Ship Mortgage" Analogy

Think of buying a ship the same way a young couple buys a house:

| House Purchase | Ship Purchase |
|---|---|
| Borrower approaches a Bank | Shipowner approaches a Shipping Bank / ECGD / Bond investor |
| Bank gives 80% Loan-to-Value (LTV) | Bank/Syndicate gives 60–80% Loan-to-Value (LTV) |
| House is **mortgaged** as security | Vessel is registered under a **Preferred Ship Mortgage** |
| EMI paid monthly for 20 years | Hire/EMI paid monthly/quarterly for 8–12 years |
| Default → Bank auctions the house | Default → Bank arrests and sells the vessel (lex specialis: **Arrest Convention 1952**) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike real estate, a ship is **internationally mobile**. The lender's security is at risk the moment the vessel sails into a foreign port. This is why ship financing has evolved highly specialized legal instruments (Preferred Mortgage under the **International Convention on Maritime Liens and Mortgages, 1993**) and specialized shipping banks (German *Schiffshypothek*, Norwegian, Greek, Korean).

---

## 1.3 Why Ship Financing is a Unique Sub-Branch of Project Finance

A ship is a **self-liquidating asset** — the very asset purchased with the loan generates the cash flow that services the loan. This makes ship financing a classic case of **Asset-Based Project Finance**, distinguished by:

1. **Singular collateral** — one identifiable, surveyable, classifiable asset (the hull + machinery).
2. **Cyclical revenues** — freight rates are notoriously volatile (e.g., Baltic Dry Index swung from 11,000 in 2008 to 663 in 2016).
3. **International enforcement** — the ship can be arrested in a Contracting State of the Arrest Convention.
4. **Specialised insurance** — **H&M (Hull and Machinery)** and **P&I (Protection & Indemnity)** are integral to the loan covenant package.
5. **Residual value risk** — vessel scrap value and second-hand market dictate recovery in default.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Term — *Single-Asset Project Finance*:**
> Because the loan is repaid *only* from cash flows of one identifiable vessel (and not the wider corporate balance sheet), lenders insist on a **ring-fenced SPV (Special Purpose Vehicle)** structure for each acquisition. Each ship = one SPV = one loan. This is a recurring 14-mark question theme.

---

## 1.4 Financing a Shipping *Company* vs. Financing a *Ship* — Critical Distinction

KTU examiners frequently test this distinction:

| Dimension | Financing a Ship | Financing a Shipping Company |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Acquisition of a single vessel | Corporate expansion, fleet renewal, working capital |
| Lender | Shipping bank, ECGD, K-Sure, JBIC | Commercial bank syndicate, bond investors, equity markets |
| Security | Preferred Ship Mortgage on hull | Floating charge on assets, corporate guarantees, debentures |
| Repayment | Voyage/TCE charter hire assigned to lender | Dividends, retained earnings, free cash flow |
| Risk Profile | Single-vessel risk, project-finance | Diversified fleet, corporate balance-sheet risk |
| Legal Framework | Maritime Liens & Mortgages Convention 1993 | Companies Act 2013, SEBI (LODR) Regulations, RBI ECB norms |

---

## 1.5 Key Stakeholders in a Typical Ship Finance Transaction

A typical KTU-style question lists the parties: identify them as the **"Four Pillars of Ship Finance"**:

1. **Borrower (Shipowner)** — single-ship SPV or operating company.
2. **Lender (Bank / Syndicate / ECGD)** — supplies the loan, takes mortgage.
3. **Builder (Yard)** — Korean (HHI, Samsung Heavy), Chinese (CSSC), Japanese (Imabari), or Indian (Cochin Shipyard).
4. **Insurer (H&M + P&I Club)** — ensures vessel is always insurable, satisfying the *'keep insured'* covenant.

Optional but recurring: **Charterer** (loan often repaid from long-term charter hire via *direct payment / assignment clause*); **Trustee** (in bond issues); **Flag State** (mortgage must be registered against the ship in the registry).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Spectrum of Ship Financing Instruments

A structured KTU answer should classify the instruments along the **Debt–Equity Spectrum**:

```
  PURE DEBT ◄──────────────────────────────► PURE EQUITY
  ──┬────────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────────┬──────┬──────┬──
    │        │         │         │         │      │      │
   Bank   Export   Bonds   Sub.    Pref.   Conv. Common
   Loan   Credit   (Sec/   Loan    Shares  Notes  Equity
          Agency   Unsec)  Debt
    │        │         │         │         │      │      │
  Sen.   Buyer   ECA    Junior   Mezz-    Hybrid  Last
  Sec.   Credit  Guarant.  Sec.   anine          Resort
```

### 2.1.1 Debt Instruments (Most Common in Shipping)

**(a) Commercial Bank Loan (Syndicated Term Loan)**
- Tenor: **8–12 years** for new buildings; **5–7 years** for second-hand.
- LTV: **60–80%** of vessel's market value.
- Margin: **LIBOR/SOFR + 150–400 bps** depending on credit.
- Security: **1st Preferred Ship Mortgage** + deed of covenant + charter assignment.

**(b) Export Credit Agency (ECA) Financing**
- Supplied by institutions such as **K-Sure (Korea)**, **SACE (Italy)**, **Euler Hermes (Germany)**, **JBIC (Japan)**, **US Ex-Im Bank**, and **ECGC (India)**.
- Covers up to **80% of the contract price** for vessels built in the ECA's home country.
- "**Knox-Keene**" principle: support follows the *yard*, not the *buyer*.
- Tenor: typically up to **12 years** post-delivery.
- **OECD Sector Understanding on Export Credits for Ships (2007, rev. 2012)** governs minimum premium, maximum tenor (12 yrs), and down-payment (20%).

**(c) Secured Bond / Unsecured Bond**
- Issued under a **Trust Deed** (Indenture), e.g., the **Norwegian Bond Market** or **Athens Stock Exchange**.
- Trustee enforces mortgage on default.
- Used by Greek and Norwegian tanker operators for **fleet refinancing**.

**(d) Sale and Leaseback (Bareboat Charter)**
- Owner sells vessel to a lessor, receives 100% of price (often tax-efficient), then leases it back for 8–10 years with a purchase option (bargain purchase option) at the end.
- Lessor is often a **JOLCO (Japanese Operating Lease with Call Option)** or **KOLS (Korean Operating Lease)** vehicle.

**(e) KG-Fund (German Tonnage Tax Model – closed in 2012, but historical importance)**
- Closed-end fund investing in a single vessel; investors get tax depreciation benefits and charter hire. Excellent KTU 14-mark topic for "innovative financing".

---

### 2.1.2 Equity & Hybrid Instruments

- **Common Equity** — IPO on Oslo Børs, Athens Exchange, Hong Kong Main Board. Issue under **SEBI (ICDR) Regulations 2018** if Indian.
- **Preferred Shares** — fixed dividend, no voting, redeemable — used by *Maran Gas* and *BW LPG*.
- **Convertible Bonds** — debt that converts to equity, popular in downturns.

---

## 2.2 The Security Package — Anatomy of a Mortgage

A KTU 14-mark answer on "Legal Framework of Ship Mortgage" must list:

| # | Document | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Loan Agreement** | Sets principal, interest, covenants, events of default |
| 2 | **Preferred Ship Mortgage** | Statutory mortgage under national law (e.g., Indian Merchant Shipping Act 2017, Part IX; US Ship Mortgage Act 1920) |
| 3 | **Deed of Covenant** | Promises to maintain, insure, not sell, not change flag without consent |
| 4 | **Assignment of Insurances** | Lender named as **loss payee**; receives H&M and P&I proceeds |
| 5 | **Assignment of Charter Hire** | Direct payment clause: charterer pays lender directly |
| 6 | **Pledge of Shares in SPV** | If borrower is a one-ship company, lender takes its shares |
| 7 | **Account Pledge / Cash Collateral** | Operating accounts, retention account, escrow |
| 8 | **Subordination / Inter-Creditor Agreement** | When multiple lenders finance a fleet |
| 9 | **Corporate Guarantee** | Parent company guarantee (often Indian promoter guarantee) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Article 1 of the 1993 Convention** defines a **"Preferred Mortgage"** as a mortgage that:
> (a) is granted over a vessel registered in a Contracting State;
> (b) is registered in the public registry of mortgages;
> (c) has priority over all other mortgages and liens **except** maritime liens enumerated in Article 4.
> This is a *verbatim* 7-mark exam answer.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Ship Finance Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **only** formulas you need to answer 14-mark numerical questions on Module 4. Memorise the table; it is exhaustive.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI)** | $E = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n}-1}$ | P = principal; r = monthly rate (annual/12); n = months | Loan amortisation, mortgage instalment |
| 2 | **Loan-to-Value (LTV)** | $\text{LTV} = \dfrac{\text{Loan Amount}}{\text{Vessel Value}} \times 100\%$ | % | Determines how much bank will lend |
| 3 | **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** | $\text{DSCR} = \dfrac{\text{Net Operating Income}}{\text{Debt Service (principal + interest)}}$ | Ratio | Lender covenant — must be ≥ 1.20 |
| 4 | **Loan Life Coverage Ratio (LLCR)** | $\text{LLCR} = \dfrac{\text{PV of Future Cash Flows over loan life}}{\text{Outstanding Loan Balance}}$ | Ratio | Project finance covenant — must be ≥ 1.10 |
| 5 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+\text{IRR})^{t}} = 0$ | % | Where NPV = 0 — investor's break-even |
| 6 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = -I_{0} + \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+k)^{t}}$ | I₀ = initial outlay; k = discount rate | Accept project if NPV > 0 |
| 7 | **Balloon Payment (Future Value of Principal)** | $B = P \cdot (1+r)^{n}$ | B = balloon at end of year n | Reduces EMI; refinanced at end of tenure |
| 8 | **Bareboat Charter Rate (Lessor Break-even)** | $L = \dfrac{(P - S_{n})(A/P, r, n) + S_{n} \cdot r}{12}$ | Sₙ = residual; (A/P, r, n) = capital recovery factor | Sale & leaseback pricing |
| 9 | **Effective Annual Cost of Leasing** | $\text{EAC} = (1+\dfrac{r}{m})^{m}-1$ | m = compounding frequency | Compare ECA vs bond financing |
| 10 | **Tonnage Tax (Lump-Sum EU)** | $\text{Tax} = \sum_{i} \text{Taxable Tons}_{i} \times \text{Rate per 100 NT}$ | € per 100 NT | EU corporate tax for shipowners |

---

## 2.4 Covenant Suite — What Every Lender Insists Upon

A KTU examiner loves "list and explain" questions on *loan covenants*. Memorise the 5 categories:

1. **Affirmative Covenants** — pay principal, maintain insurance, register mortgage, deliver quarterly accounts.
2. **Negative Covenants** — *no sale, no change of flag, no further borrowing, no charter to sanctioned parties, no change of management* without consent.
3. **Financial Covenants** — DSCR ≥ 1.20, LLCR ≥ 1.10, minimum working capital.
4. **Information Covenants** — quarterly financial statements, annual audited accounts, monthly *vessel operating reports*.
5. **Default Triggers** — payment default, cross-default, insolvency, change of control, judgment > USD 5 million, **Ousting of flag** without consent.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> *"Discuss the role of covenants in protecting the lender in a ship-financing transaction."* (14 marks) — Always structure: 1) definition, 2) classification, 3) examples, 4) consequences of breach, 5) case law reference (e.g., *Bank of Boston Connecticut v. European Grain and Shipping Ltd* (The "Prodromos") – English Court of Appeal).

---

## 2.5 Comparative Table — Financing Methods (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions)

| Parameter | Commercial Loan | ECA Loan | Bonds | Sale-Leaseback | Equity IPO |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tenor (years) | 8–12 | 10–12 | 5–10 | 8–10 | Perpetual |
| LTV / Coverage | 60–80% | 80% | 50–70% | 100% sale | n/a (raised fresh capital) |
| Cost | LIBOR + 200–400 bps | CIRR + small margin | Coupon 4–9% | Implied lease rate | Dividend yield |
| Security | Mortgage + covenants | Mortgage + ECA guarantee | Mortgage via trustee | Title with lessor | None |
| Speed | 3–6 months | 4–8 months | 2–3 months (post-IPO) | 1–2 months | 6–12 months |
| Best for | Established owner | Newbuilding from ECA nation | Fleet refinancing | Tax optimisation | Public company fleet growth |
| Indian Example | SCI vessel loans | Cochin Shipyard orders | Not common | SCI-JOLCO | Great Eastern Shipping, Varun Shipping |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — EMI Calculation for a Newbuilding Loan

> **Problem:**
> A Greek shipowner contracts a **USD 80 million** newbuilding loan at **SOFR (5%) + 250 bps** for **10 years**. The loan is fully amortising.
> *Required:* (a) Compute the quarterly EMI. (b) Build an amortisation table for the **first two years**. (c) Compute total interest paid.

### Step (a): Rate Conversion

Annual rate: $5\% + 2.5\% = 7.5\%$ per annum.

Quarterly rate: $r = \dfrac{7.5\%}{4} = 1.875\% = 0.01875$.

Number of quarters: $n = 10 \times 4 = 40$.

### Step (b): EMI Computation

$$
E = P \cdot \frac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n}-1}
$$

$$
E = 80{,}000{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.01875 \times (1.01875)^{40}}{(1.01875)^{40}-1}
$$

Compute $(1.01875)^{40}$ first:

$$
(1.01875)^{40} = e^{40 \cdot \ln(1.01875)} = e^{40 \cdot 0.018577} = e^{0.74308} \approx 2.10245
$$

Now substitute:

$$
E = 80{,}000{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.01875 \times 2.10245}{2.10245 - 1}
$$

$$
E = 80{,}000{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.039421}{1.10245}
$$

$$
E = 80{,}000{,}000 \times 0.035759 = \mathbf{2{,}860{,}720 \text{ USD per quarter}}
$$

> **Verification:** $80\text{M} / 40 = 2\text{M average principal}$, interest $80\text{M} \times 1.875\% = 1.5\text{M}$, so total ~3.5M per quarter, matches our result.

### Step (c): Two-Year Amortisation Schedule (First 8 Quarters)

The balance at the end of period $t$ is:

$$
B_{t} = P(1+r)^{t} - E \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{t}-1}{r}
$$

| Quarter (t) | Opening Balance | Interest (B×r) | Principal (E – Interest) | Closing Balance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80,000,000.00 | 1,500,000.00 | 1,360,720.00 | 78,639,280.00 |
| 2 | 78,639,280.00 | 1,474,486.50 | 1,386,233.50 | 77,253,046.50 |
| 3 | 77,253,046.50 | 1,448,494.62 | 1,412,225.38 | 75,840,821.12 |
| 4 | 75,840,821.12 | 1,422,015.40 | 1,438,704.60 | 74,402,116.52 |
| 5 | 74,402,116.52 | 1,395,039.68 | 1,465,680.32 | 72,936,436.20 |
| 6 | 72,936,436.20 | 1,367,558.18 | 1,493,161.82 | 71,443,274.38 |
| 7 | 71,443,274.38 | 1,339,561.39 | 1,521,158.61 | 69,922,115.77 |
| 8 | 69,922,115.77 | 1,311,039.67 | 1,549,680.33 | 68,372,435.44 |

**Total interest in 8 quarters** = (8 × 2,860,720) − Principal repaid in 8 quarters.

Principal repaid in 8 quarters = 80,000,000 − 68,372,435.44 = 11,627,564.56.

Total interest = 22,885,760 − 11,627,564.56 = **USD 11,258,195.44** in just 2 years.

Total interest over 10 years ≈ 40 × 2,860,720 − 80,000,000 = **34,428,800 USD**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** The **interest-to-principal** ratio in ship finance is roughly 30–40% over the loan life, depending on rate. This is why owners prefer to extend tenor or use a **balloon payment** structure (Section 3.3 below).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DSCR Compliance for a Bulk Carrier

> **Problem:**
> A Capesize bulk carrier generates an annual **net operating income** of **USD 6.5 million** (after OPEX, before debt service). The annual debt service (principal + interest) averages **USD 5.0 million** during the first 5 years. Determine whether the loan covenants of **DSCR ≥ 1.20** are satisfied.

$$
\text{DSCR} = \frac{\text{Net Operating Income}}{\text{Debt Service}} = \frac{6{,}500{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 1.30
$$

**Interpretation:** DSCR of 1.30 > 1.20 covenant threshold → **covenant satisfied**, with a **buffer of USD 0.50 million**. A 23% drop in income (from USD 6.5M to USD 5.0M) would still keep the loan compliant. This **cushion** is what the underwriter calls the *"debt service cushion"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If DSCR < 1.20, the borrower is in **technical default** even if all payments are current. The lender may demand:
> (i) early repayment of a *cash sweep* portion,
> (ii) a *top-up* corporate guarantee, or
> (iii) a *standstill* and restructuring under **English Law / NY Law** in a typical LMA (Loan Market Association) facility.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Balloon Payment (Refinancing Strategy)

> **Problem:**
> A shipowner wants to keep quarterly instalments low during the operating years. He takes a **USD 60 million** loan at **6% p.a. compounded quarterly** for **10 years**, but with a **balloon of USD 30 million** at the end. Compute the quarterly instalment.

### Setting Up

Quarterly rate: $r = 6\%/4 = 1.5\% = 0.015$.

We split the loan into two streams:
- *Stream A*: $P_1 = 30{,}000{,}000$ — **fully amortised** over 40 quarters.
- *Stream B*: $P_2 = 30{,}000{,}000$ — **bullet (balloon)**, only interest paid, principal repaid at $t=40$.

### EMI for Stream A (Fully Amortising Portion)

$$
E_{A} = 30{,}000{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.015 \times (1.015)^{40}}{(1.015)^{40} - 1}
$$

Compute $(1.015)^{40}$:

$$
(1.015)^{40} = e^{40 \cdot \ln(1.015)} = e^{40 \cdot 0.014889} = e^{0.59556} = 1.81402
$$

$$
E_{A} = 30{,}000{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.015 \times 1.81402}{1.81402 - 1} = 30{,}000{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.02721}{0.81402}
$$

$$
E_{A} = 30{,}000{,}000 \times 0.033427 = 1{,}002{,}810 \text{ USD per quarter}
$$

### Quarterly Interest for Stream B (Bullet)

$$
E_{B} = 30{,}000{,}000 \times 0.015 = 450{,}000 \text{ USD per quarter}
$$

### Total Quarterly Instalment

$$
E = E_{A} + E_{B} = 1{,}002{,}810 + 450{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}452{,}810 \text{ USD per quarter}}
$$

Compare to the fully amortising EMI for USD 60M (which was 2,860,720 from Example 3.1 scaled to 60M ≈ **2,145,540**). The balloon structure reduces quarterly outflow from 2.15M to **1.45M** — a saving of **USD 692,730 per quarter** — by deferring USD 30M to bullet repayment at year 10, when the owner expects to refinance with a new loan against a higher vessel value.

> [!TIP]
> **Why shipowners love balloons:** Vessel values often *appreciate* during the early years of a newbuilding loan. A balloon allows the owner to negotiate a *higher refinancing amount* in year 8–10, when the vessel's earnings capacity is well-established.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — NPV of an Investment in a Tanker

> **Problem:**
> An Indian shipping company considers buying a **MR Product Tanker** for **USD 35 million** today. Expected after-tax free cash flows for the next **5 years** are: **USD 4 M, 5.5 M, 6 M, 5 M, scrap value 7 M** (scrap in year 5 = USD 7 M). Cost of capital = **10%**. Should the company invest?

### NPV Computation

$$
\text{NPV} = -35 + \frac{4}{1.10} + \frac{5.5}{1.10^{2}} + \frac{6}{1.10^{3}} + \frac{5}{1.10^{4}} + \frac{7}{1.10^{5}}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\text{NPV} = -35 + 3.6364 + 4.5455 + 4.5073 + 3.4151 + 4.3472
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -35 + 20.4515 = \mathbf{-14.55 \text{ million USD}}
$$

**Decision:** NPV is *negative* → **Reject the project** at 10% cost of capital. The investment's IRR must be calculated. If IRR < 10%, the project destroys shareholder value. This is a classic exam-style problem.

### IRR — Quick Bracketing

Try $r = 4\%$:

$$
\text{NPV at 4\%} = -35 + \frac{4}{1.04} + \frac{5.5}{1.04^{2}} + \frac{6}{1.04^{3}} + \frac{5}{1.04^{4}} + \frac{7}{1.04^{5}}
$$

$$
= -35 + 3.8462 + 5.0850 + 5.3372 + 4.2763 + 5.7531 = \mathbf{-10.70 \text{ million}}
$$

Try $r = 0\%$ (no discounting):

$$
\text{NPV at 0\%} = -35 + 4 + 5.5 + 6 + 5 + 7 = -7.5 \text{ million}
$$

Since NPV is **negative at 0%**, the project itself does not recover the outlay → the investment is uneconomic regardless of discount rate. This happens when the *sum of nominal cash flows* is less than the outlay. The IRR does not exist; the project should be **rejected outright**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical note:** Sometimes, a freight-rate *cycle* makes year-by-year cash flow computation tricky. KTU questions simplify by using *contracted Time Charter Equivalent (TCE)* income, removing market volatility.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Loan-to-Value (LTV) Sanity Check

> **Problem:**
> A second-hand VLCC is bought for **USD 90 million**. The bank offers a **70% LTV** facility. What is the *equity contribution* required from the owner, and the *loan amount*?

$$
\text{Loan} = 90{,}000{,}000 \times 0.70 = 63{,}000{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

$$
\text{Equity} = 90{,}000{,}000 - 63{,}000{,}000 = 27{,}000{,}000 \text{ USD (30\%)}
$$

**Rule of thumb:** ECA loans require 20% minimum equity; commercial bank loans require 20–40% equity; high-yield bond issues for second-hand vessels may demand 35–40% equity. Below 20% equity, the loan is *"highly leveraged"* and the bank will impose *cash sweep* and *minimum liquidity* covenants.
